experiments.title: How does educational language about tribute affect tribute donations on Single Post Pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding education language on the SPP tribute widget for SPP visitors will increase donations by reducing friction around what the tribute gift is, providing clarity about what it can do, and encouraging users to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2026 - 03/16/2026
CaringBridge previously has had success with having language that is educational about what tribute giving is and how the readers tribute gift makes a difference.
For this test, we wanted to test adding that language on their Single Post Page tribute widget to see how that would affect tribute donations on the . This was tested for both mobile and desktop visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding educational language to the tribute widget significantly boosted all key metrics.
Donations (Tribute/JEN): +108.3% (99.9% confidence)
 	Transactions rose by 489%. Revenue overall increased by 604%. Donations made as tributes went up by 779%. Tribute revenue jumped by 886%. Tribute revenue for JEN specifically increased by 880%. These shifts demonstrate that educational language led to more transactions, more tribute giving, and much higher associated revenue.
In short, clearer information and less friction increase donations.
Future experiments should explore:
Testing educational language in other areas of the website or in different formats
 	Expanding this language across additional tribute widgets to understand its effect in different contexts

experiments.title: Will a specific resource in a dear reader drive more engagement than a general offer?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a Plugged In-branded resource in a Dear Reader for visitors to Plugged In’s review webpages will achieve an increase in email sign-ups because people are more likely to engage with a resource when it is closely aligned to the content they’re already consuming. because people are more likely to engage with a resource when it is closely aligned to the content they’re already consuming.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/16/2025
To capture more email subscribers, the team added a “Dear Reader” prompt at the bottom of review pages offering a free resource. The goal was to test whether visitors would be more motivated by a resource directly tied to Plugged In or a broader  tool.
Two offers were tested:
Control: A general video series, Praying the Scriptures Over Your Children.
Treatment: A Plugged In–branded ebook guide featuring curated, family-friendly movie picks.
The test explored whether contextual relevance — aligning the offer with the content just consumed — would increase conversions.
We split the website traffic 50/50 between the control and treatment, and ran this test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While both offers generated equal click-through rates from the “Dear Reader” prompt, the general series led to significantly more email sign-ups than the Plugged In–branded movie guide.
The control offer produced 49 sign-ups, compared to 29 sign-ups for the Plugged In ebook — that's a 41.4% decrease for the treatment, with a statistically valid confidence level of 97.8%
 	From what we can see in the data, the click rates on the Dear Reader itself were identical between the Control and the Treatment, indicating equal interest in the offers initially. The drop-off occurred at the conversion stage, suggesting differences in how compelling or valuable the offers felt to users.
 	Interestingly, users who signed up for the Plugged In ebook were more likely to go on to donate, even though total sign-ups were lower.
 	7 donations followed the Control offer; 6 followed the Treatment — not a statistically significant difference.
 	Average gift was higher for the Treatment ($52 vs $40), after removing a $5,000 outlier from the Treatment.
 	No significant difference in recurring vs one-time gifts.
more motivated or mission-aligned subset of users
the parenting-focused  offer had broader appeal and stronger top-of-funnel performance
emotional depth and perceived value

experiments.title: How embedding a donation form impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that embedding the donation form for donation page visitors will achieve more donations because there are fewer steps to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2025 - 08/05/2025
Dallas Theological Seminary recently switched donation platforms. The new default donation page has the “Give Now” button embedded and when the button is clicked the form pops up. We wanted to test embedding the form and skipping the button on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that embedding the donation form directly on the page, rather than requiring donors to click a separate button, resulted in a significant increase in donations. Specifically, the treatment group experienced a 36.9% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a 99.0% level of confidence. Additionally, One-Time Donations saw a 400% increase, and Revenue increased by 231%, both with high levels of confidence.
These results suggest that reducing the number of steps for donors can increase conversion rates. Future experiments should focus on streamlining the donation process and making it seamless. This could involve optimizing the donation form, testing layouts, or exploring other ways to reduce giving barriers. The goal is to create a frictionless donor experience that boosts donations for the nonprofit.

experiments.title: How using a content offer instead of a direct ask in a dear reader affects donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free ebook in a dear reader for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it provides immediate value to the audience, aligns with their interests, and incentivizes engagement by presenting a tangible benefit in exchange for their attention, making the offer more appealing and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2025 - 04/08/2025
For MPR, we decided to optimize their article pages through the addition of a dear reader. But before rolling this out to all traffic, we wanted to test how a content offer vs a direct ask would impact the donor conversion.
The content offer used in this test was their Talking Sense e-Book.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment is that the content offer version of the Article Pages Dear Reader saw a 199.1% lift in transactions at a 99.7% LOC, indicating that these results validated and therefore should be used for future decision making. The treatment version greatly outperformed the direct ask version in terms of both clicks and overall transactions.

Due to these results, we have rolled out the content offer version out to 100% of traffic to MPRNews article pages.

experiments.title: How using AI to rewrite an email improved donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using AI to rewrite and email will improve the donor conversion rate for both members and nonmembers when asking the AI to rewrite the email specifically to improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/13/2025
In our May sustainer invite campaign we tested using AI to rewrite an email to improve the donor conversion rate. Here was the PROMPT: Rewrite this fundraising email to optimize its ability to get someone to donate.
Here are the differences between the two emails:
Here is the control:
Email 6a
Sender: Tony Johnson
Subject: This will blow you away
Date: 5/17/25
Dear <First Name>,
Have you ever walked outside during a hurricane?
That is what trying to enact conservative policies in D.C. is like. Every time the Trump administration tries to take a step forward, the Washington establishment immediately pushes them back.
That’s why we need you to help The Heritage Foundation push forward President Trump’s conservative agenda.
You can support this crucial effort by activating/upgrading your Heritage Foundation Leaders Club membership using this secure link: URL
Your upgraded monthly donation will help:
Fuel The Heritage Foundation’s policy research which will help safeguard America’s founding principles for future generations. 
Promote your patriotic values through public policy and education of the American public. 
Increase economic growth so more Americans can achieve the American Dream. 
Combat the Washington establishment’s attempts to sabotage President Trump’s agenda.
Please activate/upgrade your Leaders Club membership using the secure link below.
URL
President Trump needs our help now more than ever. I hope you’ll take this opportunity to strengthen the fight to save our country through your monthly gift.
Best,
Tony Johnson
Director of Membership
P.S. Help the Trump administration beat the Washington establishment’s storm of resistance by activating/upgrading your Heritage Foundation Leaders Club membership using this secure link: URL
Here is the treatment:
Sender: Tony Johnson
Subject: This will blow you away
Date: 5/17/25
Dear <First Name>,
Have you ever walked outside during a hurricane?
That’s what trying to enact conservative policies in D.C. is like. Every time the Trump administration takes a step forward, the Washington establishment immediately pushes them back.

Best regards,
Tony Johnson
Director of Membership, The Heritage Foundation
P.S. Help the Trump administration beat the Washington establishment’s storm of resistance by activating/upgrading your Heritage Foundation Leaders Club membership using this secure link: URL
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
1. Personalization: Including the recipient's name and addressing them directly in the email can significantly improve donor conversion rates. This demonstrates that personalization plays a crucial role in engaging potential donors.
2. Clear Call to Action: The treatment email had a more compelling call to action, emphasizing the benefits of upgrading membership and how it would contribute to supporting President Trump's agenda. This clear direction likely helped in driving more conversions.
3. Emotional Appeal: The use of powerful language comparing the challenges in enacting conservative policies to walking in a hurricane resonated with the audience. Adding emotional appeal can help in creating a connection with donors and motivating them to take action.
4. Testing AI Rewriting: The use of AI to rewrite the email proved to be successful in improving donor conversion rates. This highlights the importance of continuously testing and optimizing email content to enhance its effectiveness.
Future Experimentation:
1. Utilize AI for Optimization: Given the success of using AI in rewriting the email, consider implementing AI tools for further optimization of fundraising emails. This could involve testing different AI-generated variations to identify the most effective messaging.
2. Segment Testing: Conduct further experiments by segmenting the audience based on factors such as donation history, engagement level, or demographics. This can help in tailoring email content to specific donor segments for better results.
3. Continuous Improvement: Incorporate learnings from this experiment into future fundraising campaigns. Regularly analyze data, test new strategies, and iterate on successful tactics to continually improve donor conversion rates.
4. Multi-Channel Testing: Expand experiments to include testing across various channels like social media, website messaging, or direct mail. This holistic approach can provide insights into the most effective ways to engage donors and drive donations.

experiments.title: How adding a micro-ask decision in an emergency banner impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro-ask decision in an emergency banner for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2024 - 07/24/2024
This test explored the impact of a micro ask to the emergency banner on an emergency page. The goal was to increase conversion by encouraging the user to engage with the banner. Our hypothesis was that adding a micro-ask decision in the emergency banner will effectively increase the overall conversion rates. This is because when users are presented with a Yes/No decision, they are more likely to engage, for the good or bad, rather than to bypass it by scrolling. Incorporating a micro-ask can make users more involved and attentive toward the emergency banner.
We had 3 different variants of this micro-ask, which we had originally tested in a previous experiment.
Variant A: Changed “Donate Today” to “Yes, I would like to Donate.” Added a button with the text “No, I’m not interested in making a donation.”
Variant B: Changed “Donate Today” to “Yes, I would like to Donate.” Added a button with the text “No, I’m not interested in making a donation.” Changed both button colors to white with red text.
Variant C: Changed “Donate Today” to “Yes, I would like to Donate.” Added a button with the text “No, I’m not interested in making a donation.” Changed “Yes” button color to white with red text.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Results:
Overall, all variants saw a significant increase in button clicks at a statistically significant confidence level.
 	On desktop, all variants saw an increase in donor conversion at a statistically significant confidence level.
 	On mobile, all variants saw a decrease in both donor conversion and revenue per visitor at a statistically significant confidence level.

experiments.title: How placing a donation module on the homepage impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donation module for website visitors will achieve increase conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2025 - 04/16/2025
This test explored whether adding a donation module to the hero image on the homepage of Save the Children USA increased conversion over the standard top-right donate button. Our hypothesis was that the module will reduce friction for the user by presenting the donation levels at the start of the process–rather than the user clicking on the donate button and then going to the form–and also splitting the donation process into 2 smaller steps. This initial experiment was run during a 10x match campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donation module to the hero image on the homepage of Save the Children USA significantly increased conversion rates. The treatment group, which saw the donation module, experienced a 54.2% increase in donations compared to the control group with a high level of statistical confidence (99.3%).
Moreover, there was a 72% increase in revenue per visitor on the treatment page, with a 97% level of confidence.
The increase in conversion rate reflects reduced friction in the customer journey. The module on the hero provides the user with an easy way to give while also breaking up the donating process by providing the gifting level and amount up front. Then the user just has to complete their billing information as a secondary step. The module also allows us to provide equivalencies for each giving level which helps to make the impact more tangible for the donor.
Key metrics:
Desktop overall performance:
Control: 2% conversion rate
Treatment: 3.1% conversion rate, which is a 56% increase at a 95% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 39%, with an 80% level of confidence (one large gift removed from the variant).
Mobile overall performance:
Control: 4.4% conversion rate
Treatment: 6.8% conversion rate, which is a 54% increase with a 94% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased, this time by 161% with a 99% level of confidence.
Recurring performance:
Control: 0.55% conversion rate
Treatment: 0.92% conversion rate, which is a 66% increase with a 85% level of confidence. Recurring revenue per visitor also increased by 242%, with a 97% level of confidence.
One-time performance:
Control: 2.1% conversion rate
Treatment: 3.2% conversion rate, which is a 52% increase with a 98% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 62%, with a 94% level of confidence.

For CaringBridge, we had previously ran the education copy in the tribute widget in the tributes tab. For this experiment, we wanted to how adding in that same copy in the tribute widget for JEN traffic would affect donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:

This indicates that providing more clarity and information about the donation ask can encourage users to donate.

In future experimentation, we should consider integrating educational copy or information about the donation ask in various elements of our platform to potentially increase donor conversion rates. It is important to continue testing and iterating on different messaging strategies to optimize conversion rates and ultimately drive more donations for CaringBridge.

experiments.title: Will adding a ticker to the bottom of the page increase clicks to the donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a scrolling ticker tape on the bottom of the hompage for the Wait No More Campaign will achieve more clicks than the donate button because the rotating message will draw attention to the tape.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2024 - 11/01/2024
This organization runs several high urgency campaigns a year that draw a ton of traffic to their donation pages.
For this test, we wanted to see if there was a better tactic to get people to the donation pages rather that the usual sticky donate button or the main donate buttons in the navigation menu
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the scrolling ticker tape on the bottom of the homepage for the Wait No More Campaign significantly outperformed the traditional donate button in terms of driving clicks to the donation page.

Specifically, the treatment group saw a 66.5% increase in CTA Clicked for All Traffic with a 97.6% statistical level of confidence. Additionally, there was a 665% increase in CTA Clicked for All Traffic visitors with a 98% level of confidence.

These results indicate that the rotating message on the ticker tape successfully drew attention and prompted more visitors to click on the call-to-action, leading to a substantial increase in donation page visits.

experiments.title: How offering a pledge AND resource combo performed against just the resource
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a combo resource for text message audience will achieve a higher engagement because there is additional value.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/25/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
During election season PTV created a new resource titled “How a Christian Should Vote” this offer performed well via Facebook and housefile email.
We decided to test in a text message to the PTV audience offering either “How a Christian Should Vote” vs “How a Christian Should Vote” and a pledge to pray for America.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

The key learning from this experiment is that offering a combo resource along with a pledge to pray for America in a text message to the Pathway to Victory audience resulted in a 49.2% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to just offering the resource alone. This indicates that providing additional value and incorporating elements of faith and prayer can significantly impact audience engagement and response rates.

In donations, we saw a 101% increase in donations with a loc of 100%

In revenue, we saw a 55% increase in revenue with a loc of 91%

Future Experimentation:

Based on the success of this experiment, Pathway to Victory should consider incorporating combo resources and elements of faith and prayer in their future text message campaigns and communications. This approach can not only lead to higher engagement and conversion rates but also strengthen the connection with the audience by resonating with their beliefs and values. Additionally, Pathway to Victory can continue to test different messaging strategies and combinations to further optimize their campaigns and drive better results.

experiments.title: How relocating the Tribute widget to the right impacts tribute donations for JEN traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the tribute widget outside of the comment section pinned to the right for JEN users  will achieve an increase in tribute gifts because it better spotlights the ask while also keeping the two distinct CTA’s separate: comments and tribute gifts..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2024 - 07/22/2024
When a CaringBridge follows a new post/comment notification in email, they are brought to that post on a CaringBridge user’s profile. When they arrive, they are met with a Tribute widget directly in line with the comment section with an ask to make a tribute gift. Gifts to this tribute widget have been underperforming, and part of our hypothesis is that it’s due to there being two distinct CTA’s in the comment section. 1. CaringBridge wants the user to comment on the post. 2. But also the tribute widget presents a competing call to action. As a result, both can potentially be detracting from each other.
We developed two treatments to help address this where the Tribute Widget is pulled out of the comment section and placed on the right. We believed that this would better spotlight the ask, while also not interfering with the user experience when wanting to leave a comment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that placing the tribute widget outside of the comment section pinned to the right for JEN users resulted in a decrease in donations. Despite the hypothesis that this placement would better spotlight the ask and separate the two distinct calls to action, the treatment performed worse than the control.
There are items to consider around the execution of the experiment. For users in a smaller view desktop view, the widget would likely benefit from dynamically resizing as the user resizes their window. In some instances, it can be cut off or end up offscreen depending on the screen size.
The results of this experiment highlight the importance of monitoring and analyzing the impact of any changes made to the user interface or user experience to ensure that they are not detracting from the desired outcome. It may be beneficial to further investigate why the treatment performed poorly and iterate on the design before conducting another experiment.

experiments.title: How adding additional value prop to a giving banner impacted conversion rates during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy to include a specific goal on the homepage banner for the family audience will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because of the addition of a specific goal (exclusivity) and value prop will increase the motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2023 - 01/01/2024
During CYE Focus on the Family had a Giving Banner on the top of their homepage that had a very short CTA. We wanted to see if we tweaked the copy if we could increase the motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy to include a specific goal on the homepage banner for the family audience significantly increased donor conversion rates and revenue. By adding a specific goal and value proposition to the banner, we were able to create a sense of exclusivity and increase motivation to give.

The treatment group saw a 109.6% increase in revenue for all traffic, demonstrating the effectiveness of the new copy. Furthermore, there was a significant increase in transactions, both one-time and overall, with a 1412% and 1372% increase respectively.

experiments.title: How Adding a Countdown Affects Donor Conversion on a Wikibanner
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown on the homepage wiki banner for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the countdown adds appeal and exclusivity to the value proposition as to why somebody should give a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2024 - 05/20/2024
Focus on the Family is running its SeeLife campaign.. which drives more traffic to the homepage than usual. With this increased traffic we wanted to see if we could get donors to give on the homepage by having a wiki banner on the top of the homepage.
We have tested this before with previous campaigns. However, with this one we wanted to see what was more appealing to their donor audience, a total campaign goal countdown or a daily goal countdown.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that adding a countdown on the homepage wiki banner for prospective donors significantly increased donor conversion rates. Specifically, the treatment group experienced a 35.4% increase in Transactions - One-Time for All Traffic with a high statistical level of confidence of 98.6%. This suggests that the countdown added appeal and exclusivity to the value proposition, motivating more donors to give.
Moreover, Transactions overall saw a substantial 287% increase with a statistical confidence level of 97%. This highlights the effectiveness of incorporating a countdown in driving donor engagement and ultimately increasing donations.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue leveraging the use of countdowns in fundraising campaigns to capitalize on the sense of urgency and exclusivity they create.
Ultimately, the results of this experiment provide valuable insights that can guide future fundraising strategies and help maximize donation opportunities for the Focus on the Family organization.
____
Additional Notes
The tactic of adding a campaign goal counter to the homepage giving widget was effective at increasing conversions—likely due to the increased visibility of the need for donations. It also provides a level of social proof as donors can see that others are giving together to achieve a shared goal.
The more effective treatment, however, was the daily donor goal. While this treatment also increased the visibility of the need and adds the social proof aspect, it also reframes the goal to be more tangible and achievable to the donor. A potential donor may feel that their donation is a drop in the bucket of a 39,000 donor goal. But being of 675 donors needed today may feel more accessible and attainable.

experiments.title: How providing a micro ask of $25 for 2 weeks of impact, affects giving for onsite tribute widgets
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a microask  for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because it will lower the barrier to entry for prospective donors and reduce decision anxiety .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2024 - 03/24/2024
CaringBridge has a long-standing ask of $30 on their on-site tribute widgets. This was the approximate cost to power a site for a month several years ago but has since needed updating. In order to get closer to the actual cost to-date, and to reduce the barrier to entry for those who could not give higher amounts, we tested using a $25 ask that covers a site for 2 weeks (instead of a month). This was tested among multiple places on-site where a tribute widget appeared.
The first test ran (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/j6kp) was a $60 ask and that proved to be too high for site visitors – resulting in a valid 16% decrease in conversion rate and a decrease in revenue.
The second test ran  (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/gWqq) was a $50 ask that proved to also be too high for site visitors resulting in a 10% decrease in giving but flat revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that providing a microask for site visitors significantly increased donor conversion rates, demonstrating a 39.1% increase in donations for all traffic with a 99.9% level of statistical confidence. This confirms the hypothesis that offering a lower barrier to entry and reducing decision anxiety can positively impact giving behavior.
Furthermore, the experiment resulted in a substantial increase in revenue by 329% and a 385% increase in one-time donations, both with high levels of statistical confidence. This indicates that not only did more visitors donate, but they also contributed more in terms of monetary value.
Moving forward, these learnings should be used to inform future experimentation strategies. It is evident that adjusting the ask amount from $30 to a microask significantly impacted donor behavior in a positive way. Therefore, it would be beneficial to continue testing and optimizing donation asks to find the optimal balance between donor conversion rates and revenue generation. Additionally, considering other factors such as messaging, timing, and site placement in future experiments could further enhance donation outcomes.

experiments.title: Adding a Micro-Ask In An Emergency Banner
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding a micro-ask decision in the emergency banner will effectively increase the overall conversion rates. This is because when users are presented with a Yes/No decision, they are more likely to engage, for the good or bad, rather than to bypass it by scrolling.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding a simple yes/no question on a banner could increase donor conversion on the page. By asking a prospective donor if they will donate and then giving them a choice of saying yes or no could increase the likelihood of donating because the donor has autonomy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of integrating a micro-ask into an emergency banner.
Key results from the analysis include:
+63% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop click rate.
+77% statistically significant lift (95%) in desktop conversion rate.
+174% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop RPV.
The +77% lift in desktop conversion rate primarily stems from increased clicks on the banner button. Notably, the variant exhibited a click rate lift of +63%. From a psychological standpoint, individuals are more inclined to engage when they feel a sense of control and agency over their actions. This empowerment instills a sense of investment in the decision-making process, thereby enhancing their likelihood to interact with the banner, manifested in increased clicks. Essentially, by offering users the freedom to choose, we create an experience that resonates with their preferences and motivations.
As a result, the observed click rate lift underscores this approach's efficacy in driving user engagement and, ultimately, achieving our desired outcomes. Furthermore, the +174% lift in desktop Revenue per Visitor (RPV) suggests a significant behavioral shift. Users exposed to the micro-ask demonstrated a heightened propensity for larger donations following engagement. While quantifying psychological shifts presents challenges, we hypothesize that engagement with the micro-ask fosters deeper commitment, leading to larger average donations. Partial validation of this hypothesis is observed in the directional lift in donation form conversion rates between treatments (37.5% versus 34.6%). These findings underscore a compelling insight: the introduction of a micro-ask substantially enhances user engagement despite its seemingly inconsequential nature.
Moreover, it appears to instill a sense of commitment, resulting in more substantial contributions. This indicates that when users encounter micro-asks, they not only exhibit a higher inclination to engage but also tend to make larger donations, driven by an increased sense of commitment post-engagement.

experiments.researchQuestion: Adding a micro-ask decision in the emergency banner will effectively increase the overall conversion rates. This is because when users are presented with a Yes/No decision, they are more likely to engage, for the good or bad, rather than to bypass it by scrolling.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024

experiments.keyLearnings:

Key results from the analysis include:

+63% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop click rate.

+77% statistically significant lift (95%) in desktop conversion rate.

+174% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop RPV.

From a psychological standpoint, individuals are more inclined to engage when they feel a sense of control and agency over their actions. Essentially, by offering users the freedom to choose, we create an experience that resonates with their preferences and motivations.

As a result, the observed click rate lift underscores this approach's efficacy in driving user engagement and, ultimately, achieving our desired outcomes. Furthermore, the +174% lift in desktop Revenue per Visitor (RPV) suggests a significant behavioral shift. Users exposed to the micro-ask demonstrated a heightened propensity for larger donations following engagement. While quantifying psychological shifts presents challenges, we hypothesize that engagement with the micro-ask fosters deeper commitment, leading to larger average donations. Partial validation of this hypothesis is observed in the directional lift in donation form conversion rates between treatments (37.5% versus 34.6%). These findings underscore a compelling insight: the introduction of a micro-ask substantially enhances user engagement despite its seemingly inconsequential nature.

Moreover, it appears to instill a sense of commitment, resulting in more substantial contributions. This indicates that when users encounter micro-asks, they not only exhibit a higher inclination to engage but also tend to make larger donations, driven by an increased sense of commitment post-engagement.

experiments.title: How versioning ad copy to lapsed donors affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that versioning copy to acknowledge support for past donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will personalize the ask and acknowledge their previous behavior.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2023
The Heritage Foundation was showing ads to lapsed donors to reactivate them to active donors. However, these ads were originally developed for acquisition. They wanted to test the impact of adding “acknowledgement” language of the donors’ past giving history to the ad copy, with the thought that it might personalize the copy and make the donor realize that they were being spoken to directly.
They launched a test against the control language to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding "acknowledgement" language to the ad copy for lapsed donors did not lead to an increase in conversion rate. In fact, it resulted in a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group.

Based on this outcome, it is clear that personalizing the ask and acknowledging past support did not resonate with lapsed donors in this particular campaign. Therefore, it is important for future experimentation to focus on other strategies or messaging approaches to reactivate lapsed donors.

It is also important to consider the statistical significance of the results. With a 97.1% level of confidence, we can be highly confident that the decrease in donations is not due to random chance. This strengthens the validity of the findings and emphasizes the importance of using the control group as a benchmark for comparison.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore alternative tactics for re-engaging lapsed donors. This could involve testing different messaging techniques, creative approaches, or targeting strategies. It is also essential to continue tracking and analyzing key metrics such as conversion rate, donation amount, and donor retention to assess the effectiveness of any changes made.

experiments.title: How an eBook acquisition offer dear reader affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an eBook acquisition offer dear reader for news article readers will achieve an increase in instant donation rate because readers will be more motivated to donate after receiving a valuable ebook for free.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 12/12/2023
The Heritage Foundation regularly uses dear reader ads to promote direct appeals or acquisition offers on The Daily Signal news articles.
During their year-end campaign, Heritage wanted to know whether using an acquisition offer, specifically their Guide to the Constitution eBook, in a dear reader instead of a year-end direct appeal would lead to an increase in donations.
The CYE (calendar year end) dear reader contained a countdown timer with the amount of time left to donate in the campaign, directly asked the viewer to “Donate Today”, and led directly to the year end donation page. The Guide to the Constitution dear reader contained an eBook image mockup and asked the viewer to “GET YOUR ACCESS NOW”, leading to a landing page where readers could request the eBook and then to an offer-specific donation page.
The dear readers appeared on every Daily Signal article sitewide.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an eBook acquisition offer in a dear reader instead of a direct appeal for donations can significantly increase the instant donation rate.
We ran this experiment for six days at the beginning of Heritage's year end campaign until it validated with a 97.5% level of confidence in favor of the treatment. The treatment group, which received the eBook offer, saw a 148.2% increase in donations compared to the control group, which received the CYE direct appeal messaging.
This suggests that providing readers with a valuable incentive, such as a free eBook, can enhance their motivation to donate. Offering something of value in return for their donation can make the action feel more rewarding and increase their likelihood of taking immediate action.
The results of this experiment should be used in future experimentation by incorporating acquisition offers, such as eBooks, in dear reader ads. This strategy can help to optimize donation rates and improve the effectiveness of direct appeals. Further testing might include adding a fundraising campaign's direct appeal language to an existing content offer during the campaign to determine whether the language provides an extra, timely incentive to give.

experiments.title: How a newsletter sign up compared to an ebook sign up
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer will generate more emails, a weekly newsletter or an ebook?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 -
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station. To convert web traffic to email subscribers, we tested a slide out with fields to sign up for the weekly What’s Trending Newsletter vs. an ebook offer.
WUNC has struggled in converting web traffic into email subscribers, in a previous test we experimented with presenting a newsletter sign up via a slide-in and saw a 395% increase in emails acquired. For the next iteration, we are testing the newsletter offer vs a content offer to see if the item presented has an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment conducted by WUNC found that the slide-out offering an ebook generated more email subscribers than the one offering a weekly newsletter. The treatment offered a 63.5% increase in emails acquired for all traffic, with a 99.4% confidence level.

The key learnings from this experiment are that offering an ebook is a more effective way to acquire email subscribers for WUNC. In future experimentation, WUNC should continue offering an ebook in their email sign-up prompts and test variations of the newsletter content to optimize email acquisition. Additionally, testing different types of incentives to encourage sign-ups could be a useful experiment to run.

experiments.title: How adding a goal thermometer to a site interrupter impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a goal thermometor for site visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and average gift because people will be more driven by urgency and helping the organization meet their goal .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2023 - 05/22/2023
The Navigators created a pushdown/sticky bar for their Eagle Lake campaign. This asset targeted the main homepage, and the button would take users to the instant donation page for the campaign. We ran a previous experiment where we tested the language on this pushdown/sticky bar but with a few days left in the campaign, we wanted to drive urgency. We wanted to test the impact of adding a thermometer in the pushdown itself. That way, people could see how close we were to our goal.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 351% lift in donor conversion rate (with a level of confidence of 96%) and 664% valid increase in revenue (with a level of confidence of 98%. However, we did not reach the minimum of 50 transactions for this experiment. Given the urgent nature of this campaign, we stopped the experiment in order to roll out the treatment to 100% of the traffic. However, it is recommended that we retest this concept throughout an entire campaign, or consider testing this on the donation page itself.

experiments.title: How adding a continuation headline and personalizing the body copy to come from a prominent figure impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a continuation headline matched with personalizing the donation value prop copy for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because more visitors will engage with the value prop and will be more motivated to give due to the personal message coming from the organization’s founder.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/21/2023
ACI’s Cancel Culture Pledge funnel is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. The entry rate and sign up rate are above benchmark for the offer, which presents an opportunity to optimize the offer at the donation page level to see if we can improve our donor conversion rate.
Evaluating the current donation page, what first stood out is the headline. By simply thanking someone for signing up for the pledge, users may assume that they’ve reached the end of the engagement. Most consumer transactions conclude with a thank you, but we want visitors to stick around to engage with the value proposition copy and make a donation.
That’s why we developed a treatment variant (treatment #1) with the following headline: “Your name has been added. Before you go, would you consider this special request from Dr. Ben Carson?”
Through this headline, visitors will know that something important is waiting for them, and they should therefore continue on in their engagement, which is exactly what we want for them to do.
We then transitioned to the body copy and wondered if the value proposition message would resonate more strongly with potential donors if it was presented in the first person. We felt particularly confident in this assessment given the notoriety of ACI’s Founder.
Thus, we developed treatment #2, reframing the body copy to be presented from the 1st person point of view. We kept the copy consistent with it’s previous version, only adjusting the point of view. In addition, we added the signature of the messenger to add an even more personal touch.
Let’s see how each treatment performed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a continuation headline and personalizing the donation value proposition copy for potential donors can significantly increase donations.
This combination resulted in a 114% increase in donor conversion rate with a valid 96.6% level of confidence. It outperformed the "headline only" treatment, which resulted in a 95.4% increase in donations with a nearly valid 93.2% level of confidence.
Treatment #2 was especially effective among new visitors, which produced a valid 322.6% increase in donations. This treatment also produce a valid lift in revenue.
The continuation headline led more visitors to the value proposition copy, and the personalized message within created a stronger emotional connection with potential donors, motivating them to engage with the value proposition and ultimately donate.
In future experimentation, we should continue to test the impact of personalized messaging and content on donor conversion rates. We can also experiment with different ideas for continuation headlines to find the most effective approach for engaging visitors and encouraging them to donate. Additionally, we can test the impact of personalization on other key metrics, such as user engagement and retention.

experiments.title: Which Dear Reader converts more donors, a direct ask or course?
experiments.researchQuestion: Which will convert more web traffic to become a donor, a direct donation ask, or sign-ups for a course?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2023 - 05/12/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donor conversion we decided to test a dear reader with a direct ask versus a dear reader with a sign up for the new Into the Deep course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by Avila Foundation aimed to determine which would convert more website traffic to become a donor – a direct donation ask or sign-ups for a course. The experiment ran for 16 days, and the results showed that the treatment, i.e., sign-ups for a course, saw a 51.5% increase in donations for all traffic with a high level of confidence of 97.5%. This means that the treatment won and can be implemented in future fundraising efforts.
Additionally, the experiment showed that revenue experienced a huge increase at 1369% with a 99% level of confidence. The mobile visitors saw a 750% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence, whereas others had a 2609% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence. These are significant findings that can be used to inform future fundraising strategies.
The key learnings from this experiment are that offering a sign-up for a course can be a more effective strategy to convert website traffic into donors. This should be implemented in future fundraising efforts while keeping in mind the audience that responds best to this approach. The experiment also showed that mobile visitors can be an important target audience, and strategies should be tailored to them specifically. Finally, it is crucial to continue running experiments to improve fundraising strategies and optimize the conversion of website traffic into donors.

experiments.title: How the addition of a reader’s review for a giving premium impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a reader’s review of the giving premium help increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2017 - 02/21/2017
Every month Harvest Ministries sends an appeal that offers a premium to people when they give a donation. The primary value proposition on the donation page is about the premium with the secondary message focused on how a person’s donation will help Harvest Ministries in their mission. They hypothesized if adding a reader’s review of the premium offer to the donation page would help strengthen the primary value proposition and increase donations. They developed a treatment that featured a reader’s review while all other elements on the page remained the same as the control. They A/B split their traffic to test it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The addition of a reader’s review for the premium on the donation page helped increase donations for their monthly appeal by 26%! What we can learn from this is that the review:
Strengthened the force of the value proposition on the page
 	Added credibility and relevance to the premium offer by giving a real-life testimony of the value it added to a person's life

experiments.title: Does adding an image of a premium at the top of the page for mobile users driver higher conversion rates for Pathway to Victory?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the premium to the top of the page for mobile users will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2022 - 02/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that primarily generated revenue through monthly premium offers.
For their 2022 Pathway to Victory Devotional, which is a premium, leather-bound book offer 256 different devotionals, we ran an experiment testing the placement of an image of the devotional for mobile users.
The control, standard layout of the landing page for this offer has the image of the devotional all the way down the page on mobile; our hypothesis was that by moving the image to the top for mobile users, we would have a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By making a relatively simple modification to the layout of the landing page/donation form for mobile users only, and adding the image at the top, above the fold, Pathway to Victory experienced a 66% increase in donor conversions with a 99% level of confidence. Moreover, revenue from the treatment page increased by 102% with a 97% level of confidence.
What is also notable is that new visitors to the page had a higher donor conversion than returning visitors (65% versus 59%), showing that giving new visitors a visual cue first, then copy about the premium, was more appealing to potential donors.

experiments.title: How site flow interruptor offers affect conversion on mobile devices
experiments.researchQuestion: Which site flow interruptor will maximize conversion on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2017 - 06/16/2017
On the mobile experience, the slide-out offer produced a 91.6% lift in conversion over the exit intent popup. This is primarily caused by the way the two site flow interruptors display on mobile devices—the slideout actually functions more like an exit intent, taking up the entire screen. Since exit intent popups require a cursor, that experience is replaced with a very small offer bar at the bottom of the screen which is easier to ignore. While the exit intent popup produced a lift on desktop, this test result shows us that we cannot assume that the same tactic will work for different experiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Illinois Policy Institute wanted to promote their new eBook, Madigan’s Rules to their website visitors. However, they wanted to match the right promotional tactic to the correct audience segment. They had previously tested slide-out offers and exit intent popups, but knew that the two tactics presented differently on mobile and desktop.

They did a split test between the two offers and closely monitored traffic and conversion from desktop visitors in contrast to traffic and conversion from mobile visitors to match the ideal “site flow interruptor” to to the optimal experience.

experiments.title: How implementing an abandoned donation pop up impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an abandoned donation page pop up  for main donation page visitors who did not complete their donation  will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/03/2022 - 10/31/2022
IMB had a series of experiments done on their main donation page without much of a difference in results. Our continued hypothesis was that the individual comes to their main donation page with a high motivation to give and so there isn’t much that can be done on the main donation page to motivate the person to give. So, we wanted a way to capture the people who may have left the donation page without finishing the donation process. Our hypothesis was that an “abandoned donation” pop up would result an increase in donor conversion rate for their site visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month, this experiment resulted in a 24% valid lift in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 99%. This result affirms our hypothesis in that a pop up is effective in capturing those who may have abandoned the donation experience on their main donation page. Being reminded of the gift left behind and  the impact of the gift ("transform lives") will recapture those wouldn't have completed the donation process. It is recommended that this test is run again with a specific event attached to the pop up's donation button to discern what kind of lift we're seeing in donor conversion rate specifically through this pop up.

experiments.title: How testing a new offer in a “Dear Reader” ad affected clicks and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a relevant, time-sensitive resource for website visitors will achieve increase clicks and donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2020 - 10/24/2020
As we prepared to launch a new offer to the housefile and in acquisition, we were faced with a dilemma. The new eBook offer was relevant and time-sensitive, but we didn’t want to kick out an offer that was already performing strongly and miss out on additional conversions by losing a month of run time. So instead of replacing the current Dear Reader eBook offer outright, we decided to make the new offer compete against the old offer for the spot.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found is that the new offer had lower performance than the current offer across the board. We saw a 32.8% decrease in conversion rate for the new eBook offer, which you can see broken down by segment below, resulting in fewer engagements in both clicks and donations. These results helped us reach a conclusion to keep the existing offer in place rather than rolling out the new eBook offer in this high-traffic Dear Reader spot. New is not always better! For future new offers, we will plan to run additional A/B tests to determine which offer will lead to maximum results instead of giving away valuable real estate to an offer with unknown performance and risk losing potential revenue and have to make up for lost time later. This experimental approach with initially rolling out to only 50% of site visitors will help us focus less on if the offer is fresh and instead care more about if the offer works. 

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 52.4% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 29.7% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 61.9% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 49.1% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	All Traffic visitors had a 32.8% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 23.0% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 40.4% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 34.5% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 46.6% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How an offer-focused welcome series impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on promoting similar content offers for newly acquired emails in a welcome series will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2020 - 11/24/2020
When The Fund for American Studies was just beginning to focus on building their online program, they began promoting a downloadable PDF resource for prospecting targets to request. They would market this offer with digital ads, which directed people to click to a landing page with an email signup form to have the resource sent to them. Finally, when they completed the registration form on the landing page, they would be directed to an instant donation page.
Initially, they did not have many content offers to provide to prospects, which forced them to initially launch with a welcome series that was focused on telling the story of the organization, or “proving the concept” of their impact by sharing story-based appeals that focused on high-profile alumni. This series also ended with a direct appeal, which was trying to get them to become a first-time donor.
Over time, the program began to build very quickly, and the organization expanded the number of available “content offers” to the market.
They wondered if moving them from the org-centered welcome series to a series that was focused on promoting more content offers like the one they had downloaded would improve their ability to produce first-time donors that had just been acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 99.8%, we observed that the new offer-focused welcome series improved the donor conversion rate by +920%.

Beyond that, here are some additional metrics that were achieved along with this new approach during the study period:
Email Click Rate: +2.8% (LoC: 79.5%)
 	Email Open Rate: +86.1% (LoC: 100%)
 	Unsubscribe Rate: -42.0% (LoC: 100%)
 	Revenue: +1,091.4% (LoC: 99.9%)
The "control" welcome series ran for a substantially longer period of time (a year) vs. the new "treatment" (offer-focused) welcome series only ran for around 45-days.
 	The "treatment" experience overlapped the "control" experience for the time that the "treatment" experience was running.
 	We split the contacts by adding a pre-checked checkbox on the signup forms. If the box remained checked, they would go to the "control" experience, whereas if it were unchecked, those emails would go into the "treatment" experience.
 	During this time of overlapping experiences that were running, roughly 89% of the people that signed up went into the "control" experience, and only 11% went to the "treatment" experience.
 	It can also be assumed that the people that unchecked the box were presumably a less motivated audience than those that left the box checked upon signing up—why? Because they intentionally took a step to say "I don't want anything else from you" when they signed up. Even so, this much smaller and assumed to be less motivated audience outperformed the "control" experience not only for the 45-day period of time when they were running simultaneously, but also when the performance data for the "control" experience was expanded to include a year's worth of results, which is what is included in the study data above.
Why would this new welcome series be so much more effective than the control?

experiments.title: How specifically focusing on non-donors affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that acknowledging that a donor has not given for non-donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/29/2022
CaringBridge was running an appeal celebrating their 25th anniversary. Earlier in the campaign, they had tested the concept of asking donors who had given for three consecutive years to avoid “breaking the streak”, with much success. In their final campaign email, they wanted to test whether specifically messaging to non-donors with language that acknowledged that they hadn’t given before would inspire them to give.
Their control email was a “last-day”, urgency-focused message. They added a single line of copy: “my notes show you’ve never supported CaringBridge before”. They used this careful language intentionally, as they didn’t know if they might be reaching a donor through a secondary email and didn’t want to anger them.
Then, they split the audience of non-donors and sent the two emails to measure the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment segment, with the additional sentence "my notes show you've never supported CaringBridge before", produced a 176.3% increase in conversion rate, delivering nearly twice as many donors from a similar-size segment.
This shows that non-donors either aren't aware that they aren't supporters, or that the reminder of their current "status" is a powerful motivator to give.
This concept may function like a "last date given", where donors are reminded that it has been some time since they have given.

experiments.title: How migrating systems can improve conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving to a new system have any significant impact on email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2018 - 03/08/2018
Buckner International was in the final stages of migrating from one email and landing page system to another. Before moving the email acquisition campaign entirely, we wanted to verify that there were no unforeseen issues or technical bugs that would pop up. To do this, we created an A/A experiment in which both pages were nearly identical; the only major difference was the system used to host the pages and send the emails.
Our goal with this experiment was to demonstrate that there was no negative impact on conversion. Basically, with this kind of test, no statistical significance would have been considered a win.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a full 10 days, we discovered that the new system was actually able to fix a bug that we didn't know we had. It resulted in a 15% increase in conversion.

We had seen comments in Facebook (which was the main traffic source for this campaign) about people having trouble accessing the resource. After multiple tests, we were unable to reproduce any issue so we assumed it was a user error given the already high conversion rate. These results would indicate that the issue did exist and the new system was able to correctly address it.

experiments.title: How a “one click” donation experience impacted giving from current donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction for returning donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2020 - 12/21/2020
During The Heritage Foundation’s year-end campaign, we wanted to streamline the process to capture gifts from active members. To reduce friction from filling out multiple form fields, and simplify the gift processing option for existing donors, we presented a “one click” experience similar to what one might experience with other online transactions as a repeat visitor, like Amazon or Paypal. Instead of the donor having to re-enter payment details, we presented them with a message indicating the last 4 digits of the most recent card they used on a past donation while still giving them the option to change or update their payment method. We also created a second treatment that allowed the donor to “swipe” the button to make a gift using their past payment details.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By acknowledging our pre-existing relationship with the donor and reducing friction for mobile visitors we saw a 122.1% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence. Since we know the year-end giving donation page receives highly motivated traffic during the month of December, we would want to re-test this experience during other campaigns as well to compare results but given the increase in both conversion rate and revenue, we will move forward with this approach for future appeals to current members.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 242.9% increase with a 98% level of confidence.New visitors had a 95.7% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radical change to a main donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction from the donation experience for potential donors will achieve a greater number of donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2022 - 05/23/2022
I Am Second is a ministry that uses film to capture the true and authentic story of individuals whose lives have been changed when they put Jesus first in their lives. I Am Second has huge brand awareness due in large part to the celebrity nature of the film subjects, but they do not put a large focus on fundraising due to legacy donor support.
Usually, NextAfter suggests making a singular change to a control in order to isolate lift and motivation. In this case, we decided to make a radical change to the main donation page to test the removal of a variety of items that we feel are causing friction. Here is a summary of what was changed:
We swapped out donation platforms (keeping the donation form fill on a singular page)
Changed the headline to align with the organizational value proposition
Removed the navigational header
Changed the body copy to be more donor-centric, vs org centric
Removed footer elements
It is important to note that the action being tested and tracked was the donate button. As these were two different donation platforms (due to challenges with making changes to the donation pathway testing), we wanted to isolate the actions to just button clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While I am Second does have campaign-specific donor appeals that generate a decent amount of traffic to their campaign-specific donation pages, their main donation page has always been secondary to the overall goal. As such, it does not receive much traffic, and therefore sample sizes can be difficult to achieve validity. However, in this case, we were able to see an 8.7% increase in donor conversions (the donate button click) with a 96.3% level of confidence.
Key takeaways: While sample sizes are low, we still reached validity in the experiment. However, since we are changing so many things radically, it would be best to work backwards, seeing what we could add back into the page w/out creating more friction. Also, since we used two different donation platforms, it would be interesting to see if we could rerun the experiment on one platform.
As I Am Second is undergoing an entire site redesign, they plan on using the learnings from this experiment to guide changes to the new main donation page.
Additional observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning  visitors had a 14.3% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 9.7% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How reducing friction by removing a phone number field impacted gifts

rows.name: 1 Samuel Donation Page - No Phone Number Field

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing form friction by eliminating optional fields for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2022 - 05/14/2022
We were looking for test ideas for an upcoming fiscal year end campaign. We were launching a new free online course ahead of the FYE campaign and knew that we would be getting lots of traffic from sending the offer to the housefile so we wanted to put a test in place on the donation page. We were hoping to get a learning ahead of the fiscal year end campaign that would help us present the most optimized version of the donation form during the campaign. We noticed the donation form fields take up a good amount of space on the page and it looks like a lengthy and difficult process. We wanted to remove the barrier of “work” from our donation page visitors so we asked if there were any unnecessary form fields that could be eliminated as well as any fields that could be grouped together to reduce the amount of space they were taking up on the page. We pulled out phone number and put City/State/Zip all on the same row so it condensed the appearance of the form fields.
experiments.keyLearnings: By taking out the phone number field, even though it was optional and not required, we saw a 77.1% increase in conversion rate with 98.4% level of confidence. Revenue also experienced a 66.8% increase with a 96% level of confidence. We believe removing the phone number field reduced anxiety for page visitors as they didn't have to wonder "Why do you need my phone number in order for me to make a gift?" or "Who is going to contact me and how often if I give away this personal piece of information?"  We were also able to reduce friction by making the form appear shorter and look like less work for the donor when filling out the form. Going forward into the FYE campaign, we will remove the phone number field on the donation form and find ways to ask for it after the gift is made rather than interrupting the donation process to get this information.

experiments.title: How using direct and specific language in an email appeal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity to an email appeal  for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/16/2022
Navigators was running a high urgency campaign for Eagle Lake. They had slow traction for the first half of the campaign so we wanted to test using more direct, specific language in their email appeal. The previous emails had passive language that encouraged the prospective donor to “support the next generation.” Instead, we wanted to tell them specifically to send a kid to camp. For additional specificity, we added how many students still needed scholarships and how much it cost to send one child to camp. Our hypothesis is that this will give the prospective donor a clearer, more tangible goal and in turn would generate more donations.
As an additional note: this email was #6 in the campaign – 1 week removed from the final deadline and right before the urgency-focused emails were sent.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 218% lift in donor conversion rate with a 98% level of confidence. These results indicate that the Navigators' audience responds more to a direct specific ask and goal. Being direct on what results from their gift "sending a child to camp" vs a macro goal of "impacting the next generation" will inspire more gifts.

experiments.title: How using an authoritative figure in a dear reader impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an authoritative figure to a dear reader for blog readers will achieve a higher email conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2022 - 05/13/2022
RTB’s website is rich with blogs and content. Most of their traffic is driven by these blogs and articles. In order to maximize results from this traffic we wanted to add a dear reader at the end of the blog content to capture more names. We wanted to test how humanizing the dear reader by making it sound like it’s coming from an authority figure (Hugh Ross, founder and President) would impact email acquisition. We hypothesized that readers would respond more to seeing Hugh’s face and reading a message from him, especially since we were specifically targeting Hugh’s blogs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Results of this experiment indicate that RTB's audience are more likely to respond when hearing from a specific person rather from the organization. Humanizing messaging and using an authority figure has proven to be a viable strategy for RTB. This experiment resulted in a valid 62% increase in email acquisition with a level of confidence of 98%. Furthermore, people were more likely to give through the dear reader that came from Hugh. We saw a 197% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 92% (not validated by a directional lift).

experiments.title: How adding a dear reader to high traffic blogs impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a topically relevant dear reader for google grant traffic will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2022 - 05/13/2022
RTB has been driving traffic to their blogs using Google grant but not seeing a lot of traction in terms of name acquisition. Their blogs mostly just had CTA’s at the bottom to share on social, email, etc. By adding a dear reader that featured an offer that was relevant to the blog content, we hypothesized that we would see an increase in email acquisition. We specifically targeted the top 15 blogs that received the most traffic from the google grant.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment resulted in a valid 980% lift in email acquisition with a level of confidence of 99%. By giving audiences a clear offer we were able to dramatically increase their email acquisition. However, in reviewing the sample size in comparison to the conversion rate, we want to test placing this offer higher up above the fold or using it as an exit intent pop up. While we're getting a lot of traffic to these pages, readers may not be scrolling all the way to the bottom of the blog. There's opportunity to catch them earlier in their experience.

experiments.title: How switching to a digital premium for Parables of Jesus impacts email acquisition rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a digital premium for a Facebook audience will achieve a change in email acquisition rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2022 - 05/11/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Most of their new name acquisition efforts (not donor acquisition) focused around offering up brochures and pamphlets that were then mailed to the recipient. Because this fulfillment process usually took a month, we hypothesized that offering an instant digital download of the same exact premium, in PDF format, would drive a higher email acquisition rate at a much lower cost to acquire.
This is an extension of an experiment we tested with a single premium in April; for this experiment, we were testing the Parables of Jesus
experiments.keyLearnings:
Pathway to Victory was able to achieve a 115% increase in email conversion rates, with a 100% level of confidence. Moreover, the cost to acquire an email address was 6% lower on the digital premium.
We believe this positive result is due to a combination of factors:
By offering up immediate gratification through a digital download, versus potentially waiting up to a month to receive your resource in the mail, the audience was much more likely to sign up to receive it.By not requiring a full mailing address for the digital version, we removed some friction in the sign-up process.
We will continue to test this hypothesis, as well as track the downstream impact on revenue of these email sign-ups.

experiments.title: How placing a premium offer higher on the homepage affected donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by bringing the premium offer higher on the homepage for web viewers that we will achieve increased donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2022 - 05/04/2022
Pathway to Victory (PTV) is a ministry with a donor base built on premium offers that are publicized on both offline (TV, radio, etc) and online channels. Each month a new premium offer is promoted as a resource to donors when they make a donation to the organization. As this is the main source of revenue for PTV, we wanted to test bringing the premium higher on the homepage so that it is above the fold and one of the first things viewers see.
All PTV promotions (direct mail, TV, radio, email, paid, etc.) point to the same premium each month. So when someone visits the PTV website, they are likely, in search for that specific premium, book or bundle.
By moving the offer higher on the homepage, we hypothesise that more people will make a donation for the book as it is easier to locate and learn more. We also added additional information about the specific premium being offered so that there is more clarity around the premium and what is being offered.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By bringing the premium higher on the homepage, and thereby removing friction to getting to the month's featured premium, we saw a 17.2% increase in donations. With making the premium more visible and easily accessible to viewers, more donors were likely to take advantage of the premium offer.
Since we saw positive results with this experiment, we are going to run it again when a premium releases to ensure that we can get repeatable results. If results stay positive, we will move forward with always having the premium in the higher spot.
Future experiment ideas, related to this one, includes making the homepage banner image only about the premium and adding an exit intent popup that highlights that month's premium offer.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page impacts average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium to an instant donation page for ebook downloaders will achieve a higher average gift .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2022 - 05/07/2022
The Navigators have an offer running on Facebook for the How to Memorize Scripture  ebook. This has been one of their more popular ebooks on Facebook. We had a lot of traffic coming through these ads an a high sign up rate. In order to increase ROI for this offer, we wanted to increase the average gift that is given on the instant donation page. In order to do so, we wanted to run an experiment where we would use a thematically relevant resource (Topical Memory System) as a premium for a gift of $50 or more.
Traffic to this funnel is primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for a little over two weeks we saw a 183.6% valid lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 99.7%. When page visitors were given a specific ask of $50 or more and an incentive in the form of a premium, the average gift was $47. When there was no specific ask amount or premium incentive, the average gift was $15. We can conclude that The Navigators' audiences are motivated by premium offers and are more likely to give a larger gift when presented with a specific amount and product premium. There was virtually no difference in donor conversion rate so this informs us that the premium is not why they give but rather an incentive to give an even larger gift than perhaps initially intended.
The next step in testing for The Navigators is to consolidate the giving process in 1-2 steps instead of the current 3-step approach.
