experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form impacted donor conversion on acquisition pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tabbed form  for acquisition pages will achieve an increase in overall giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2022 - 04/25/2022
When reviewing the layout of the donation form, we wanted to know if having a visual representation of a tabbed form. The idea was to find out before moving to a new donation platform if presenting the different gift frequency choices, one-time or monthly, as their own separate experiences would increase donor conversion. We are getting ready to move to a new donation platform that would allow us to use this feature across all donation forms, so we wanted to find out how it impacted overall giving before making the change. The second version of this test will be to see how this tabbed approach impacts recurring donors by featuring specific messaging highlighting the need and benefits of monthly giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: While our sample size of transactions was small, we did see a 508% increase in overall donor conversion with a 98.7% level of confidence after running for two weeks. We also saw a 437.0% increase in revenue on the treatment with a 95% level of confidence. These lifts were consistent across email visitors, desktop visitors, and returning visitors. By adding clarity to the process surrounding the giving frequency selection on the pages, were able to increase both transactions and revenue. We will be moving forward with the tabbed version of the form as the default format on acquisition offers in the new donation platform based on these results as we have no reason to believe this will decrease overall conversion if we do.

experiments.title: How removing a premium offer on a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the premium on RTB’s main donation page for page visitors will achieve either a marginal difference in results or an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2021 - 07/30/2021
RTB has typically always had a a premium on their main donate page that cycles out every month and/or quarter. We wanted to test removing the premium offer to see how this change would impact donations and overall revenue. We thought that the premium ask may be overwhelming visitors, leading to anxiety and friction. Furthermore, we wanted to see if perhaps RTB did not need a premium at all as an incentive. Would prospective donors still give if they did not have a premium option. If the difference was negligible, the case could be made that the premium isn’t always needed and there could be additional cost saved for the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 69.5% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence. The results indicate that RTB's audience is highly motivated by the premium offer on the donation page. With a 100% confidence, we know that their constituents are conditioned to expect the premium on the donation page and are more likely to give when it is featured. We have concluded to keep the premium on the donation page. Given that the premium is such a strong driver, it is recommended that we add the premium option to the instant donation pages to their offers that have a low ROI.

It is recommended that we next test into using the founder's (Dan Burke) image and signature to humanize the message in the dear reader to see if that provides an additional lift in conversions.

experiments.title: How a different messaging approach affects conversion rate on an offer page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which version of the value proposition for the email appeal template would lead to more downloads?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/19/2022
In this experiment, we were launching a new email appeal resource and came up with two strong ideas for positioning the appeal of the offer. One approach was to assure the reader that they’d have more confidence in the email appeals they would be sending out by following this template. The other approach was to assure them that this template would make their life easer by helping them have a simple and clear approach to writing their appeals.
We created a treatment to address the each version of the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The copy treatment that focused on making life easier for the fundraiser with a simple and clear approach led to a 26.3% increase in conversions compared to the treatment focused in having confidence in results.
This is an interesting insight into the mindset of the fundraiser. For those seeking out a tool like this, they appear to be less motivated by improving the outcomes and more motivated by tools that free up time and allow them to work faster. This is an approach we can continue to test and see if it holds up for other offers outside of this email appeal template.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using acknowledgement copy in the headline for tribute donor prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/03/2022
Tribute widgets provide a large proportion of CaringBridge’s fundraising revenue. In the tribute widget, users are given the opportunity to send a message to their friend along with their donation. However, the team noticed that the ensuing donation page did not acknowledge the fact that they had sent a message, in order to provide context and a “smooth transition” into the full donation process.
They created a treatment with “acknowledgement copy”, or, language that welcomed the donor from their previous step in the headline to see if it improved conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 12.7% increase in conversion—which had a big effect on overall revenue, given the volume that flowed through this page.
This showed that contextual orientation is key for the donor, and making the donation a clear "next step" by acknowledging their previous step helped more people take the next step.

The Navigators expressed the desire to add a zip code field on their offers’ landing pages so as to append constituent data. Before rolling out this change to all their name acquisition forms, we wanted to test this on a specific offer in order to determine impact to email conversion rate. We tested this on their offer titled “Journey Through the Psalms” because of the high sign up rate on the page. This informs us that the test will validate quicker.
Traffic to this page is primarily through Facebook paid ads.

This experiment validated with a 16% decrease in email conversion rate. This means that adding the zip code field created friction among users which caused a decrease in name acquisition. If the purpose of adding the zip code is for appending existing records, the data informs us that there is a valid 25% decrease for returning users. Furthermore, when accounting for the cost per name for this offer and the drop in conversion rate, the cost of a zip code is $0.27 per name.

Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Most of their new name acquisition efforts (not donor acquisition) focused around offering up brochures and pamphlets that were then mailed to the recipient. Because this fulfillment process usually took a month, we hypothesized that offering an instant digital download of the same exact premium, in PDF format, would drive a higher email acquisition rate at a much lower cost to acquire.
We tested this across one single premium, using the same Facebook ad creative, and are now testing this with multiple premiums.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was a large increase in email acquisition rates with the digital premium offer, with almost a 70% increase in email sign-ups. This is most likely due to the fact that we did not need to ask for a physical address in order to mail the digital premium, coupled with the instant gratification of receiving the download.
Moreover, the cost to acquire an email signup using the digital premium was also significantly less, with a $0.59 cost per email signup on the digital premium, and a $6.24 cost per email signup on the physical premium.

experiments.title: How asking for less than a donor’s last gift impacts emails donations for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking a donor for less than their last gift for an email audience will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2022 - 04/30/2022
As part of their April campaign, Pathway to Victory wanted to test a relatively new concept known as the downgraded ask. For this experiment, they created a chart of standards based upon the donors’ previous highest contribution where they would ask for a certain number of donors to give a specific amount. For the treatment version of this ask, they asked for a level below what the donor had previously given and added language acknowledging that they were being asked for less.
This was then tested across an email campaign that included 3 separate emails, with a resend 3 days after the initial send of each.
Pathway to Victory had also never tested included a donors’ previous last gift, so this was a completely new approach for their email file.
We tested this only on the donor file, with the rest of the file receiving the control version that did not have a downgrade ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
From a donor conversion standpoint, there is a 70% increase in conversion rates. When looking at the results from a revenue perspective, the positive trend continues, with a 85% increase in revenue per visitor and a level of confidence of 100% due to the larger number of gifts made to the treatment version. There was also a 26% increase in click-through rates a 100% level of confidence. And finally, of those that clicked the links to donate, 35% of them were more likely to convert to donors with the downgrade treatment version.
With the current economic climate (inflation at an all time high), positioning the downgrade ask with a reference to that climate, as well as combining it with a number of donors needed at a given giving level, is helping to drive a more positive response to the treatment version, as well as create a sense of community that is banding together to help provide support to Pathway to Victory.

Hoover Institution has a quarterly publication called the Hoover Digest, which is a compilation of recent articles from Hoover scholars. A donor can receive all ‘subscription’ to all 4 publications by making a donation over $100. In this experiment, we tested whether adding the premium offer to a donation page from an ebook offer impacted donor conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of the Hoover Digest premium drove down conversion rates, as well as the average gift. While this experiment did not reach the total level of donations that would provide a valid result, directionally it showed that the Digest negatively impacted results. This could be attributed to the lack of awareness of what Hoover does, as well as nature of the Digest offer, as it contains articles on many topics, and therefore might not be appealing to someone going through the funnel for a specific offer.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an offer in a dear reader that is relevant to the author for readers will achieve a higher email conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2022 - 04/10/2022
Reasons to Believe is a science-faith organization that has a plethora of blog content that receives a lot of traffic. They also have specific scholars/authors who have their own blogs and articles. We wanted to see if using an offer in a dear reader that is specific and relevant to the author will yield a higher email conversion rate. In this particular case we were targeting Fazale Rana’s articles. In this experiment we used an evangelism survey as the control and an eBook authored by Fazale Rana as the treatment.
All traffic for this experiment is from organic web traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Using the eBook that was related to the author gave us a valid 50% lift in email conversion rate. This is consistent with the previous experiment's (Hugh Ross' blog/dear reader) findings. In addition to a lift in email conversion, we also see a lift in donations and revenue. However, it is still unclear as to whether the lift was caused by the author relevance or the fact that the ebook is simply a better offer than the survey. We are following up with an experiment that uses the ebook dear reader and uses first person language so it sounds like it's from the author himself.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Donations experienced a 252.7% increase with a 96% level of confidence.Revenue experienced a 601.1% increase with a 96% level of confidence.

Reasons to Believe is a science-faith organization that has a plethora of blog content that receives a lot of traffic. They also have specific scholars/authors who have their own blogs and articles. We wanted to see if using an offer in a dear reader that is specific and relevant to the author will yield a higher email conversion rate. In this particular case we were targeting Hugh Ross’s articles. In this experiment we used an evangelism survey as the control and an eBook authored by Hugh Ross as the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this experiment we saw a valid 89% lift in email acquisition. This informs us that relevancy is an important aspect in inspiring email conversions. While this validated our hypothesis, it is difficult to discern what aspect of the offer drove our results. It could be that the eBook offer was a more appealing offer than the survey offer. In order to hone in on the use of authority, we will test making the dear reader come from the author himself, using his headshot and changing the copy to be first person.

experiments.title: How shortening a matching gift announcement impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the matching gift language and making it more direct for non-donors will achieve a donor conversion and revenue increase.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2022 - 03/13/2022
A generous donor made a matching gift available to the Canadian Taxpayer Federation so that they could specifically inspire non-donors to become first-time donors.
They wanted to know how a matching gift announcement for non-donors who just signed a petition would react and whether or not the matching gift would increase giving for this audience.
Initially, we tested a message that took its time to explain the importance of the match and why the donor should take advantage of it. Based upon previous research on the Taxpayer donor audience, we hypothesized that shortening the message and being more direct about the existence of the match would be the first test that we should try to inspire non-donors to make their first gift (and have it matched by this generous donor).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 98.8%, we observed an increase in dollars raised by +23.4% — all from visitors who had no giving history to the organization! This revenue lift was driven by two factors, which are:
We saw an increase of 16.9% in donor conversion rate (people saying "yes" to make a gift of any amount), andWhen someone decided to give through the treatment, they decided to give a gift that was 5.2% higher than donors in the control.
So, not only did we get more people to say yes, but when they did, they gave more generously — all because of the matching gift message.
It's important to note that in a previous experiment, we tested a variant of the matching gift announcement with the same visual appearance and placement on the donation form (and on the same pages), but that variant actually decreased donor conversion rate by -32%.
If we would have stopped there, it's very possible that we would not have been able to inspire these first-time donors to say "yes" at a higher rate and gift amount.
It's our hypothesis that the overly verbose announcement of the matching gift in the first test variant was long enough to cause a wrap, and was a paragraph that used multiple sentences to say what this variation said in a single line of text and in one sentence.
The key learning here is this: When announcing a matching gift, get straight to the point. Spending too much time trying to justify why someone should make a gift to have it matched by another donor may not be necessary.
By communicating too much at this point in the giving experience, it's very likely that we introduce anxiety — or "psychological resistance" — to the visitors to this page to say "yes" to making a gift at all.
By shortening the copy and being more direct, we mitigated that resistance, and unlocked generosity that lifted overall results.

experiments.title: How the placement of your end of article ad impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the dear reader ad up ahead of the related articles for CampusReform.org readers will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/24/2021 - 02/25/2022
When the Campus Reform website was redesigned to better manage articles and update the front-end design, the web development team that rebuilt the news website hard-coded the ad that was placed at the end of the article (which we call a “Dear Reader” ad) in the wrong location — moving it below the “related articles” and social share links.
We wanted to run an experiment to determine whether the placement actually mattered at generating email signups on the pledge and subsequently donations/revenue on the subsequent instant donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 99.2%, we observed an increase in donor conversion rate of +511%!
We also observed an increase of emails acquired by +171.6% (LoC: 99%) and drove significantly more dollars through this treatment experience.
When it comes to where to place your end of article ad (or "Dear Reader" ad) — the placement absolutely matters. In this case, it just makes sense when you take a moment to think about it.
When a reader clicks a link to read an article, they came there to read the article. Previous experimentation with ad placements on long-form article content within news websites shows us that the placement at the end of the article is the most productive location in which to place your ads. Why? Because the reader came to read the ad — so, it's only after the value exchange has been completed (that you've allowed them to read the entire article they came to read) that they'll consider another action.
Therefore, what comes next matters the most.
If the goal is to promote additional articles, then showing the "related articles" next would make sense.
In this case, we're optimizing to increase the number of emails, donations, and revenue we're producing from readers of our articles. Therefore, promoting our donor engagement pathway (or "Dear Reader" ad) right after the article ends makes the most sense to drive the largest response rates through that pathway.

experiments.title: How a micro-goal slide in impacts donor conversion during an emergency over an alert banner
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro goal for website visitors during an emergency  will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2022 - 03/09/2022
When emergency relief is needed somewhere around the world, Save the Children traditionally places an emergency alert banner on their site. The banner can’t be missed on the site because it takes up quite a bit of real estate, is bright red, and says “Emergency Alert”. Save has tested the copy that is on this banner in the past, but there hadn’t been significant testing around the tactical approach of driving urgency and conversion.
When the Ukraine crisis hit, Save saw an unprecedented amount of traffic to their website and wanted to see if they could find an another way to convert this high volume of traffic into donations. To test this, we developed a slide-in module that used a micro-goal of the number of donors needed this hour to help children in crisis. The micro-goal numbers in the slide in were dynamic and reset every hour. If a user clicked “donate now,” and then returned to the homepage within the hour, the number would be lower than what they saw previously. The slide in module was sticky and stayed in view when the user scrolled down the page and had the ability to be removed via clicking on the “x” in the top right corner, similar to the emergency banner. Both of these CTA’s drove to the same donation page for the control and treatment
experiments.keyLearnings:
The micro-goal slide in banner increase donor conversion in the five days that this ran by 21% (with a 99% LoC)! There was also a proportional decline in the number of undesignated purchases. This was of much interest, as users were directed to an undesignated form in both the variant and the control. Our assessment is that users shown the variant were better able to determine the slider was headed to a more general donation form – be it from a difference in copy (context/phrasing), the slider color, or slider location. The ability to identify form intent led variant users to seek out a form that matched their motivations (in this case, Ukraine), leading to an increase in emergency purchases and a decrease in undesignated purchases. As there was no difference in overall purchases, conversion rate, or revenue/visitor, there was possibly less potential friction for variant users. A few days into the test, there was a slight uptick in call center donors who requested their gifts be specifically designated for Ukraine. We shifted 100% of test traffic to the variant, as these users were more likely to give an emergency gift (potentially reducing call center calls). Shortly after, we shifted fundraising from Ukraine designated to CEF, which made it difficult to measure the impact of the shift.
We know that the emergency alert banner has worked in the past, but when users see a specific goal with a specific timeline/deadline, this increases the urgency of the ask and then increases the motivation and conversion of visitors.
Next steps: Additional testing is needed with this tactic and also the emergency alert banner to better identify if it was the micro goal or the slide in functionality or some other element that really led to the success of the variant.

experiments.title: How a one-column format donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reformatting the existing two-column donation form page layout to a single column for potential donors will reduce cognitive friction, increasing focus and improving donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2022 - 03/26/2022
Upon reviewing the existing Boy Scouts of America donation page layout and flow, we recognized an opportunity to conduct an experiment based on previous NextAfter experiments and learnings. Based on this library of experiments, we made several significant changes to the layout of this critical donation page.
We first removed the imagery to elevate the communication priority of the reinforcing copy on the page. Next, we refined the layout from a two-column layout to a single-column format, introducing a more linear flow we believe reduces cognitive load that leads to a higher percentage of donors completing the form and making a donation. We also emphasized the perceived security of this form by boxing the payment information fields and reinforcing trust with the use of a lock icon, a web standard that most online shoppers today are familiar with.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The redesigned single-column donation page layout produced a 126% lift in donor conversion rate. This experiment shows us that the single-column form layout reduces user friction, introducing a more linear user journey that produced a much more likely positive donation outcome.
This provides many future opportunities for experimentation for BSA, demonstrating conclusively that relatively simple to execute form optimizations can measurably reduce friction and increase donor conversion.
Additional observations of the impact of this experiment show that this CVR was improved across all core visitor segments:
Organic Search visitors had a 312.5% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 414.3% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.New visitors had a 108.0% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 180.0% increase in donations with a 90% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 40.0% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How using a drop down option instead of an open text field on a calculator impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that that using drop-down fields for Home Equity Tax Calculator visitors will achieve an increase in email signup rate and will lift donor conversions overall.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/15/2022 - 03/13/2022
As a part of a new campaign to promote a calculator that would help citizens determine what a tax bill on the sale of their home if a proposed Home Equity Tax Bill were to pass, the Canadian Taxpayer Federation wanted to experiment with drop-down fields on the calculator instead of the open text fields in the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we observed a valid increase of +13.9% (LoC: 100%) in email signup rate, we noted that the visitors coming through the drop-down treatment experience were 58% less likely to make a donation.
Our hypothesis is that the drop-downs, which used a slightly different calculation may have been showing a lower tax bill forecast than when visitors entered their own information specifically.
Therefore, we decided to turn off this experience and set up a new experiment designed to mitigate anxiety (psychological resistance to filling out the calculator form) to see if we could drive the higher email signup rates experienced within the drop-down experience, but keep the higher donor conversion rate values found in this control experience.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Email Signup Rate experienced a 13.9% increase with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding an instant donation page after a webinar registration process affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an instant donation page for webinar registrants will achieve more revenue .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/21/2022
The Avila foundation has regularly scheduled webinars each month. Using ticket tailor, they have historically added a suggested donation of $15 upon registration. We wanted to test using an instant donation page at the end of the process instead of the ask during registration. The idea is that this could potentially produce more revenue and encourage a higher gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment has validated and revealed that Avila's audience responds more to the $15 suggested donation at registration as opposed to the instant donation page. Average gift was comparable and when looking at donor acquisition rate, the control clearly outnumbered the treatment as well. This could be for varying reasons. The Avila audience might already be conditioned to give at the point of registration instead of the end. There also seems to be an anomaly in the data. While the sample size of emails opened was evenly split, the actual views of the registration page varied (even though the email was exactly the same). 1078 from the control actually saw the registration page whereas only 478 participants from the treatment saw the registration page.
Our recommendation is to retest this in light of the anomaly in the data. Another test idea could be to keep the suggested donation amount at registration but test into: a higher gift amount or adding a line of value prop language to inspire a higher conversion rate.

experiments.title: How reordering elements on a journal page to clarify calls to action affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clarifying the calls to action for journal readers will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/06/2022 - 01/17/2022
CaringBridge’s tribute widgets on their journal pages were one of the primary sources of donations. They were always seeking to find ways to improve the user experience and increase revenue.
As they reviewed the page, they sought to clean up an area that seemed confusing from an experience perspective. At the end of a journal entry, the user was prompted to “heart”, “comment”, or “share” the post, followed by a quick tip encouraging the user to comment. Immediately below that was the tribute widget, where they could leave a message and start the gift process. And below that was the actual journal section.
The team wondered if this was confusing—the initial CTAs were all about journal writing, but the tribute widget was wedged between those CTAs and the section where they could comment.
They designed an experience that moved all of the journal-related activities below the tribute widget in order to provide separation between the two calls-to-action and activities. They closely monitored journal comments as well as donations to determine whether this was an improvement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the separated CTAs for tribute widget and journal comment, produced a 48.2% increase in donor conversion rate, which led to a 43% increase in revenue.
Some of this had to do with the prioritization of the tribute widget over the journal comments—but journal comments were not seriously affected, with only a slight 5.6% decrease that did not statistically validate.
This is a great example of how testing design principles can increase conversion.

experiments.title: How clarifying the intended action and donation amount affects sponsor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the ask amount and call to action for people who click the main CTA will achieve an increase in completed sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2021 - 01/11/2022
Prison Fellowship International had been running heavy acquisition for online sponsorship acquisition for more than a year. At the top of the page, people were introduced to a child and given the chance to sponsor them. When they clicked the “sponsor now” button, they were taken to a re-skinned RaiseDonors form at the bottom.
The team noticed upon examination that once you reached the bottom of the page, the form didn’t look like a traditional donation form. First, there was no price or array. This was because sponsorship only has one price ($39/month). So the team set up a treatment that installed a single button, preselected, for $39/month to reinforce the ask amount.
There was one more missing link between the top and the bottom of the page: the intended action was not repeated. Even though they clicked “sponsor now” to get down there, it was disorienting to have no ask amount and no CTA. They added the language “Sponsor [ChildName] Now”. Once they did that, they added another tab to the form that gave the donor an out from the $39/monthly gift—”give a one-time gift”. This defaulted to an array which could possibly attract additional revenue from people who were not ready to commit to a sponsorship.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The changes to the donation form produced a 126.5% increase in conversion. This revealed that there was confusion and disorientation in the mind of the donor once they got to the bottom of the page. The added language and reinforced CTA and price point helped more people finish the process. In fact, some amount of this conversion increase was just because it was visually clear that it was a donation form.
Surprisingly, during the test period, the extra tab did not generate any additional gifts, although since the treatment was rolled out, it has generated several hundred dollars in revenue.

experiments.title: How asking for less than a donor’s highest gift impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than a donor’s highest gift for donors will achieve actually increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2022 - 02/18/2022
As a part of a February matching gift campaign, the Leadership Institute wanted to experiment with whether asking a donor for less than their highest gift would impact overall donor conversion rate and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When personalizing the ask amount, and setting it at a lower value than the donor's highest gift amount, we observed that the donor conversion rate increased by +367.6% (with a confidence level of 99.2%).
Overall, we also observed a +640.5% increase in revenue (with a level of confidence of 97.7%), although we did not validate that study due to only having achieved about half of the samples we would normally need on a revenue lift study.
One final question we wanted to answer was: How did the actual gift the donor make compare to the amount they were asked for?
In reviewing the treatment segment, we observed the following outcomes:
43% of the gifts and 60% of the revenue came from people who gave more than the amount we asked for in the treatment segment.50% of the gifts and 36% of the revenue came from people who gave exactly what we asked for.7% of the gifts and 4% of the revenue came from people who gave less than the amount we asked for in the treatment segment.
Meanwhile, all of the gifts secured in the "control" segment actually gave less than what was asked for.
This incredibly effective, yet simple approach undoubtedly increases donor responses rates — and drives greater revenue from those who respond, as well. It would be good to retest this across a longer span of emails within a campaign to see if it holds up, or if it can be overused, but it certainly appears that this approach can drive both a higher response rate and raise more money overall.

experiments.title: How breaking your campaign goal down for non-donors impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that breaking down the fundraising goal into $10 ask amounts for non-donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2022 - 02/18/2022
As a part of continued testing and experimentation related to what drives the best response rates within the context of a high urgency fundraising campaign from non-donors, the Canadian Taxpayer Federation wanted to test an “open ask” against the $10 ask for non-donors to verify whether or not the specific ask amount would drive results if it was the only variant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We observed a +74.1% increase in donor conversion rate (with a 97.4% level of confidence) with the treatment experience.
Furthermore, we noted an increase in revenue of +141.1% (with a 99.4% level of confidence).
Like with the other study, we found that the control experience only produce just one $100+ donor, while the treatment experience actually produced 9 $100+ donors.
Why? Our thought is that the $10 ask amount for a non-donor makes it easy for someone to click through to the donation page and that the decision to give more is a combination of both (a) a good gift array, and (b) compelling donation page copy (that builds momentum for donors to give more).
Overall, it appears that the $10 ask amount is the way to go with non-donors, as this verifies the lift that we saw with previous experiences of a similar experience.

experiments.title: How the amount you ask for in your campaign impacts your non-donor response rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ask amount and donor goal drives the best response rates from non-donors within the context of a fundraising campaign?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2022
As the Canadian Taxpayer Federation continued experimenting with breaking their campaign goal down into smaller, more achievable goals, they wanted to experiment with what drove the greatest non-donor response rates.
To experiment with what drives the best response rates, they broke their campaign goal and ask amounts into four groups.
Those groups were:
$10
$25
$50
$100
They then built a treatment like a previous email used for the same campaign that broke down the fundraising goal into one of those four ask amounts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In total, asking in this way showed an aggregate lift of +126.7% in donor conversion rate (with a 100% level of confidence).
There's no doubt that asking in this way drives non-donors to respond at a higher rate (more than 2x when compared to the control experience).
In terms of what ask amount drives the greatest response and revenue, as shown above, the evidence points to asking for $10 from non-donors (since that had the largest lift — +270.3% in donor conversion rate, fully validated with a confidence score of 99.9%).
But the question is: Are we leaving money on the table by asking for so little?
The answer? ABSOLUTELY NOT.
Here is how revenue increased when compared to the control ask experience across the various ask amount segments:
$10 Ask Amount: +147.7% (LoC: 99.3%)$25 Ask Amount: +97.5% (LoC: 97.5%)$50 Ask Amount: +124.1% (LoC: 98.1%)$100 Ask Amount: +49.7% (LoC: 83.9%)
The only ask category that didn't fully validate as an increase from the ask amounts listed above was the $100 ask amount category, but there is directional data that points towards that lifting results, as well.
Additionally, here is how the percentage of gifts and revenue were spread across the various ask amount categories within the treatment segment:
$10 Ask Amount: 41% of gifts & 32% of revenue raised in the treatment segment$25 Ask Amount: 24% of gifts & 24% of revenue raised in the treatment segment$50 Ask Amount: 21% of gifts & 27% of revenue raised in the treatment segment$100 Ask Amount: 13% of gifts & 17% of revenue raised in the treatment segment
This means that asking for $10 from non-donors not only generated the greatest response rate but also drove the largest share of gifts and revenue.
Finally, we wanted to check to see if we received a disproportionately lower number of $100+ gifts in the lower ask segments of the treatment. Here is what we found: The $10 ask amount produced 5/12432 (or 0.04% of gifts) at the $100 gift level, while the $100 ask segment produced just 6/12476 (or 0.05% of gifts).
Simply put, this means that asking for $10 from non-donors produced just as many $100+ gifts, while also producing 3.1x the number of gifts compared to the number secured within the $100 ask segment, and 1.9x the revenue from that segment, as well.
All of this is to say: It appears that asking for $10 not only activated the highest number of donors but it raised the most money of any ask amount from non-donors.
Further experimentation is advised for your program, but at least for the CTF — asking non-donors to give $10 to a high urgency campaign effort is very much the way to go moving forward.

experiments.title: Will using smart content to send a resource in a digest email lead to more resource downloads?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that targeting digest content for our general subscriber list will achieve more conversions than sending a general list of content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2022 - 02/14/2022
For our monthly digest email we wanted to see if sending only one resource that we know a contact has not downloaded yet would be more effective than sending multiple resources, regardless of if they downloaded or not.
To do this we set up our control email which is the typical digest design sharing 4 resources with no smart content. This was sent to half of our general subscribers list.
For the experiment group, we set up a series of 4 emails each only sharing one resource and recognizing that we know they’ve previously downloaded something. We used a workflow in Hubspot to filter contacts by form submission so that each contact would only receive a resource that they haven’t downloaded before. If a contact had downloaded all resources they received a blog post. This was sent to the other half of our general subscribers list.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment led to a 51% increase in conversions with a 99% confidence. This tells us that our contacts are most engaged when there is less clutter and the content is specific to them. Moving forward, we will use smart content to provide a better experience and more tailored content to our subscribers.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium to an instant donation page for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate and higher gift average.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/13/2022 - 02/10/2022
The Navigators launched a new offer, Topical Memory System (TMS) for Kids. The resource offered was a sample of their physical resource. So, we positioned the physical resource as a premium if someone gives $50 or more. The hypothesis is that we would increase donor conversion and average gift for this particular funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this experiment would need 50 transactions to validate a revenue study, the current level of confidence at 96% gives us reason to believe that this is a successful strategy for the TMS for Kids instant donation page. The average gift was successfully increased with the treatment—now over $61.43 (over the suggested ask amount of $50). The treatment for this funnel was deployed to 100% of the traffic.

experiments.title: How breaking down your campaign goal impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that breaking your campaign goal down into smaller target gift amounts for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/04/2022
As a part of a campaign designed to raise money to run radio ads about a new legislative initiative, the Canadian Taxpayer Federation wanted to better understand how best to ask their donors and subscribers to contribute to their campaign.
In an attempt to do something radically different than how they have asked in the past for short-term, project-based fundraising goals, they decided to break their goal down into smaller amounts and publish the number of gifts that it would take at that smaller target goal per donor to make the larger fundraising goal seem more realistic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed an increase in revenue of +81.1%!
We also observed an increase of +103.7% in the donor conversion rate metric (which also validated with a 100% level of confidence).
Our hypothesis as to why the treatment worked so much better than the control is because large fundraising goals make smaller dollar donors feel like the problem or burden to achieve that goal will need to be solved by a donor with much, much larger capacity to give a really large gift. Additionally, it could also make the small-dollar donor feel shame that the smaller gift that they're capable of giving today isn't "good enough" to help the organization—and because of that—they decide to not give a gift at all.
The breakdown of the larger fundraising goal into a smaller gift amount not only drove more than twice the number of gifts created by the control experience but what's really interesting is that the average gift between the two segments only decreased by 11% when compared to the control segment.
Finally, it's worth noting a few more observations, which include:
43% of the treatment donors gave more than what they were asked for and were worth 79% of the funds raised in the segment.42% of the treatment donors gave what they were asked for and were worth 17% of the funds raised in the segment.Only 16% of the treatment donors gave less than the amount they were asked for and were only worth 5% of the funds raised in the segment.While the control segment drove a higher percentage (21.5%) of donors at/above $100 when compared to the treatment (17.6%), the overall number of $100+ donors increased by 65% within the treatment segment.
Our recommendation is to use this campaign goal breakdown approach moving forward for all big fundraising campaigns.
More experimentation could be done in terms of setting daily goals for the campaign or making the ask amount to an amount that is relevant to a donor's most recent gift (or highest gift) or setting a smaller number of donors that need to achieve a gift smaller gift amount (and note that you'll combine those gifts with others to achieve the overall goal).

experiments.title: How introducing “donor fees” impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that allowing donors to pay the credit cards fees for potential donors will achieve and increase in overall revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2022 - 02/06/2022
As part of the instant donor conversion page for an education survey, ACI wanted to find a way to increase the overall revenue. One of the more popular options for nonprofits is a “donor covers fees.” The software being used, “Anedot,” had this feature so we decided to test it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The biggest impact of the test was a 38.5% decrease in conversion for the “donor pays fees” option. It is worth noting that ~60% of donors opted to cover the fees but this resulted in a 3% increase to their average gift which didn’t make up for the significant decrease in overall giving.
A big takeaway from this experiment was the negative impact that adding transactional language had in the conversion process. Donating is a largely irrational action and introducing transactional language like “fees” resulted in potential donors abandoning the process
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment Was that overall revenue experienced a 20.5% decrease with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How emphasizing an offer is free impacted email acquisition

rows.name: Revelation Registration Page with For Free Headline

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing an offer is free for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2021 - 12/02/2021
When reviewing ways to optimize sign-ups for free online courses for DTS, we wanted to find out what would happen if we reminded landing page visitors that the offer was free in the headline. For the treatment page, we added two words: “for free” and split traffic to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was that simply adding the words "for free" to the headline on the online course landing page improved conversion rate by 11% with 98% confidence. Emphasizing the value not only made a difference on the initial landing page, but we saw subtle lifts on the instant donation page following the course registration page as well. Reminding people that they were receiving something for free made more people willing to give away their email address and this approach can be replicated across other course offers for DTS.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
55-64 visitors had a 2.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a campaign-specific “unsubscribe interruptor” affected opt-out rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a campaign-specific unsubscribe interruptor for email recipients will achieve a decrease in unsubscribe rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/11/2022
One of CaringBridge’s internal issues was that people often unsubscribed around their holiday appeal because of the volume of fundraising asks. They wondered if using a “campaign-specific unsubscribe interruptor” would decrease unsubscribe rate.
They created a version of an email that included an offer to “opt out of holiday appeal messaging” for people who “aren’t able to give during this holiday season”. Clicking this link would add people to a suppression list that would only be used for this specific campaign—which would allow them to receive emails in the future and not permanently opt out.
They split the file and monitored unsubscribe rate to determine effectiveness.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the interruptor reduced unsubscribe rate by 14.2%, statistically valid.
This proved to reduce unsubscribe rate, which, for CaringBridge, meant retaining hundreds of emails. This experiment also prompted other questions like "did this allow people to opt out who might have given?"
Future appeals will test this concept with a split audience throughout the entire campaign to see if overall fundraising results are affected.

experiments.title: How including a story of impact in a calendar year-end campaign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a story of impact for prospective donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/11/2022
CaringBridge had used lots of stories in fundraising appeals before. But one of their challenges is that the value proposition of CaringBridge is largely built through personal experience—your ability to interact with a friend or loved one—and, thus, third-party stories sometimes fell flat. However, they wanted to do more testing to understand how usage of a story could potentially impact the results of an appeal.
They saw an opportunity in their holiday appeal to test the concept by inserting a story into the middle of an otherwise straightforward match appeal. They positioned the story so that it came as a “personally impactful story”, not just a random case study on the impact of CaringBridge.
They split the email file so only half saw the story of impact and tested the concept.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 42% increase in conversion rate, just over the 95% threshold for a valid level of confidence.
CaringBridge believes the success of this experiment is largely due to the way in which the story was positioned. While this is a great lift, it needs to be tested further to determine if there are diminishing returns.

experiments.title: How breaking your year-end fundraising goal down into a smaller ask impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that breaking your large year-end fundraising campaign goal down into a smaller, relevant ask for the reader based upon their giving history will increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2021 - 12/27/2021
During the Leadership Institute’s “2021 Year-End” campaign, we wanted to experiment with asking donors to be one of a few donors to give a specific gift amount to see if it would increase the donor’s likelihood to give, or their generosity altogether.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed a +252.7% increase in revenue for the treatment. Additionally, we observed a 24.5% increase in donor conversion rate, but that did not validate—only achieving a 64.8% level of confidence.
The results included 4 typical "outlier" gifts that would usually be removed from other study types, but we decided to leave them in because this treatment is specifically designed to drive an increase in revenue per donor (or average gift amount).
Of these 4 outlier gifts, here's what we observed:
One donor gave a large gift, but it was less than their highest previous contribution (HPC).One donor tied his highest previous contribution (HPC).Two of the donors gave their new highest gift to the organization through this campaign (one of these donors increased from $500 to $5,000).
It would appear that asking a donor to be one of the X donors to make a target gift amount (that is at or slightly higher than their highest previous gift amount) drives donors to be more generous.
The real question is: Why?
Our hypothesis is that setting this "micro goal" for the reader (e.g. "10 donors to give $25 or more") is far more motivating for donors than communicating the overall fundraising goal ("our goal is to raise $500,000 this month").
When someone sees a request to be "one of the X donors" that gives a gift of "$Y or more" — and that $Y amount is around what I've given to the organization before — then I'm more likely to say "YES" to this request.
Alternatively, when the organization communicates their large campaign goal (in this example: $500,000) and a request to make a "special gift of any amount"—it makes me feel like my much smaller gift is not significant enough to make a difference, and therefore it's up to the major (or mega) donors to make the donations necessary to accomplish the campaign goal.
Our recommendation is to use this approach during large fundraising campaigns moving forward.

experiments.title: How a shorter message impacted donor conversion for texting audiences
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the donation page copy for text-based prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2021 - 12/25/2021
For a text-based prospecting campaign, the American Cornerstone Institute was doing donor acquisition around their children’s learning program, The Little Patriots. Since the visitors were coming from a text message, we wanted to understand how important the length of the message was in the conversion process. We often find more comprehensive value proposition statements for prospective donor audiences do better but text was a new enough channel that we wanted to validate that concept here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a full two weeks, we found that the shorter version had a 32% increase in donor conversion. It is our belief that reducing the friction associated with reading the full donation page for a visitor that is coming specifically from a prospecting text message was the key. This is an audience that is looking for information quickly and were reacting to a messaging asking them to donate.
It is important to note that the shorter version was arguably able to keep the same value proposition strength as the control version. When shortening copy, we will often remove key information that donors need when deciding to give.
The next step would be to test this same treatment with an email audience to see if it is truly specific to the text channel or if the shorter version works regardless of acquisition source.

experiments.title: How a message from the founder influenced donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing the message as a direct note from the founder for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2021 - 12/31/2021
For their education survey, the American Cornerstone Institute wanted to determine the impact using Dr. Carson’s voice would have on donor conversion. The existing donation page was already getting a 6%+ instant donor conversion rate but the belief that framing the ask as a direct message from the founder, would be more meaningful to the people who knew of him. We set up a new treatment to validate the belief.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for two weeks, we found that the message directly from Dr. Carson, resulted in a 28% increase in donor conversion. It validated our hypothesis that people are more likely to take action if they feel the CTA is coming from the person they are most familiar with instead of the relatively new organization.
There are some additional elements worth noting:
The treatment moved the image of Dr. Carson up to the top. This may have inadvertently influenced the donor conversion rate. This was done because the original image didn't work in the new treatment and we felt that removing a Dr. Carson image entirely would be an even more significant change. We'll want to validate the placement of his image with future experiments.Mobile visitors had a 105.1% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence. The page formatting was similar between the two and they were similar in length. Our best hypothesis is that this may be influenced by the placement of the Dr. Carson image. Again, something to be tested in the future.

experiments.title: How layering a donation ask impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that by asking the user to make one decision at a time and layering the two donation forms, we could reduce mental friction and decision friction on the page and increase conversion rate and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2014 - 10/10/2014
The Heritage Foundation and its sister organization Heritage Action For America wanted to run a joint campaign to raise money for both organizations and highlight the individual strengths of each organization. This campaign concept mirrored a direct mail concept that included two separate reply envelopes, one for each organization.
Since the organizations are independent from one another, they needed to have two donation forms. Initially, the campaign put both of these donation forms on the same page, right next to each other. We developed a treatment that layered these two asks, one after the other, with the ask for Heritage Action first.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Layering the two asks meant accepting a trade-off: the site visitor would not be allowed to choose to give to either organization. So, it was not a surprise when overall conversion dipped 12.5%. However, the layered asks resulted in more people choosing to give to both organizations, which had a significant impact on average gift. Overall, layering the two asks resulted in a revenue increase of 98.8% -- nearly doubling the previous revenue per visitor.
This provided a great learning for future campaigns -- and was immediately put into use on the main Heritage donation form. After a donor makes a gift on that form, they are immediately offered the opportunity to give to Heritage Action as well. This has resulted in an additional, consistent revenue stream for Heritage Action.

experiments.title: How reducing friction and bringing the problem to the headline affect conversion rate on desktop devices
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that bringing the problem into the headline and streamlining the donation experience  for child page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2021 - 10/14/2021
PFI had been running ads promoting child sponsorship for many months, and had seen a predictably low conversion rate, as a monthly ask of $39 is a high bar for many donors.
Wanting to improve conversion on their sponsorship pages, they tested two changes at once. First they looked at the headline, which appeared somewhat vague. They wondered if presenting the problem in the headline—that the child was in prison—would increase motivation to step in and help through sponsorship.
Secondly, when the sponsor was ready to commit, they had two options: they could either scroll all the way down the page past a large amount of copy and a “view more children” button, or hit a sponsor now button that, on desktop, was all the way to the right of the page and would scroll them to the bottom. Once arriving at the bottom of the page, there was a lack of orientation as to what to do.
They decided to test bringing the donation form all the way up to the top of the page, under the copy, so that it was right in front of the user as they made the decision to give. They also linked the copy to the form through a call-to-action headline.
They made both of these changes at once and launched an experiment to discover the impact on conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
At first the results of the test looked flat, or trending slightly downward...
However, splitting the traffic into desktop and mobile revealed something fascinating. The treatment produced a 199% valid lift in conversion for desktop devices, which make up 39% of conversions....which is being offset by a 51% near-valid decrease in conversion for mobile devices, which make up 61% of conversions.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted year end giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest amount during a year end fundraising campaign will increase overall revenue and response.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2021 - 12/07/2021
During the beginning of their year end campaign, KCBI decided to see if they could statistically validate a concept they had stumbled upon during a previous high urgency campaign. The idea was that if we asked existing donors for less than their previous highest gift amount and gave them a reason for doing so, we could inspire more people to give overall.
Originally, our belief was that it may lower average gift but would increase response rate enough to improve overall revenue. However, we found that it was able to increase both average gift and response rate for the segment but did not reach the 95% level of statistical validity we look for when determining a winner. With this in mind, we tested the same concept again at the start of their year end campaign.
For this test, we divided the file between control and treatment. For the control, we used email personalization to ask them for a specific amount (based upon a previous highest gift amount) and identified that we’re looking for a specific number of donors. For example, if a donor had given $20 in the past, we would ask: “Would you consider being one of 50 donors to make a gift of $25 right now.”
For the treatment, we used the same logic but asked them for a level down compared to the control. For example, if a donor had given $20, we would ask them to be one of 50 donors to make a $10 gift right now.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
After sending three separate emails to the distinct segments using similar language in each language and maintaining the same control / treatment split through the entire series, we discovered that the treatment group saw a 193.9% increase to overall revenue. This came from increasing conversion rate by 49% and average gift by 97%.
When digging into the results, we found that the biggest revenue increase came from donors giving between $250 and $500. The treatment group had eight donors giving in that range while the control had none.
It should also be noted that we removed a $2,600 gift from the results since it was such a large outlier when compared to the other gifts. When it is included, we observed a 39% increase with the treatment with a 73% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest contribution for existing donors will achieve increase overall response and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2021 - 12/02/2021
As part of their Giving Tuesday campaign, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test a relatively new concept known as the downgraded ask. For this experiment, they created a chart of standards based upon the donors’ previous highest contribution where they would ask for a certain number of donors to give a specific amount. For the treatment version of this ask, they asked for a level below what the donor had previously given and added language acknowledging that they were being asked for less.
This was then tested across both emails that were sent out on Giving Tuesday and results were determined based upon all online giving (excluding recurring gifts) during Giving Tuesday and the day following.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The "downgrade" treatment resulted in a 34% increase in overall revenue with a 95% level of confidence. However, it did not reach the required sample size to statistically validate this learning so it should be viewed as a directional lift.
When digging into the data, we analyzed a few distinct segments to better understand how the decreased giving language impacted donors based upon how much they have given in the past. Here is what we observed:
Highest gift between $1 - $99: We saw a 10% increase in conversion rate but an 8% decrease in average gift. The net result was a 1% increase in overall revenue which was not statistically significant.Highest gift between $100 - $999: The treatment segment saw an increase in both conversion rate (10%) and average gift (52%) which resulted in a 67.3% statistically significant increase in revenue (both LoC and Sample Size validated). This means we that, despite asking for less than they have given in the past, the segment actually gave more!Highest gift greater than $1K: This segment did not have enough gifts to be able to observe a trend.
This means that for the lower-dollar donors, the tactic was able to increase overall response rate but at the cost of average gift. In the future, for this segment, we would want to consider this tactic only for those we're trying to reactivate (like lapsed donors) rather than the whole file.
For the $100-$999 donors, this is absolutely a tactic that should be repeated in future campaign. We would need additional testing to validate the $1K+ segment in the future.

experiments.title: How removing distractions during the donation process impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that locking the donation form in place for mobile visitors will achieve improve overall conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/24/2021 - 12/03/2021
During Giving Tuesday, ACI was trying to find tactical ways to increase donor conversion given the highly motivated audience. One hypothesis was that we can remove potential distractions by “locking” the donation form in place once the visitor gets to that point in the process. By removing all other distractions, we hoped that it would improve conversion for mobile visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite running for a relatively short period of time with a smaller audience size, we found that the locked donation form decreased conversion by 34%. One potential reason for this is that the locked form removed all of the associated value proposition that is normally available to the visitors once they hit the decision point.
Ideally, we would have had more visitors before shutting off the experiment but, since it was on Giving Tuesday and the potential for loss was too great, we decided to end it a bit prematurely. However, given the direction the experiment was going, it is not worth re-testing this at a later date.

experiments.title: How an open field on a donation form affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does an open field on a donation form affect donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2017 - 06/06/2017
CaringBridge had executed several tests on their main donation page and found that the motivation to give on that page was very different than on their “tribute” pages, which was where most of their revenue came in. Their main donation page had a high average gift, and the motivation to give had proven to be much less personal and much more mission-based.
Since average gifts were high on this page, they wondered if the six-button gift array (which had beaten a three-button array in a previous test) were actually hindering average gift and conversion. The suggested gift amounts were meant to provide relief to decision friction—but what if they added to it?
CaringBridge decided to test this array against an “open field”, which allowed the donor to decide on their own gift amount, with a stated minimum donation of $10. They split the traffic to determine which had a greater effect on donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 125.9% lift in conversion on the main donation page. This showed that the array, which was designed to combat decision friction, might have actually contributed to it. One danger in the open field treatment was that average gift would dip without the suggested amounts to provide “anchors”. CaringBridge kept a close eye on average gift, but found no valid difference in the two forms, which meant that there was a valid revenue increase from this test.
This prompted a series of tests to separate desktop traffic from mobile and determine the individual impact of each experience on conversion.

experiments.title: How justifying why you sent the email right away affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that justifying why we’re sending the email sooner in the copy for our email housefile will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2020 - 12/14/2020
We had previously run an a/b test where we saw that telling a longer personal story was more effective at generating clicks than a brief personal story. But I wondered if actually getting to the point of why we’re emailing right off the bat might be even more effective – while still utilizing a personal tone and format.
Version A led with a personal note about the holiday season and how our family was getting in the Christmas spirit. Version B jumped right to the primary reason I was emailing – to give them a free ebook to increase their year-end revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Getting straight to the point and justifying why we are emailing right away led to a 36% increase in people downloading the ebook. In fact, it also led to a valid increase in every key metric: opens, clicks, and conversions (see below).
While we have clearly seen the power of being human and personal in email marketing, this experiment shows us that our subscribers are looking at their inbox and engaging with emails that they consider to be important, helpful, and valuable. As a result, our email envelope and copy need to clearly convey the value of the content we're sharing.
7.1% increase in email opens (99% LOC)32.4% increase in clicks (99% LOC)

experiments.title: How removing the child biography step from the birthday search process affects child sponsorship conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that taking users straight to the cart page for people who click the main CTA will achieve an increase in completed sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/17/2021 - 08/23/2021
The Compassion team had run an experiment that showed that reminding a user who comes through their birthday search funnel of the shared birthday could get more people to click the main call-to-action on that page, which said “sponsor me”. However, these additional CTA clicks did not translate into more sponsorships, because the funnel conversion metrics seemed to slow down significantly after that click. One hypothesis as to why was that after clicking “sponsor me”, the user was taken to the child’s biography page, rather than to the checkout page. The team hypothesized that there was a lack of agreement between the CTA and the expected next action.
To test this, the team set both the control and the treatment to have the “reminder” copy on the search results page. Then, half the traffic went straight to the cart when they clicked the button, and half the traffic went to the child biography page instead.
They launched the test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which went directly to the cart (or beginning of the checkout process), reduced conversion rate to sponsorship by 46.6%. This suggests that people who click the main CTA might not be

However, a review of the creative shows that the initial callout and copy on the search results page was not instituted in the experiment, which means that it should be run again with that integrated.

experiments.title: How strengthening contextual orientation and reinforcement affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening contextual orientation and adding reinforcement language for birthday search prospects will achieve an increase in completed sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2021 - 08/04/2021
Compassion had been running “birthday search” as an offer leading to sponsorship for more than a year, allowing potential sponsors to search for a child who shares their birthday. To find a child, the user put their birthday into a search widget and then arrived on a “search results” page. Originally, this page had very little context, making it difficult to understand if the child you were looking at shared your birthday. However, this was optimized by pulling one child to the top and showing that the child’s birthday in the headline.
The team wanted to continue to optimize this funnel. When looking at it from the donor’s perspective, they realized that just showing the date might not be enough—they wanted to reinforce that it was the same date the user had put into the widget on the previous page. They added a small, multicolored banner that said “we share the same birthday!” This would serve two purposes: first, it would contextually orient the user to the page, letting them know that they are on the logical next step after the birthday search widget. Second, it would provide reinforcement that this was the right child for them, as it said in words what the date callout also said.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page produced a 25.8% increase in clickthrough rate on the "sponsor me" CTA. Most of the traffic was mobile, which had a 24% increase in clickthrough rate, but desktop had a much larger impact, with a 103% increase in clickthrough rate on the main CTA.
This showed that contextual orientation and reinforcement has a big impact on getting prospective donors to take the next step in the funnel. However—looking down the funnel, there was actually a slight decrease in adds to cart and absolutely no difference in completed sponsorships.
This brings up two potential hypotheses: first, that when the user clicks "sponsor me", they are expecting to go to the cart page, instead of the child's biography page. This should be tested next—sending the "sponsor me" straight to the populated cart.
The other hypothesis, should this test not validate, is that the contextual orientation is lost once the user arrives on the child biography page, and that reinforcing the shared birthday on that page would lead to an increase in adds to cart.

experiments.title: How a dramatically simplified petition experience impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that dramatically simplifying the petition signup experience for prospective donors will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/09/2021 - 07/29/2021
Americans for Prosperity was promoting a new petition to acquire new emails and donors. As a part of recent testing, we noted that much of their traffic was coming from a simplified ad experience (using the “Yes/No” questions at the ad level) to tap into people’s core beliefs on a particular subject.
Subsequent testing proved that we were able to put a sticky bar on the petition page to “affirm” the visitor’s decision to click the ad, and push them through the petition signature experience.
However, this variant wasn’t lifting the email signup rate (but rather the donor conversion rate). Therefore, we wanted to test a radically shortened/simplified petition experience by doubling down on other belief statements they may not yet have seen.
Would this simplified experience increase email signups, donor conversion rates or the revenue that was being secured by the paid promotional campaign audiences?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The simplified experience drove an increase in email signup rates by +10.4%, with a 99.7% level of confidence.
However, we also observed a decrease in donor conversion rate of -24.9% (LoC: 72.7%) and subsequently revenue by -53.9% (LoC: 88%).
Our hypothesis is that the post-petition donation form is so incongruent with the new simplified experience, that it created a moment of anxiety (psychological resistance to give a gift) because it felt so disconnected.
Our next experience should be to reconfigure the donation page behind the simplified petition experience and launch a control experience vs. a congruent donation page behind the simplified petition signature page.
This will allow us to isolate this hypothesis and determine whether this is indeed driving decreases, or if there is another reason why we're seeing donor conversion rates drop after we have secured a higher volume of emails through this new approach.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Paid Social visitors had an 11.1% increase in email acquisition with a 99% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 15.4% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 16.4% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.New  visitors had an 11.5% increase in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 13.9% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 15.9% increase in email acquisition with a 96% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 44.1% increase in email acquisition with a 94% level of confidence.18-24 visitors had a 52.7% increase in email acquisition with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing the ways to give and additional donation information section impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the additional ways to giving and donation information from the bottom of the page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2021 - 07/11/2021
As Focus on the Family continues to optimize their primary donation page since moving to a new platform six months ago, they are testing into every element on the page. This even includes elements on the page that are below the actual giving form. When looking at the donation page, they wondered if the section under the giving form that communicated all of the different ways a person could give and had additional legal information (not necessary to include on the page in full text)  about a person’s donation was impacting donor conversion. They tested this by simply removing both of those sections on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Removing the additional ways to give and the donation disclosure information, donor conversion decreased by 26%. This was surprising to us because we hypothesized it was causing friction to the giving process, when in fact it was helping to increase motivation and added a sense of security in a person's decision to make a gift.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 33.9% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 6.3% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Female visitors had an 81.0% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
