We decided to launch an experiment with a new headline that emphasized the value the reader receives instead the work they have to do.
experiments.keyLearnings: The headline that emphasized the benefit to the reader resulted in a 15.3% increase to the email acquisition rate. This is a great reminder that the visitors we send to this page are not necessarily sold on the offer and that we need to frame every micro-decision in the terms of the value to the potential subscriber.

experiments.title: How a broader targeting strategy with a lifetime budget affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will broader audience targeting with a lifetime budget acquire more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/10/2017
When launching Facebook ads for our new online course, we had been encouraged by a partner to target a broader audience and allow Facebook to do to the work of optimizing the audience based on conversions.
We set up our targeting in the traditional way, but we also set up a broader ad set targeting people in the United States within our target age range. The budget was set for a lifetime spend over the course of a month.
We wanted to see if this targeting recommendation would outperform our traditional targeting both in conversions and in cost per lead.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, this targeting methodology did not work for this offer. We saw a 75% decrease in registrations, and a 208% increase in cost-per-lead after running it for 8 days.
It’s possible that spending more up front could lead to enough conversions for Facebook to learn which users are most likely to convert – causing the cost-per-lead to level off over time. But even in this is the case, the cost-per-lead would have to come down enough to off-set the initial investment.

When launching the promotion for a new NextAfter webinar, we wanted to ensure that we were choosing an effective title.
We had observed that many nonprofits are focused on acquisition during the first quarter or the year. This led us to hypothesize a title focused on “online donor acquisition” could be an effective way of getting the attention of nonprofit fundraisers.
But we also knew that many people we talk to specifically mention that they don’t know how to get value out of Facebook. Many have given up on it as a viable channel for fundraising. That led us to think that focusing our title around “Facebook fundraising” could also be effective.
The only way to know which would be most effective was to test.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that the treatment title “Cracking the Code of Facebook Fundraising” resulted in 197% more webinar registrations. By testing this through our Facebook advertising channels before rolling it out to our email file, we were able to capitalize on this learning in order to drive higher conversion throughout the promotional cycle.

experiments.title: How clarity in advertising copy affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer ask to “learn how” deliver more conversions than a direct ask to “get the eBook”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2016 - 08/12/2016
The Stanford Graduate School of Business ran Twitter ads to offer their new eBook Driving Innovation and Ideas to the market. However, they were not sure about how motivated their audience was to download an eBook. They ran a test to determine if a softer ask could produce a higher clickthrough rate. The “Learn how” ad in this test produced a 43.2% increase in clickthroughs, which proved the hypothesis correct. However, they knew that all clicks are not created equal, and wanted to make sure that the clicks from the treatment ad were also producing a higher number of conversion.
They ran a second set of data to determine if the treatment ad also produced an increase in conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the treatment ad produced a 43.2% lift in clickthrough rate, the “get the eBook” ad produced nearly 50% more conversions. This is a great reminder that “all clicks are not created equal”. Sometimes, traffic can be increased by lowering the clarity of the offer, which can produce less motivated traffic that is less likely to accept the offer. In this case, an increase in clickthrough rate does not outweigh the increase in conversion, making the direct ask the winner.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity in advertising copy affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does increasing clarity in advertising copy affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2016 - 05/19/2016
The Stanford Graduate School of Business was promoting their free eBook, Driving Innovation. They knew that the ad image on a Facebook ad was the most visible and impactful part of the ad. They wanted to test the impact of copy within the image, and how much specificity within that copy would increase conversion.
The control version of the ad used copy that highlighted the “desired effect” of the eBook: “Learn the secrets to driving innovation at work”. Since the rest of the copy reinforced this “desired effect”, they developed a treatment that was much more specific about the offer: “Get the new eBook from Stanford Business”.
They launched A/B ad test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with added specificity, resulted in a 52% increase in conversion. This shows us that while desired effect psychology might increase conversion on the landing page, at this stage of the funnel, we must be more specific about how the user is going to achieve that desired effect. In this case, they will achieve that by downloading an eBook.
Increasing clarity around the offer increased conversion, even when that “micro-yes” was early in the conversion funnel.

experiments.title: How “desired effect” psychology affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which psychological approach will convert the most visitors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2015 - 01/25/2016
The Stanford Graduate School of Business launched a new ebook centered around developing a culture of innovation. They wanted to test different psychological approaches with the headline to see if one delivered a measurable lift over the others. They drafted four headlines:
1. “Driving Ideas and Innovation”: this simple headline tested the power of congruency, as it mirrored the title of the ebook.
2. “Get 10 must read articles from Stanford Business on driving innovation”: this headline was the only one that included the brand name, and told the reader what they would receive.
3. “Generate breakthrough ideas and create a culture of innovation”: this headline tested the power of the “desired effect” — not telling the reader what they would get immediately, but the result of the learnings
4. “Find out how you can develop a culture that drives growth and encourages innovation”: this headline teased the visitor with what they will learn, and assumed they were at the very top of the funnel in their decision-making process.
They launched a test that evenly distributed the four headlines between inbound traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test proved that the “desired effect” headline produced an 8.2% lift over the control and the other variants, which tells us that when we tell people the end result of consuming the content, they are most incentivized to accept the offer.

experiments.title: How the right offer affects emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer in the footer of the NextAfter website will lead to the greatest number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:

When we first launched the new NextAfter.com website, we had some internal debate about the best offer to use in the footer of the site. Historically, we had offered a subscription to our weekly newsletter, This vs. That, which allowed subscribers to receive one experiment per week from the Research Library.
Since this was our main website, we hypothesized that maybe the more general offer of “Staying Informed” would work better for the visitors. We decided to set up a test that would allow us to evaluate these two different offers.
experiments.keyLearnings: We discovered that the “Stay Informed” offer led to an infinitely better number of emails captured (none versus some is by definition infinitely better). While the This vs. That newsletter is still one of our more popular offers, a more general contact form was a better fit given the motivation of the people visiting the website.

experiments.title: Which email sign up headline produces the highest conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: What email value proposition will produce the most email sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2015 - 08/28/2015
Every once in a while we get around to running tests on our own website to make sure NextAfter is practicing what we preach. So we set up two different email signup pages encouraging visitors to get the This vs. That weekly email which showcases one of our latest test results. The goal was to see which email sign up value proposition would drive the most conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test is another example of how clarity trumps persuasion. Clearly stating what a person is signing up for, getting a test each week, is a better description than talking about how a person will benefit. The surprising aspect to us was that even with the low traffic to the page the test validated.
The copy used to promote offers is the most important element in getting someone to say yes. Just throwing something up on the page and not testing it would have seriously affected the number of email sign-ups the page generated.

experiments.title: How finding the right incentive affects the email capture rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which incentive is the most effective in capturing an email address?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2014 - 07/04/2014
The Texas State Historical Association (TSHA) has a large volume of traffic to their websites each month. In order to capitalize on this traffic, they have set up several email capture offers to use at different times of the year.  One of those offers was a 10 question quiz named Are You Smarter Than a Texas 7th Grader. At the end of the quiz, the visitors are offered a digital resource in exchange for their email address.
We wanted to find the digital resource that would be most appealing to the TSHA audience so we created an experiment that would allow us to test out several offers.  We offered visitors either a section of the TSHA’s Texas Almanac, a free eBook entitled the Battle of the Alamo, or a collection of popular articles from their publication Southwestern Historical Quarterly.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Battle of the Alamo eBook proved to be the most popular offer when it lifted email capture rate by 50%. This same fact held true a year later when we tested this same eBook against the full eBook of the Texas Almanac. However, a year later the impact was not as significant when the full Texas Almanac was offered.

experiments.title: How testing into a new offer affects name acquisition

rows.name: The Battle of the Alamo eBook

experiments.researchQuestion: Which name acquisition offer produces the most new email addresses for the file?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2015 - 06/05/2015
To take advantage of their high website traffic, the Texas State Historical Association has been offering a free eBook version of their popular 2014-2015 Texas Almanac in exchange for visitor email addresses. This name acquisition offer has been running for the past 6 months and continues to be a high performer. However, with the release of a recent History Channel show, the motivation of website visitors had shifted and we wanted to see if a different offer could further improve the acquisition efforts.
As a result, the TSHA ran a three way split on the traffic offering three different eBook options, the Texas Almanac, The Battle of the Alamo, and Remember Goliad! We wanted to see which of these offers could collect the most new email addresses for the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Alamo eBook improved email acquisition by 23.6% while maintaining the immediate donor conversion on the follow up screen. This increased volume of email addresses will allow for additional conversion opportunities down the line.
As a result, the TSHA will be changing offers to just focus on the Alamo eBook for the next several weeks. Once the show is over, we will want to re-validate these results to ensure the motivation (and corresponding conversion) does not change.

experiments.title: How an alternate offer, given at the right time, affected emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an alternate offer, shown when the visitors are leaving the name acquisition page, increase the number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2015 - 10/19/2015
The Texas State Historical Association had been running an email acquisition campaign around their latest eBook, The Civil War in the Lone Star State. Visitors would be given the eBook in exchange for their email address. This tactic had proven successful with previous eBooks and had historically received a 7% to 10% email acquisition rate. While this is a decent conversion rate, we wanted to find a way to improve this.
We had the hypothesis that we may be able to acquire additional email addresses if we could give the visitors an alternative offer. We didn’t want to compete with the primary offer of the eBook so we decided to only show the alternative offer if the visitors showed signs that they were leaving the page.  We created a treatment that would show a popup offering a free subscription to their Texas Day-by-Day emails when visitors displayed an “exit intent” by moving their mouse toward the back button.
experiments.keyLearnings: This alternative offer, shown when a visitor was leaving the page, ended up increasing the number of emails acquired by 36.9%.  What is interesting about these results is that this lift was not achieved just by the offer on the popup. It would seem that, when the popup was shown to people, many visitors would go to close the window and then ended up downloading the ebook after all.  This popup served to arrest the attention of the visitor right before they committed to the decision to leave the page.

experiments.title: How creating a linear path down the page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page format will acquire the greatest number of email addresses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2015 - 11/02/2015
It is said that the key to a successful landing page is a good design. However, the definition of “good” is where designers will often disagree with marketers (and fundraisers). We would posit that the definition of a “good design” one that is able to best convert, not the page the looks the best.
We were able to put this theory to a test with a recent landing experiment launched by the Texas State Historical Association. At a recent MECLABS optimization conference, Phillip Farr, a designer and developer for the TSHA, was in the process of creating a landing page for an upcoming eBook to be promoted on the TSHA website. This conference focused on the concept of optimizing landing pages by increasing value to the visitor while reducing the associated cost.
After one of these sessions, Phillip took another pass at the design he had previously created. Using the principles promoted at the conference, he re-designed the page using similar style as the original page but in a format he felt would better communicate value and that would reduce friction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new landing page treatment resulted in a 7.7% increase in the number of email addresses acquired. This new treatment achieved this lift by making several modifications to the original design:
The headline was moved out of the background and placed inline with the rest of the page copy. This made it easier to catch the visitors’ attention as many people have “banner blindness” when they first visit the page.The name acquisition form was moved below the text so it be within the visitors’ eye path as they moved down the page.He removed some of the opacity around the form to make the section easier to readHe changed the sub-headline to communicate the reason for the form.

experiments.title: How an animated banner affected email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which banner will lead to the greatest amount of clicks and emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2015 - 11/02/2015
The Texas State Historical Association gets a significant amount of organic traffic to their website each month. Our primary means to monetize this traffic is through email acquisition campaigns that we then use to convert subscribers into members.  The difficult part of the process is capturing the attention of the visitors once they get to the site.
We have historically used banner ads placed in a strategic locations throughout the website. These have always been a static image that promotes whatever the current free offer is. We had the hypothesis that an animated banner would be able to better capture the visitors’ attention which would subsequently lead to an increase in the number of emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The animated banner was able to increase the number of emails acquired by 18.6%. This tactic was able to increase the click through rate of the banners enough that we could statistically lift the number of people that converted.
In the future, we will want to monitor the impact animated banners to ensure they maintain their increased performance.  Often times these gimmicky tactics tend to slowly lose their effectiveness as time progresses.

Our control landing page took the approach of communicating the “why” behind the book to explain why the book was so important and why the story needed to be told.  This was a concept we had tested into previously.
We had a hypothesis that visitors would be more likely to download the book if they could see the contents and understand the source of the information. As a result, we created a treatment page with a new design and different copy from the control.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment landing page resulted in a 23.8% decrease in the number of email addresses acquired. The goal of the treatment was to add value to the eBook by citing the source information and giving visitors a look into the premium. However, based upon these results, it would appear the animation and copy may have been more of a distraction than a value add.

The campaign drove a huge amount of traffic to the download page, which contained a lot of information about the almanac and a name acquisition form to receive a free digital copy. We thought that if the traffic coming to the page had a high motivation to get the almanac, then we might be actually adding friction with so much descriptive copy. We designed a treatment that was much simpler, to quickly give them exactly what they are coming to get.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page resulted in a 20.5% decrease in name conversion, showing that visitors didn’t have the high motivation that we thought — they still needed to be sold. Less information and a smoother path to conversion is not always better, especially when it reduces the amount of value placed on the premium.

They wanted to test the impact of urgency — even though no end date was clearly defined. Two ads were created: one positioned itself to appeal to Texas history lovers, and the other used “while it lasts” language.
experiments.keyLearnings: The power of perceived urgency is impactful — not only did it attract many more clicks, the visitors who clicked through were much more likely to convert. This suggests that future campaigns might deliver better results with some sort of suggested deadline.

experiments.title: How selling the final call-to-action too early affects names acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Does promoting member benefits on the initial email acquisition page increase the number of names and donors acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2015 - 10/01/2015
For the Texas State Historical Association, the primary source of new members is through a subsequent membership offer once a visitor downloads one of their free eBooks. This two step process consists of two landing pages; the first uses language that sells the value of the eBook with the subsequent page promoting the benefits of membership.
We had a hypothesis that the visitors to the eBook landing page might be motivated enough that they didn’t need as much language to “sell” of the eBook (it is given free in exchange for their email address). Instead, we could use the headline and subsequent paragraph to begin talking about the benefits of membership which would then make the subsequent offer on the second page more attractive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By promoting a TSHA membership before first “selling” the initial name acquisition offer (the eBook), we ended up decreasing the number of email addresses acquired by 15.8%. Additionally, it led to a drop in the number of members acquired by 37% since we were driving less traffic to the membership page and they were not as interested to learn more about the associated benefits.
After further analysis, we realized that we skipped several steps in the donor funnel by promoting the membership before the initial offer. The visitors need to be taken through a mental conversation that ultimately leads to member conversion but each step along the way is important. We must first address their reason for visiting before we sell them on any other offer.

experiments.title: How reducing an ad’s clarity affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which website banner will result in the greatest email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/09/2015 - 11/29/2015
To attract their organic website visitors to their latest email acquisition offer, the Texas State Historical Association created a series of banners to be used throughout their website. The standard approach to these ads was a simple design with a clear call-to-action of getting the free eBook.
We had two hypotheses on ways we could improve these results. The first concept was to make the banner’s message intentionally vague. We felt that if we could drive more traffic, this would result in an increase in the overall number of emails required. Alternatively, we felt that a more designed banner would stand out better on the site which would, in turn, increase the appeal of the offer.  We decided to run a three-way split to find out the answer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both of the new treatments were not able to outperform the original concept employed by the control. The simple and direct banner ad was able to increase both the clarity and appeal of the offer more than the two alternatives.
As an interesting side note, the “Before They Were Stars” banner was actually able to drive 300% more traffic than both the control and the second treatment. However, the audience was not motivated to download the eBook like the other banners which resulted in the lowest overall email acquisition rate.

With previous eBook offers, we found that a simple banner that displayed the cover of the eBook and a short call to action were the best performing. With this offer, we had the opportunity to also incorporate external relevance by utilizing language that linked it to Black History Month. We created a new treatment that featured a well-known image and text that mentioned the Black History Month.

This banner add drove 18.5% more traffic which then converted at a higher rate due to the increased motivation. The text of the ad was able to maintain the clarity established with the control add and the relevance added additional value in the visitors’ minds.

experiments.title: How a 3rd party endorsement affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a 3rd party endorsement improve the email acquisition rate on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2016 - 02/15/2016
After the initial launch of the Texas State Historical Association’s eBook celebrating Black History Month, they received an endorsement for the work from a well known public figure, the mayor of Houston. The quote promoted both TSHA and the eBook itself. We had the hypothesis that if we added the quote to the page, it would decrease the anxiety which would, in turn, increase the conversion rate. We decided to test this out against the existing control page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The 3rd party quote ended up decreasing the email acquisition rate on the page by 28%. We have two hypotheses for the cause of this decrease:
The audience has come to expect that the products released by TSHA are of high quality. As a result, they didn’t need the added 3rd party credibility. Instead, this quote added length to the page which added friction to the conversion process.
It is possible that the endorser, the Houston mayor, is unpopular with the audience demographics. Regardless of their reasonings, if the visitors did not like the Houston mayor they would be less likely to convert.
In either situation, it made sense to remove the quote and continue with the original page.

experiments.title: How giving instant gratification affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which method would acquire more names?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2016 - 06/01/2016
Susan B. Anthony List developed an online quiz called the “Truth About Abortion”. This was a five question quiz that tested and educated people on facts about abortion. At the end of the quiz, a person would get their results. In efforts to optimize name acquisition, they tested this part of the funnel by creating two different ways to acquire a persons name:
If a person wanted to see how well they did on the quiz, they had to give their name and email and SBA List would email them their results
A person would receive their quiz results immediately after they took the quiz without having to give their name and email address to see it, but then be asked to add their name (give their name and email) to a pledge to “Vote Pro-life”.
experiments.keyLearnings: The pledge acquired 96.7% more names than the acquisition method to ask for a person’s name in order to see their quiz results. Asking for someone’s name in order to see their quiz results may have come across as gimmicky and caused anxiety. By giving them their quiz results without having them provide anything to the organization, a person received instant gratification and value and were then more motivated to engage with the organization.

experiments.title: How ad creative affects  conversion rate

Timeframe: 01/20/2016 - 02/03/2016
Susan B Anthony List was promoting a petition in support of the Hyde Amendment. This petition was created in response to an announcement Hillary Clinton made wanting to repeal the Amendment. They launched an ad campaign on Facebook to attract people to sign the petition. Because the petition was created in response to Hillary Clinton, they created an ad more politically focused. But, since the Hyde Amendment is about saving the lives of unborn babies, they also wanted to create an ad using imagery and language to support that message. They did an A/B test to see which would convert more petition signers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Hillary Clinton ad was able to increase the number of emails acquired by 129%. This was partially due to the 10% increase the ad was able to make to the click through rate. By driving more visitors and higher motivated visitors, we saw the significant lift in emails acquired.
SBA List is the political arm for the pro-life movement. While people are motivated to save a baby’s life, for this petition, people were more motivated by the political message of the ad. The political ad had a more direct connection with what the petition was about. The political nature of the ad and petition were timely too with the political climate of the country.

experiments.title: How personalized copy and tone in an email appeal affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a personal tone with personalized copy increase donation conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2016 - 03/14/2016
Jews for Jesus had a process in place to convert direct mail letters to eAppeals, which they sent on a monthly basis to their email file. While this raised money and kept digital donors engaged, they hypothesized that the tone of a direct mail piece wasn’t really aligning with the medium of email.
So while keeping the value proposition of the email intact, they completely rewrote it with a conversational tone. They also stripped all the elements of branding out of the email to make it seem like it came from a person, rather than email marketing software. Finally, they inserted personalization throughout the appeal.
Then, they split-tested this with their email file, dividing it in half to make sure they could validate a result.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new email produced a 304.8% increase in donation conversion rate. This provided insightful learnings about the Jews for Jesus digital donor:
They want to feel like they are being personally engaged.They want to feel like the appeal is only written to them.
This is difficult to do in direct mail, but the email provides many great ways to personalize communications. This points a way forward for all emails to be “high-touch”.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to the signup process affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will add clarity to the sign up process while increasing donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2015 - 09/09/2015
The Constitution 101 online course is one of the primary methods for email and donor acquisition for Hillsdale College.  This is a free course available in exchange for the visitors’ name and address.
After signing up, the registrants are led to a donation page in hopes of an immediate donor conversion. The trouble with this process is that some participants mistake the donation page as a payment page for the course which causes some frustration.
We wanted to find a way to clarify that the donation is not required without giving the visitors an immediate exit path. The current control had
reduced the number of complaints but had also reduced donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment resulted in a 22.6% lift to the overall donor conversion on the page.
Anecdotally, the number of subscriber complaints did not see any significant increase during the test period either. This leads to the conclusion that this new treatment provided the necessary clarity to the subscribers to ease any potential confusion that may have occurred while maintaining the high visitor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a two-step form reduced affects cognitive friction and conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a two-step form allow for the capture of more information compared to a single page form that acquired all of the information at one time?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2015 - 09/09/2015
For The Heritage Foundation’s Presidents’ Club Meeting registration, we wanted to find a way to increase the number of attendees to the event. Historically, the forms had all been a single page with all of the data capture fields required before someone could register. These fields included selecting each session they would like to attend and entering all of their information into the form.
We had the hypothesis that if we moved the process to a two-step process, it would create less friction in the visitor’s minds since the forms would be shorter. This two step process would also allow us to look up the donor’s information (based upon their email address or ID) and use this to pre-populate the second form.  We were able to not only fill in their address information but also pre-select the sessions that their membership level qualified for.  We wanted to see if this process would lead to an increased number of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The two step form led to a 99% increase in the number of donors completing their registration information. This increase is the result of more than just the two step form; the ability to pre-fill the donor's information into the second form would have significantly reduced the friction associated with the process. This reduction in friction made the form easier to understand end simpler to fill out.

experiments.title: How the delivery method used to present an email acquisition offer impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would an exit-intent popup modal increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2017 - 02/13/2017
Live Action News is the publishing arm of Live Action and gets high volumes of traffic. They had an offer running on their website asking people to sign a petition. This offer was presented to people using a slide down modal at the top of the website. While this method worked well, they hypothesized that a new delivery method that was more intrusive might be more effective in acquiring petition signers/emails. They decided to test an exit-intent popup that would appear when visitors moved their mouse outside of the browser window. We made sure to target this experiment at desktop users only as mobile users would not be able to experience the effect.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The exit-intent popup decreased email acquisition conversion rate by 76%. There are a couple of take aways we can pull from these results:
The primary reason is that the majority of Live Action News traffic comes from mobile devices. The exit-intent pop up doesn’t effectively work on mobile devices causing the significant decrease in conversion.The kind of offer we presented to people required them to do something and not “get” something.
We recommend testing this again using a different kind of offer where the end-user receives something of instant value and also making the test exclusive to desktop users only to see if our results would be different.

experiments.title: How presenting a donation ask when a reader is most engaged impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Could we increase donor acquisition by adding a giving opportunity within the article content on the site?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2017 - 05/19/2017
Live Action News is the publishing arm of Live Action. They received large volumes of traffic every month on this site People come to Live Action News to read and learn more about what is going on in the pro-life movement. Live Action News produces human interest stories, videos, and news on a daily basis. With the volume of traffic coming to their site, they wanted to find a way to convert these site visitors into donors. There is a donation button on the primary navigation of the site, but they wondered if they could present an offer that was more intrusive within the articles on the site. They developed a “Dear Reader” treatment that presented a donation offer within the content of each article.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By adding the “Dear Reader” giving opportunity within the content on the site, we were able to increase donor acquisition by 89%. Here is what we can learn from this:1. Even though there is always the ability to give a gift on the site with the primary navigation, visitors were not as likely to take action.
2. The Dear Reader treatment presented a donation ask at the most opportune time – when the end user is most engaged. By doing this we were able to take advantage of their high motivation and present a value proposition to give.

experiments.title: How the kind of offer can impact name conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which content offer would acquire more names?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/03/2017 - 10/10/2017
Live Action has a publishing arm and website called, Live Action News. On the site they publish the latest pro-life news articles and human impact stories. The site receives a high volume of traffic and they wanted to convert this significant traffic into new emails.  To do this they presented visitors who came to the site with content offers. One of the content offers was their weekly newsletter that features the top pro-life news stories of the week. They tested this offer against a pledge that people could sign in support of life being a human right. The offers were presented as a slide out feature on the website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The “Life is a Human Right” pledge increased name conversion by 616%! Even though the Live Action News newsletter was very relevant to this audience, the newsletter had a higher mental cost for the website visitor – not only were they having to give their name and email address for the content offer, but they did so knowing that they’d be receiving more communications from Live Action. The pledge simply asked them to give us their name and email address if their beliefs aligned with those of the pledge.
While relevancy is a key factor when presenting a content offer on a website, the mental cost associated with the offer is an even more important factor.

experiments.title: How the homepage offers impact email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer will produce the highest email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2016 - 11/11/2016
Live Action was in the process of redesigning their main website. During the process, it was suggested that they replace the existing email acquisition offer (a petition) with a more general newsletter sign up. The petition had been running for over a year so it made logical sense to update it.
Prior to launching the new site, we decided to test the new design and offer to ensure it would have the same impact as the long running petition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ideally, we would have been able to test the newsletter offer by itself and then also the newsletter with the petition. Since the homepage received a limited amount of traffic, we wanted to make sure we achieved a valid lift so we opted for just one treatment.
What we found was that in both the control and treatment, the petition offer received a 1.4% email acquisition rate. However, in the treatment, the newsletter sign up received an additional 0.5% conversion rate. These two offers combined to result in a 49% lift to the control.
This demonstrated two key takeaways:
The email newsletter offer is not as appealing as the petition offer. The petition received 180% more email signups. While this may have taken from the newsletter offer, it was definitely the more appealing choice for most visitors.
The diversity of offers increased the overall acquisition. The petition did not appeal to all audiences so having a “softer” choice allowed subscribers that did not want the petition to opt into communications from Live Action.

experiments.title: How reducing copy length in an email affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does reducing the length of email copy affect the force of the value proposition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2016 - 12/19/2016
Live Action sent many email appeals as part of their year-end fundraising campaign. One email in particular had a complex value proposition that took a significant amount of copy to fully explain. Since the holidays are a busy time for many people, they wondered if people would take the time to read the entire email. However, they knew that shortening the copy of the email risked removing crucial parts of the value proposition—and that would significantly affect donor conversion.
They created a treatment that removed sections of the copy, and re-edited the copy to make sure it presented a unified value proposition. However, this presented one more caveat: removing an early section of copy removed a link from the email, so the treatment had one less “clickable” opportunity.
They created two test segments and sent a test to each to determine which version attracted more clicks (the initial metric) and donors (the ultimate metric).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The email with shorter copy had a sharp increase in clickthrough rate. But not all clicks are created equal—they must be properly motivated to give (as that was the call-to-action). If more clickthroughs does not equal more gifts, then the call-to-action may be driving the wrong kind of traffic.
However, upon review, the shorter copy produced an 89.5% increase in conversion, which was astounding. The increase in clickthrough rate was only 24.7%, but somehow these donors were almost twice as motivated to give. One hypothesis for this is that removing copy from the email removed an early link and call-to-action, which may have kept these readers reading further so that they absorbed more of the value proposition.
This test is a fascinating learning that shows that when the value proposition is communicated clearly, you don’t need many words to inspire donors to give.

experiments.title: How personal, conversational email copy and tone impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a more conversational and personal email help increase the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2017 - 02/13/2017
Live Action has the largest online presence for the pro-life movement. They are experts in the pro-life space and present their content with professionalism. Lila Rose, their founder and president, is the voice for a lot of their communication, including their monthly email appeals. They hypothesized that because the email was coming from Lila, the tone of the emails wasn’t matching the personal communication medium that email is.
While keeping the value proposition from the control email intact, they completely rewrote a treatment email with a conversational tone and inserted personalization throughout the copy. They also stripped all the elements of branding and the picture of Lila out of the email to make it seem like it came from a person, rather than email marketing software.
They split their email file in half and tested.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email produced a 74.9% increase in donations. What this tells us is that:
More people will respond when they feel personally engaged.
People give to people. People want to feel that Lila is personally emailing them rather than an email marketing machine.
Donors want authentic and conversational communication in high-touch channels like email. Email provides an opportunity for Live Action to personally connect with their audience unlike any other communication channel.

experiments.title: How reducing friction on mobile devices affects name conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does reducing copy on mobile devices increase the likelihood of conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2014 - 07/23/2015
Human Liberty was promoting a petition directed at the International Justice system in support of North Koreans held in captivity by a totalitarian government. They noticed that much of their high-converting traffic was coming from mobile devices. Conversion was relatively low, even for the highly-targeted audiences they focused their advertising on. So, they proposed a test to discover exactly how highly motivated the visitors were. They created a new treatment with drastically shorter copy. If the audience was already highly motivated, the additional copy on the control might actually be slowing them down on a mobile device.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new treatment increased conversion by 76.2% — validating the hypothesis that mobile traffic was not only highly motivated and needed less “convincing”, but also responded well to shorter messages that took less time to read. This was highly valuable for all of Human Liberty’s mobile-focused campaigns.

experiments.title: How the order of application questions affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: By moving the project selection later in the process, we are allowing volunteers to become mentally invested in the application so that the selection process does not cause as many drop offs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2012 - 10/03/2012
Selecting which project to volunteer for is one of the most thought provoking decisions a volunteer makes when signing up the Global Volunteer Network.  The original sign up process had volunteers selecting this information at the beginning of their application.  It was out hypothesis that putting such an important question at the start caused a significant drop off in the sign up process. We wanted to run an experiment to determine what moving the question further down the process would do for the number of volunteers acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: Moving the project selection later in the application process resulted in an increase of 51.5% in volunteers acquired.  The volunteers had already filled out their basic biographical information so were already invested in their application.  When the project selection question did appear, they were already committed to the process so the challenging question did not result in as many dropoffs.

experiments.title: How removing a free giveaway increases name acquisition for gated content
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing a “free giveaway” increase form submissions for gated content?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2019 - 04/11/2019
Compassion International had a page for their Compassion Sunday campaign that let visitors invite their pastor to participate in the event by giving them a Pastor’s Guide PDF download. The page had a simple form that allowed visitors to enter their pastors name, which was valuable to Compassion for follow-up purposes. However, right next to this form was a simple link to the PDF guide itself. They hypothesized that removing this “free giveaway” of the gated content would result in more form captures, and thus more value to the organization. They set up a simple A/B test to validate a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Removing the free giveaway resulted in three times as many form completions. While there is some value in having the Pastor's Guide on display, Compassion isn't able to capture that value. However, with the Pastor's Guide removed, they are able to capture three times as much value from the page, which results in much more downstream value from Compassion Sunday events.

experiments.title: How the style of offer impact email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a statement of belief offer increase email acquisition compared to the generic email newsletter offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2018 - 03/13/2018
In a previous experiment for Alliance Defending Freedom, we had found that a Statement of Belief dramatically outperformed the standard email newsletter offer for visitors coming from paid social media ads. Based upon these results, we wanted to see if the same impact would be seen if we applied the Statement of Belief offer to organic visitors to the blog. The ADF blog is a significant source of visitors to the site and, over the years, has become a great source for email acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings: Through this experiment, we were able to increase email acquisition by 98% for both mobile and desktop visitors. This is a significant leap forward in email acquisition for the visitors being driven to the blog. It is also a great indication that we need to expand our thinking when it comes to the offers we’re presenting to visitors. We need to make sure they are unique and appealing to our ideal audience; we will also need to make sure these offers are constantly being tested to stay relevant with the audience.

experiments.title: The impact of reinforcing the security of the email address field
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding visual security indicators around the email address field increase overall email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2019 - 03/04/2019
Alliance Defending Freedom had been running a statement of belief acquisition offer on their blog for well over a year. In experiments with similar nonprofits, we had discovered that adding visual security indicators to the credit card section of a donation form increased donor conversion. Our hypothesis was that this was due to isolating and reducing the mental friction associate with the area of the form that created the most anxiety. With this observation, we theorized that adding the same treatment to the email field of an acquisition form would yield similar results.
experiments.keyLearnings: We observed a statistically significant decrease of 19.5% with the addition of security indicators to the email address field. When we dug deeper into the metrics we discovered that this was primarily isolated to the mobile traffic. These visitors saw a 29% decrease (99.9% level of statistical confidence) while the desktop users did not see any significant change in their conversion.

experiments.title: How the length of a story in an email affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will spending less time on the story and more time on the content affect webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2019 - 02/20/2019
In this experiment, our original email was trying to communicate the importance of a/b testing by telling a personal story. This is typical of many emails that we write – we often look for a personal story to make help the recipient see that we’re humans (not just marketers) and use that connection to lead into a content offer of some type.
But we wondered if it might be more effective to tell this same story in a much shorter format, and spend more time talking about the specifics of what someone will learn in the webinar, rather than solely relying on the landing page to cover those details.
experiments.keyLearnings: Our treatment saw a 48.4% increase in webinar registrations. This is a very interesting result as it can make a significant impact on how we structure of emails related to free content offers in the future. It's often hard for us to validate an experiment like this based on our scale, let alone see an increase this large. So a 98% level of confidence gives us great reassurance that this strategy will be on to continue test and iterate on moving forward.

experiments.title: How a more aggressive messaging angle impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the more aggressive messaging approach be more appealing to the ADF audience resulting in increased email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2018 - 09/07/2018
Jack Phillips, a client of Alliance Defending Freedom, had two significant events happen to him in a short window of time. After his Supreme Court victory, he was attacked again by the State of Colorado for a situation remarkably similar to the original case. As a result of this attack, Jack decided to take the fight back to the state and is suing them for the continued harassment of his religious freedom. With these two events happening around the same time, we wanted to find out which angle the ADF subscribers would find most appealing.
To do this, we created a two statements for visitors to add their name to. One took the standard approach of “Defending Religious Freedom” while the other was far more aggressive and focused on “Fighting Back.”
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for over two weeks, we found that the more aggressive "fighting back" language increased email acquisition by 15%. This unique situation allowed us to tap into a messaging approach that is not often applicable to the types of cases that ADF represents. In most cases, it is all about defending an injustice but, with this situation, we were able to take an approach that appealed to a greater number of visitors.

experiments.title: What impact does a matching gift have on donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including a matching gift have a positive impact on donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2018 - 10/11/2018
Alliance Defending Freedom had a unique opportunity during their most recent high urgency campaign. They had secured a matching gift and the campaign they were running was being executed solely through email. This meant they could run an experiment to validate the impact of a matching gift without the risk of cross contaminating the control segment. (This can often happen if there are banner ads in addition to the email program which may cause subscribers in the control group to see messaging intended only for the treatment.)
They decided to split their subscriber file (a random split for both donors and nondonors) and maintain this split for the initial messaging of the campaign. They then sent the control group campaign messaging but without the mention of a matching gift. For the treatment group, they used the same basic messaging but incorporated language about doubling their gift and a deadline to take advantage of the match. They wanted to understand the impact this tactic would have on overall conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The images above are the first email in the campaign; this same style of messaging was used in the first three emails of the campaign. After those three emails, we saw a 87.9% increase in donor conversion for the subscribers that were a part of the matching gift group. This is a great reminder of the perceived impact this kind of offer can have for potential donor conversion.

experiments.title: How the style of offer impacts email and donor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a survey produce any measurable impact on email or donor acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/13/2018 - 11/29/2018
Alliance Defending Freedom had been running a statement of belief on their blog as a means for email acquisition for well over two months. While it still resulted in a significantly high conversion rate, we wanted to determine if a new offer would allow us to potentially increase either email acquisition or, potentially, the downstream instant donor conversion.
We had created a “Religious Freedom Survey” to be used in some prospecting efforts and it had seen decent donor conversion. We decided to test out a version of that offer on the blog to see if that style of acquisition would produce any measurable impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for over two weeks, we did not see any significant change in downstream donor conversion but we did observe a 20% decrease in email acquisition. Ultimately, we would have been okay with a decrease in email acquisition if we were able to convert a higher number of those new subscribers into donors downstream. Since we did not see that happen, we decided to turn off this experiment and try a new statement of belief in the hopes of using a topic that aligns better with future email solicitations to see if that helps increase downstream conversion.

experiments.title: How we validated the impact a new offer would have on email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a new offer yield better results than the long-standing Jack Phillips Statement of Belief?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2018 - 01/11/2019
Alliance Defending Freedom had been running the current statement of belief on their blog pages as a method for email acquisition for well over three months. The statement related to one of their more recent cases, Jack Phillips, and focused on the need to fight back against government suppression of first amendment rights. Since it had been running for so long, we started to notice a decline in results as many of the visitors likely to convert had already done so.
With this finding, we decided to launch a new statement of belief. This time, we opted for a statement that would be more evergreen, one that didn’t rely on a specific case. We also wanted to try a more positive approach in the messaging to attract a different style of subscriber.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for nearly 3 weeks, we saw that the new statement of belief that focused on "What we are for" instead of "Fighting back" increased email acquisition by 25%. We also examined downstream donor conversion and noticed no significant difference between the two offers.
This finding will allow us to move forward with the new offer and continue to iterate our testing on the new control.

experiments.title: How a motivational postscript increased non-member donor conversion rates

Ended On: 02/12/2019
During The Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff Campaign in 2019, we wanted to experiment with a more motivating postscript at the end of an email that was sent to non-members. Most of the postscript messages within the campaign were transactional in nature (if you received this in error, we apologize). Would sending a core message in the postscript to motivate non-members to give work?

Email Clickthrough Rate: +19.1% (with a 94.9% level of confidence)
We recommend further experimentation and finding the right balance between too much and not enough, but it would certainly appear that the postscript message for The Heritage Foundation represents opportunity to drive increases in donor conversion rates from non-member emails within the Kickoff Campaign.

experiments.title: How an exit intent offer impacts email acquisition rate on desktop devices
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an exit intent offer increase email acquisition rate on desktop devices for ‘News/Press Release’ website traffic?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/09/2017 - 09/08/2017
In a previous experiment, Alliance Defending Freedom discovered that exit intent offers dramatically increase email acquisition rate when shown to blog visitors. They wondered: Will there be a similar increase in email acquisition rate if an exit intent offer is shown to visitors on the ‘News/Press Release’ page? They set up a treatment using their winning exit intent offer and launched a test on desktop devices to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Over the period of a month, Alliance Defending Freedom found that the exit intent offer increased the email acquisition rate of the ‘News/Press Release’ page by 297%. The results of this test lead us to a couple of conclusions.
First, it’s clear that giving a visitor a more direct opportunity to sign up for weekly updates improves email acquisition rate on a page. We’ve seen this to be true before and this offer’s general effectiveness is proven to be true again here. Second, it makes sense that this principle remains true for a exit intent offer on ‘News/Press Release’ page. The visitor on this page is geared toward getting the most up-to-date information on a particular matter. It’s possible that the perceived value of the exit intent offer is even higher for these visitors, since news and press releases are generally time sensitive–whereas a blog post may not be. Alliance Defending Freedom will continue to test varying offers and their overall effectiveness for other types of pages on their site.

experiments.title: How streamlining a mobile child sponsorship experience affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will streamlining a mobile experience by removing text value proposition affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/27/2019
Compassion International was examining its child sponsorship funnel and noticed that the mobile experience did not bring children who could be sponsored into the viewport. They wondered if this kept people from realizing that the page allowed them to sponsor children by forcing them to scroll. They developed a treatment that streamlined the “trust seals” into a horizontal row and removed the description of what happens during sponsorship. They launched an A/B test just on mobile to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment reduced conversion by 26.1%. This suggests that there is value to forcing the user to read and absorb the trust seals and value proposition. It also suggests that "access" to the available children is not the issue that is holding conversion back on that page. It suggests that the user is not properly motivated or ready to sponsor immediately—the slight pause and "absorption" of the value proposition has an effect on a visitor's readiness to sponsor.

experiments.title: How leading with our beliefs lifted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion:
With the copy treatment, we really had two components that we wanted to consider testing against the treatment:
Can setting the tone of the message by clearly stating what we believe gain alignment with the audience and inspire them to give? And,
Can we let others tell the reader how awesome we are instead of having to tell them ourselves?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2019 - 01/25/2019
Heritage Action for America was running a campaign through a series of emails to their house file to raise money to empower their lobbyist teams to be able to apply pressure to elected officials to end the government shutdown, and more specifically to approve the president’s request for funding to build a wall along the southern border.
The donation form conversion rate was perceived to need improvement, and we were asked to build a treatment that we believed would increase the donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Due to a temporary "cease fire" announcement from the president, agreeing to a "short term" plan to reopen the government (which has been shut down for 35-days to this point) over a lack of funding for a border wall, the experiment was cut short due to the fact that no further traffic will be sent to this landing page.
With that said, early results seemed promising for the treatment, but further experimentation is required to truly know the impact of leading with a belief statement, and/or if testimonial statements carry more weight than stating that ourselves.

experiments.title: How campaign slidedown and slideouts affect email acquisition on the ‘About Us’ page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a campaign-specific slidedown and slideout increase email acquisition rate on the ‘About Us’ page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2017 - 11/28/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom recently documented an experiment that showed a huge increase in email acquisition rate by using campaign slidedowns and slideouts on their blog pages. They wondered: Will the same campaign elements also increase email acquisition rate on the ‘About Us’ page? They launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Alliance Defending Freedom found that these campaign elements also increased email acquisition rate on the ‘About Us’ page, showing a 523% lift in conversion.  It’s clear that these campaign-specific slidedowns and slideouts were also relevant to visitors of the ‘About Us’ page, though not quite to the same extent as blog visitors. It’s likely that these visitors are higher up in the marketing funnel and, thus, are still in the discovery phase, whereas most blog visitors are actively consuming content they like.
This test reinforces that, when used purposefully, campaign slidedowns and slideouts can be an effective conversion tool for the marketer.

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified landing page help improve email acquisition rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 48% increase to the number of emails acquired. This emphasizes the need to focus a landing page on a single call-to-action when there is one primary metric to influence. In this example, the control had four different actions it was requesting the user to take which took the emphasis of the primary action of subscriber for email.
As a note, the original treatment included an exit intent that the treatment did not have (the result of an oversight in implementation). This means that the impact of the simpler version would likely have been even greater had this been included on that page.

experiments.title: How migrating systems can improve conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving to a new system have any significant impact on email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2018 - 03/10/2018
Buckner International was in the final stages of migrating from one email and landing page system to another. Before moving the email acquisition campaign entirely, we wanted to verify that there were no unforeseen issues or technical bugs that would pop up. To do this, we created an A/A experiment in which both pages were nearly identical; the only major difference was the system used to host the pages and send the emails. Our goal with this experiment was to demonstrate that there was no negative impact on conversion. Basically, with this kind of test, no statistical significance would have been considered a win.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a full 10 days, we discovered that the new system was actually able to fix a bug that we didn’t know we had. It resulted in a 15% increase in conversion! We had seen comments in Facebook (which was t he main traffic source for this campaign) about people having trouble accessing the resource. After multiple tests, we were unable to reproduce any issue so we assumed it was a user error given the already high conversion rate. These results would indicate that the issue did exist and the new system was able to correctly address it.

experiments.title: Does a paginated survey process drive higher survey response rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a paginated survey process drive higher survey response rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2018 - 12/07/2018
After launching a 13-question survey for donors, prospective donors activists and acquisition targets, of which the original treatment was a single-page all questions required survey, we decided to test whether or not a paginated (2-step) survey would drive a higher number of survey takers (or emails acquired).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Final Results:
We found that launching a paginated survey actually drove a 2.3% full survey completion rate. However, we also noted that page one (which only showed the first 6 questions, of which "Your name" and "Your Email" was the first question), actually generated 19% more emails because the survey button click from page 1 to page 2 of the survey actually captured the survey-taker's email address. Once they reached page 2, only 82.9% of survey takers completed the second page of survey questions. The results shown above are "apples to apples" in terms of full survey completions. So, effectively, a 2-step paginated survey, with an email capture on page 1, actually drove 19% more emails acquired, but only 2.3% more fully completed surveys.
Recommendations:
Always run surveys with multiple pages.Make email/name capture the first question in your survey.Be sure you develop email creative/copy to remind survey drop-outs from page 1 to 2 to remind them to complete their survey.Be sure to creatively treat each page of where the survey-taker is in competing the survey (e.g. Page 1/2, or Page 2/2).Be sure to use button text on page 1 that shows them that they're "almost done" with the survey (e.g. "Continue to Page 2/2 »").

experiments.title: Impact of adding additional clarity to the form
experiments.researchQuestion: What is the impact of indicating on the form which step the visitor is on towards completing the CTA?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/19/2018 - 10/26/2018
Since we were testing requiring an address on our pro-life 101 acquisition funnel, we added a sentence to the first step landing page saying “step 1 of 2.” The control page had no address redirect, so there was no other step. Thus, we did not have any text over the form indicating what step it was in the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When we added this language, we did not expect it to increase conversion. If anything, we expected that the promise of an additional, still unknown step, would DECREASE conversion. Instead, it has a significant increase. Our hypothesis is that the further clarity on the form removed doubt in the visitor's mind regarding what was necessary in order to enroll in the course and thus outweighed any negative impact introduced by the friction of an additional step. 

We will test this language on other landing pages to see if it has a similar impact. Visitors to this landing page come via a pledge. We want to see the impact of "step language" on landing pages that do not have pledges before them.

The next test in this series was to test our original offer, the Tough Guys and Drama Queens course, against our newest online course, Bridging the Gap With Your Teen. The layout and targeting was the same as the original test, with copy adjusted.
experiments.keyLearnings: 45% more people signed up for the Bridging the Gap course when presented it than the TGDQ course. It's likely that most people who are regular blog readers of HL content have either already signed up for TGDQ, or have seen it offered multiple times, so offering new and fresh content is the most probably reason for the lift. However, this could also be due to a higher motivation and a more clear value proposition offered with the new course.

experiments.title: How a longer, more rigorous survey affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a longer, more rigorous survey affect completion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/02/2018
The Heritage Foundation was running their Annual Member Survey, which asked members for their opinions on key policy initiatives and then gave them the opportunity to give. The initial treatment for the survey came from the direct mail piece, which was a very basic survey that just asked members to check boxes with the policy initiatives they thought were important. This might have worked in direct mail, but digitally, this seemed very underwhelming to the Heritage team.
They crafted a second survey that was much longer—asking multiple, nuanced questions for each policy area and leaving room for open-ended responses. They knew that this was risky—common wisdom says that many people don’t know what to write in open-ended responses—but they thought that deeper engagement would result in higher conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The longer survey delivered an 8.2% increase in completions—which is counterintuitive due to its length and requirements. But it suggests that people might look at the control survey and think that it is either too light and not worth completing, or requires too much repetitive motion. Some of the success of this experiment might be in the design of the treatment survey, that it varies between style of question and allows open-ended response. 

Interestingly, the treatment survey also produced an 81% increase in donor response, which meant that it was immediately rolled out everywhere.

experiments.title: How deeper survey engagement affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a longer survey that requires more time and attention from each respondent affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/02/2018
The Heritage Foundation was running their Annual Member Survey, which asked members for their opinions on key policy initiatives and then gave them the opportunity to give. The initial treatment for the survey came from the direct mail piece, which was a very basic survey that just asked members to check boxes with the policy initiatives they thought were important. This might have worked in direct mail, but digitally, this seemed very underwhelming to the Heritage team. They crafted a second survey that was much longer—asking multiple, nuanced questions for each policy area and leaving room for open-ended responses. They knew that this was risky—common wisdom says that many people don’t know what to write in open-ended responses—but they thought that deeper engagement would result in higher conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Interestingly, the longer and more engaging survey produced a 9% increase in survey completions, which might have led to an incremental increase in donations just based on volume. But when the team looked at the analytics, the treatment survey (longer, more engaging) produced an 81.9% increase in donations. This indicates that the more a survey requires, the more invested the user might be. And deeper investment, in this case, led to increased response rates in terms of donor conversions. 

This brought up one significant question—what if Heritage tried this in the mail?

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a message that focuses on Donald Trump lift response rates for The Heritage Foundation email file?

Ended On: 08/05/2016
During the 2016 Heritage Board Challenge moneybomb, we wanted to validate a finding from a recent donor study. In this study, we found that Heritage donors had a high affinity for Donald Trump. Given Heritage’s impact on the future administration, we wanted to use this as a reason to give a gift. We decided to test this against the standard “challenge” style messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Donald Trump message resulted in a 346% increase in response rate to the email. This experiment demonstrates the importance in aligning the messaging in the appeals to the donors’ affinities. However, we do need to be careful to validate this in the future, especially related to Trump, as affinities can change over time.

experiments.title: How moving video in a header affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will moving video in a header affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2018 - 04/13/2018
Compassion International had heard that moving video as part of a homepage design increases engagement and hypothesized that it might help provide more of the “emotional heft” that could deliver more conversions as well. They developed a quick-loading motion video image that could load in place of the static image and launched an A/B test to see if it resulted in more child sponsorships—or how it affected metrics as a whole.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment had no discernible effect on child sponsorships—not surprising, since it was on the homepage, and people come to Compassion’s homepage with many varied motivations. This is why homepage testing is particularly difficult! However, the treatment did increase birthday searches (which take place in the header box) by 11%. This is an important finding, because downstream optimizations have been found in the birthday search funnel that could be applied here to increase sponsorships through that funnel.

The next step for Compassion is to institute these downstream optimizations and see if the increased search traffic can be sent through optimized pages to increase sponsorships.

experiments.title: How visually setting off an acquisition offer in an article impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would visually setting apart the content offer within the article increase email conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2018 - 08/13/2018
On the Care Net blog, at the end of the articles, they offer people the opportunity to sign a ledge and get enrolled in a free online course. When looking at the blog/article page, we hypothesized that the content offer was getting lost within the article from from the other content at the end of the article (blog comments, sharing, etc.). To test this, we proposed setting off the content offer visually by putting a small box around it. The content and copy of the offer didn’t change at all, we simply just set it apart within the article.
experiments.keyLearnings: By setting off the content offer from the other content within the article, we were able to increase email acquisition by 36%. Through previous testing we found that just putting a content offer at the end of an article we can acquire a significant amount of names. But now we know that when we visually set that content offer apart we can increase that conversion even more. The content offer was getting lost before. But when we put the box around it, we were able to visually grab people's attention and capture more emails with the offer.

experiments.title: How shortening the length of content impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shortening the value proposition on the page increase email acquisition rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
The Open Letter to the Senate had been a high performing email acquisition campaign for Americans for Prosperity for several weeks now. With an email acquisition rate higher than 60%, we had found it difficult to improve results through optimization.
Based upon the high motivation of the visitors coming to the page, we decided to see if simplifying the page copy could improve the results. The hypothesis was that, given the high motivation of visitors, they did not need as much value proposition to make the conversion. Our goal was to maintain the value expressed through the copy but shorten it up so that new visitors would find it easier to convert.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It would appear that the length of the page did impact the likelihood of a highly motivated audience to convert, albeit only a minimal amount. We were able to statistically prove that the reduced length of copy increased conversion rate by 1.9%.
This is a unique situation where the motivation of the visitors was so high, they did not need much convincing. When audiences are less motivated, when the conversion rate is not as high, increasing the perceived value of the offer (even if it makes the page longer) has proven to be a more effective method for boosting conversion than getting the form “above the fold.” The lift of only 1.9% is evidence of just how minimally impactful the friction associated with copy length is to the conversion process.

experiments.title: How referencing President Trump impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will referencing President Trump in the ads increase the overall email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2018 - 08/06/2018
Americans for Prosperity had been running the Senate Open Letter offer for several weeks as a method of email acquisition. Multiple iterations of testing had led us to the current control. We decided to test out a factor that had proved successful with many other “conservative” organizations and that was leaning into the popularity of President Trump among the potential subscribers.
To isolate and test this factor, we created a new version of the ad that both mentioned President Trump at the beginning of the ad and then incorporated an image of him in the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 2 weeks, we discovered that the President Trump emphasis led to a 15% decrease in email acquisition. It is worth pointing out that during this testing period, AFP and President Trump were mentioned in the news due to a tweet sent out by President Trump referencing them. This likely impacted the test since those that were in favor of President Trump were encouraged to avoid AFP. This is a great demonstration of the dangers of aligning an organization with broad goals to a single political figure.

It is also worth noting that, despite the drop in email acquisition, the cost per email acquisition was actually lower for the Trump-focused ads. This is due to the way Facebook handles its bidding and ad placement. For this reason, we decided to keep both ads running to maximize the potential for the offer.
