experiments.title: How disclosing the amount raised affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will stating progress towards a goal in an email appeal affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2016 - 09/20/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was raising money in honor of North Texas Giving day, and had a group of donors pledge $50,000 if they could raise $50,000 in a week.
They had written an email to existing donors that was very personal in tone, and wanted to test the impact of telling the donors how much had been raised up to that point. They created a treatment email that included a single extra line of copy:
“As of right now, we’ve already raised half of that amount.”
They split the donor file and sent one treatment to each segment to see if they could quantify the impact of this single statement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had a limited sample size due to the targeted nature of the experiment. While we were able to reach a statistically significant result, it should be noted that this small number of conversions could be causing sample size distortion and should be tested again with a larger segment of donors.
The treatment copy with the goal increased conversion by 130.8%, which shows that giving donors specific details about the campaign increases the likelihood that they will convert.
There’s another force at work here: social proof. Persuasion science shows that people are more likely to get involved in an activity if they know other people are doing it as well. Disclosing the amount implied that many other donors have already given, and gives the current reader (who has not yet given) the opportunity to be the “closer” on the campaign.
This is a powerful learning that can be employed in many different forms to reduce anxiety and increase conversion.

experiments.title: How clarity in the call-to-action affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call-to-action with a compelling video increase course signups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2017 - 02/19/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a new course called “Can You Trust the Bible?” They had shot a video of their Academic Dean explaining the course, and wanted to see how it performed against a direct invitation from the President to join the course. So they created two entrance paths to the course. The first was their control—a direct ask, via email, from the President of DTS. The second was an invitation from the President with a call-to-action to “watch the video to learn more about the course”. They hypothesized that the softer call-to-action would drive more traffic to the landing page, and the short video with a clear call-to-action would produce more overall conversions.
They split their file and closely monitored the data to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 32.6% decrease in signups from the control. This shows that the softer ask does not produce more motivation to sign up for the course, even with the added benefit of the video. There could be a few factors at play here. First, all clicks are not created equal—a click to “learn more”, in this case, is far less valuable than a click to “sign up”. Second, video might actually be obscuring the value proposition by not allowing the user to read it in their own time. Many people can read much faster than a video can communicate information, which leads them to be more likely to accept an offer where they control the cadence of the value proposition.
This does not necessarily mean, however, that the video doesn’t have a place in the marketing schedule of a new course—it may just mean that it needs to be placed later in the schedule to motivate users who were not incentivized by the original message.

experiments.title: How video ads affect email acquisition rate for a free course
experiments.researchQuestion: Will video ads produce an increase in email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2017 - 03/03/2017
DTS was promoting their new course, called Can You Trust the Bible?. They had a special video from their Academic Dean to promote it and wanted to test using this video to promote the course. Video ads had shown in some experiments to improve conversion for offers, although DTS had previously used static ads.
They split audiences and tested each ad unit to see which resulted in a better cost-per-acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The video ads produced a 37% decrease in conversion when tested against the static ads. While a deeper look at the data showed that many more people engaged with the video ads (which means they clicked somewhere on the ad), the video ad was either too distracting or not compelling enough to convince them to take the next step and sign up for the course. This seems to indicate that, in this case, video is more of a distraction than a motivator to take the course.
This doesn’t entirely rule out video ads on Facebook. There have been experiments where video ads produce a lift, and they can be effective at remarketing. DTS will continue to test video as a promotional tool for their free courses.

experiments.title: How a ‘Campaign Opt-Out’ Link Affects Email Unsubscribe Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a ‘Campaign Opt-Out’ link Decrease Email Unsubscribe Rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2017 - 09/13/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary was running a campaign for North Texas Giving Day, a huge fundraising opportunity each year for North Texas nonprofits. As they planned for this campaign, they wanted to give their audience a way to remove themselves from further communication about North Texas Giving Day if they weren’t able to join this year. They wondered: If we allow individuals to ‘opt-out’ of a campaign, will the unsubscribe rate decrease? They created a treatment with one line of copy and a campaign ‘Opt-out’ link for viewers to click, and then they launched an A/B split test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary found that the treatment version decreased the unsubscribe rate by 33%. It’s clear that this treatment reduced the friction and anxiety of the email viewer by offering a clear alternative option for dismissing themselves from this campaign communication. This test shows us that some individuals may not want to unsubscribe from all communication, but when they are left with no other option, they will.
By providing a clear and direct way for viewers to ‘raise their hand’ and decide that they won’t be able to join in a fundraising campaign, we can decrease our email attrition rate and keep a wider audience engaged for a longer period of time with the mission of our organization–in this case, the equipping of Christian leaders.

experiments.title: How a personal tone affects donations in an email fundraising appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radically more personal email increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2016 - 03/22/2016
CaringBridge sent several email fundraising appeals as part of a campaign. Their team had the second appeal ready to go, a personal letter from one of the development team members. However, they hypothesized that they could increase results by removing the parts of the email that stood out as “marketing” and increasing the personal tone of the email copy.
The control had a logo at the top, which stood out as something that normal personal emails don’t have. It also had a call-to-action button, which rarely, if ever are used in personal emails. But the biggest shift was in the copy tone. The control was written from a detached third-person point of view that didn’t feel like someone sat down at a computer and wrote it.
They created a treatment that moved the logo to the signature area, replaced the button with a text link, and drastically changed the copy. The treatment copy started by acknowledging
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment generated a 145.5% lift in donations. This shows, along with several other tests, that personal tone can have a radical effect on fundraising results. As marketers, we spend too much time crafting a message engineered to deliver the desired result, without remembering the way that people actually write emails. When we remember that fundraising is about relationships, we can use empathy to actually communicate with our donors.
Email is so much more than a “one-to-many” tool — it’s something that most people use every day to stay in touch, and it gives us the ability to give that personal experience to thousands of people at the same time.

experiments.title: How clarity and ordering on a donation form affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will ordering the donation steps and clarifying language increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2016 - 08/04/2016
CaringBridge had done some user testing in anticipation of launching a new site experience. During that testing, a few users asked questions that suggested that the donation process was unclear and could use clarity and ordering. Based on these responses, they developed a treatment that numbered the header copy in the donation steps, used more personal and descriptive language, and increased perceived security around the donor’s information.
In the first header, they changed “Make your donation” to “1. Choose your donation amount”. Then, they added a number to the next call-to-action, “Write a Message of Support”. Finally, they changed the header “Your Information” to “3. Enter Your Information (It’s secure)”, which was designed to add clarity and reduce anxiety.
They launched an A/B test to quantify the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the ordered copy produced a 12.5% lift in donations, which proved that the user testing had unlocked a powerful principle. While CaringBridge saw their donation process as one big step, the donors responded positively when they were told what it took to complete each step with numerical ordering and increased clarity.
This is a powerful reminder that we must learn to see our donation processes through the eyes of the donor to properly assist them and markedly improve conversions.

This suggests that CaringBridge hit on some powerful language that hits home with their donors, which inspired them to isolate how much the matching challenge affected results by re-testing this in a normal time period. .

They calculated that it takes $30 to power a single CaringBridge site for a month, and developed a treatment of their tribute widget (which appears on the most highly-trafficked sections of their site) that called this out.

The treatment, with the tangible connection to the gift amount, produced a 34.3% increase in donations. This showed that while the emotional “connection-based” language had been (and was still) effective at attracting donations, there was a segment of users who needed to better understand what their gift did—and how their choice to donate could have a “tangible” impact when the entire platform they were supporting was virtual.
This prompted more testing, specifically around how to leverage this messaging in the gift array.

experiments.title: How an offer is presented on a homepage impacts email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which version would increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2017 - 08/23/2017
Care Net offers a free online course called Pro-Life 101. To take advantage of their site traffic and convert those visitors into names and potentially donors, they put this content offer on the homepage of their website. They tested two different ways to present the offers. One way was telling people about the course and having a button that said, “Get Started Today!”. The other way was telling people about the course and having an inline form for people to sign up and take the course right on the homepage. They did a three way test with the control, course offer with button and a course offer with an inline form to see which version would increase email acquisition from their homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Simply drawing more attention to the course on the homepage was not enough to effect a statistically significant difference in course registrations (conversions). We had to draw attention in a way that did not present the ask to register (form fields) too quickly. Thus, it was the combination of getting someone’s attention and an additional page (with additional value proposition information and form fields) that resulted in the greatest possible result.
This ultimately teaches us that the value proposition for the course is complex and we need to achieve mini-yeses in the acquisition process. In this case, the “Get Started Now” button was a mini-yes in the larger funnel to convert more people with the online course.

experiments.title: How focusing on the benefits of the offer impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing on the benefits of the action the offer promotes increase email acquisition for a biblically focused offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2017 - 07/31/2017
Buckner International had been running a devotional offer for parents as a means of email acquisition for roughly 2 months. We had performed a series of experiments to boost the performance of the campaign but none had given us the kind of lift that we look for on a campaign like this.
We noticed that the cost per email had begun to rise over time. Instead of turning off the campaign, we decided to try out one more experiment. In this instance, we wanted to see if we could reframe the offer to highlight the benefits of prayer itself (not just the benefits of the offer) and incorporating actual scripture into the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test teaches us the importance of testing each of the reasons/needs for someone to engage in an offer.
While the control’s benefit is perfectly fine, it is limited in value according to this audience. By connecting the offer to a deeper, more powerful value/desire in the audience (i.e. leaving no stone unturned for your kids versus making prayer more interesting/less boring), more people responded.
As a result, the new ad achieved a 79% increase in emails and reduced cost per email by 43%.

experiments.title: How framing the audience for an offer affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Are people more likely to download an offer if they personally benefit from it, rather than that offer benefiting someone else?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/23/2017 - 09/12/2017
Buckner International ran an offer focused on helping your children learn how to react to poverty. They wanted to know if refocusing the offer copy around the person receiving the ebook, rather than how their children would benefit, would impact conversion rate. The ads that preceded these landing pages also reflected this reframing of copy. The other page elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with parent-focused copy produced a 39% lift in conversion rate. What we learned from this test is that people are inherently more likely to download something that immediately betters their own understanding or helps them grow personally, rather than benefiting another person (their children, in this case.) Framing copy with this in mind increases the value to the person downloading the offer.

experiments.title: How friction affects email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Which email signup form will produce the best conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2015 - 07/01/2015
In order to boost the organization’s digital reach and impact, Alliance Defending Freedom (ADF) is looking to grow their email list size. One way to do that is to increase the number of emails acquired organically on their website. To accomplish this goal, we decided to create a new treatment of their existing email acquisition page.
Our hypothesis was that we could increase the perceived value of the newsletter by unpacking the benefits to the subscriber. We explained the value of each publication and offered links to the type of information they would receive. Additionally ADF was going to be moving to a new website design later in the year and we decided now would be a good time to incorporate elements the new design.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It is not uncommon in our attempt to make things look better that we take a step backwards when it comes to conversion. “Ugly converts” is a phrase that we throw around to the chagrin of our designers and, in this case, the data backed it up. In our attempt to add more value for the user, the form becomes visually complex, but more aesthetically pleasing. When you look at the forms side by side you can see how the addition of news items in the sidebar and email list options in the treatment gave users additional decisions to make and distracted them from the ultimate goal of signing up.
Our hypothesis is that, while the design of the treatment form is different, the likely reason why the control form won is that it limited the number of decisions a person needed to make to sign up. The headline for the control form is straightforward, visually the form is simpler and appears easier to fill out and there is no question that you are going to be signing up to receive emails.
Going forward we are going to see if there are other treatments we can would add perceived value to the user without adding friction or distracting from the ultimate goal of the page.

experiments.title: How changing the offer and acquisition process affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will offering an eBook in place of the general email acquisition offer increase the number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2016 - 01/28/2016
In the week leading up to the March for Life, Alliance Defending Freedom had plans to publish a series of pro-life blog posts. They also had just produced a new downloadable resource that contained stories and explained the legal rights of “Sidewalk Counselors.” Since the two topics were so closely aligned, we thought this new resource might be an appealing offer for the blog traffic.
The current offer on the site was a general email sign up opportunity that the visitors could fill out right on the page. This new resource required the visitor’s full address information so it would require a link to a second page requesting additional information.
We decided to create a new slide out banner that we would then test against the existing email signup opportunity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The end result of the new “Sidewalk Counselor” resource was a 79.4% decrease in the number of email addresses acquired. The added friction that came from the two step process and the additional fields required to download the resource, significantly impacted the conversion rate.
Another interesting aspect of the test is that we were able to track the number of people that clicked on the resource link. Even when comparing these clicks to those that filled out the general email offer, we still saw a 34% decrease in conversion. It would seem that the added friction was only a portion of the problem; there was also not as much interest in such a specific resource. Moving forward, we are going to try experimenting with resources that would have a broader appeal.

Once the messaging approach for the second email in the year end campaign was proven with a previous test, Heritage wanted to determine the best way to roll this email out to the entire file.  They developed a test to determine if giving the constituents more information or less in the email would drive additional traffic and, concurrently, additional giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: The shorter email drove 83% more traffic to the website.  Often times, emails that drive incremental traffic produce lower response rates due to the traffic being less qualified.  In this instance, the overall response rate was 128% higher than the longer email meaning we drove more traffic that was more highly qualified.  This is a messaging approach to be used in the future.

experiments.title: How banner imagery impacted visitors exploring the website
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the banner increase the visitors likelihood to engage with the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/29/2016 - 05/04/2016
In Alliance Defending Freedom’s continued efforts to optimize the visitor experience on their blog, we created a new experiment with the goal of increasing visitor engagement. The format of most blog posts includes a large banner image at the top with the headline over the banner. We had the hypothesis that this might be decreasing a visitors’ likelihood to read the full post due to “banner blindness” or people ignoring the banners. If someone did ignore the banner, they may miss the headline which would cause a disconnect on the social post that brought them to the blog.
We decided to test moving the banner image to be within the copy and placing the headline at the top. We then monitored the number of people that read to the bottom of the page, the number that explored the website, and how many emails were acquired from the visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The changes made to the page decreased the likelihood of a visitor exploring the full website by 26.6%.  However, this same experiment increased the likelihood of a visitor reading the full article by 3.5%. The decrease in visitors exploring the site is likely due to the removal of the sub-navigation.
While this experiment did increase the likelihood to read the article, it was such a smaller increase compared to the percentage that did not explore the site. As a result, it would seem this treatment is not ideal to implement at a broad scale. Instead, we should look for ways to highlight the content so that visitors are encouraged to explore the site.

During discussions for the campaign launch, we identified a variety of options to use with the final call to action. We decided to test two of the more popular options, a more general statement about bathroom privacy versus one that specifically mentioned a recent North Carolina bill that had been in the news.
experiments.keyLearnings: The North Carolina specific pledge was able to increase email acquisition by 29.7%. By using recent, high profile news topics, we were able to make the pledge more relevant to the visitors. With increased relevancy, we saw an increase in their likelihood to convert.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2015 - 10/21/2015
Hillsdale College was promoting a free first chapter of Dr. Larry Arnn’s book on Winston Churchill to grow their email list. Facebook advertising allowed them to target very specific audiences, which meant that our visitors presumably had a very high motivation to download the offer. While the page was converting more than 25% of visitors, we hypothesized that we could improve this conversion rate. Typically, motivation is the hardest conversion factor to affect. But since we were able to focus our targeting to highly motivated prospects, we wondered if the amount of copy on the page was too much, as it pushed the name capture form below the fold. We set up a treatment that removed the extra descriptive copy and reduced friction on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test shows that when you can hypertarget your traffic sources and drive highly motivated visitors, you might not need to “sell” as much. However, if we were attracting visitors from less targeted sources, we might want to re-test this control.

experiments.title: How a two-step form reduced affects cognitive friction and conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a two-step form allow for the capture of more information compared to a single page form that acquired all of the information at one time?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2015 - 09/09/2015
For The Heritage Foundation’s Presidents’ Club Meeting registration, we wanted to find a way to increase the number of attendees to the event. Historically, the forms had all been a single page with all of the data capture fields required before someone could register. These fields included selecting each session they would like to attend and entering all of their information into the form.
We had the hypothesis that if we moved the process to a two-step process, it would create less friction in the visitor’s minds since the forms would be shorter. This two step process would also allow us to look up the donor’s information (based upon their email address or ID) and use this to pre-populate the second form.  We were able to not only fill in their address information but also pre-select the sessions that their membership level qualified for.  We wanted to see if this process would lead to an increased number of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The two step form led to a 99% increase in the number of donors completing their registration information. This increase is the result of more than just the two step form; the ability to pre-fill the donor’s information into the second form would have significantly reduced the friction associated with the process. This reduction in friction made the form easier to understand end simpler to fill out.

experiments.title: How talking about the larger vision at the wrong time affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which treatment will produce the greatest impact on donor conversion and average gift, the immediate goal of the course or the long term vision of Hillsdale’s College.
experiments.summary:

As part of a March fundraising campaign, we created a landing page that cast a bigger vision for Hillsdale College’s Constitution 101 course. In contrast, the immediate conversion page when people first sign up for the course is more about the here and now. As successful as the March campaign was, we wanted to test these two variants to see which would be most effective in donor’s minds.
experiments.keyLearnings: The 29% decrease in conversion demonstrated to us that the bigger vision was too much for people who had not yet experienced the course. These individuals did not yet see the reason that more people should enroll in the class so did not buy into the larger vision.  This messaging approach may work for future conversion options for these constituents but not this early in their donor lifecycle.

experiments.title: How a visitor-focused headline affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What language inspires more people to sign up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2015 - 03/16/2015
Headlines are one of the most critical elements of landing pages.  They are the first thing most visitors see and their job is to put the entire offer into context for the reader.  Each word carries a measure of influence on the readers ultimate decision.
Hillsdale was launching a free online class to acquire more emails addresses for their file.  We wanted to test language that had historically been used internally at the college when talking about classes (“enroll”) versus one that, in our minds, carried less friction (“activate”).
experiments.keyLearnings: Changing just one word in the sub-headline of the page led to a 31.5% increase in the number of emails acquired. The word “enroll” carried a high amount of cognitive friction since it is often associated with both the time and effort of enrolling in something.  Alternatively, “activate” is a quick action which carried far less friction.

experiments.title: How point-of-donation upgrading affects  conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Do members to the Heritage foundation give more in order to receive greater benefits?  How does communicating the available benefits of the next level affect conversion and average gift amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2015 - 01/23/2015
For existing members to the Heritage Foundation, the donation form is prepopulated with the donor’s prior giving amount as a suggested gift.  However, in the case of the control, it is not clearly presented to the member what level their donation qualifies them for and what benefits are provided.  As a way to upgrade these donors, this experiment contemplated the value of communicating the membership level and associated benefits for a donation at the next highest level of membership.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this experiment are very interesting.  On one hand, including the upgrade language increases average gift amount by 12.2%.  But on the other hand, the upgrade language decreased average gift by 12.6%.  This suggests that though the tactic may prove to be effective to get a donor to upgrade their gift amount, it may also turn others off.  More testing needs to be conducted to understand how changes in the language might be able to mitigate the drop off in conversion while still positively affecting average gift amount.

experiments.title: How specificity in an email appeal affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will vision language outperform project-specific language in an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2016 - 09/12/2016
Hillsdale College was running a campaign to raise money to support their Constitutional education programs, in honor of Constitution Day. They drafted many email appeals for this campaign, and wanted to test them against each other to see which one performed best.
Their control in this case was a project-based appeal. They were launching a new initiative, the National Index of Constitutional literacy, and focused an appeal around this specific project.
They created a control that used “vision” language, explaining the change that Hillsdale hoped to see in our country because of their Constitutional education programs. This appeal was much less specific and more emotional.
They split their file and launched an A/B split test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment “vision-casting” appeal produced a 68.3% increase in donations. This tells Hillsdale that emotion-based appeals may be more effective than project-specific appeals, at least to the majority of donors.
This doesn’t necessarily mean that Hillsdale should go all-in on vision-only appeals, but it does give helpful insight into the nature of communications early in the campaign.

experiments.title: How third-party validation affects email acquisition for a free digital offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity and adding social proof increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2016 - 07/01/2016
The Heritage Foundation was giving away a digital version of their popular 475-page Guide to the Constitution in exchange for an email address. They had a landing page that was converting 58% of visitors. But this meant that 42% of visitors weren’t downloading the digital Guide, so their team wanted to try four tweaks to increase conversion.
One confusing part of the offer was that the digital version of the Guide was in the form of a microsite, so they were really offering “access” rather than a digital download. This caused potential confusion to the user, so they marketed the offer as a digital download (since there was an available PDF).
They rewrote the headline to increase the clarity of the offer and who it was from.
They added multiple images to the sidebar to show that the Guide could be read on any device
They added user reviews (pulled from Amazon) to increase social proof.
They changed the button copy from “get access now” to “get the Guide now”
Then, they launched an A/B test to see if the new page increased conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment converted 65.4% of visitors to accept the offer — an increase of 11.2%! This shows that the new landing page tweaks did in fact increase clarity and add some social proof that helped convince the visitor to take action.
While this is a great lift and results in more emails for the same spend, it still means that 35% of our traffic is not converting—which means more testing and optimization is needed!
