experiments.title: Will a personalized dear reader perform better than a non personalized?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a personalized ask for the dear reader on the content page will achieve a higher donor conversion because the ask is more humanized and they can associate it with the editor that they see writing them everyday.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
In previous campaigns we’ve seen dear readers work remarkable well for TW. For this test we wanted to build off of our previous tests to find the best way to present a dear reader to their audience.
In this case, we wanted to see if having a personalized ask for the editor would improve conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that having a personalized ask for the editor in the content page did not result in an increase in donor conversion rates as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze why the personalized ask did not resonate with the audience as expected. It may be beneficial to conduct further research to better understand the preferences and motivations of the audience before implementing personalized asks. Additionally, it is essential to always run experiments to statistically significant levels of confidence (95% or higher) to ensure the results are reliable.

Moving forward, it may be valuable to test different types of personalization or messaging to see what resonates best with donors. By learning from this experiment and making adjustments based on the findings, we can continue to optimize our strategies for increasing donor conversions in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How sending “View Full Post” traffic to the single-post page impacts tribute giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending “View Full Post” traffic to a single-post page will help improve the user-experience while also increasing traffic to the 16003 Tribute ask—increasing tribute giving and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2025 - 02/17/2025
In the current experience, longer posts from an author are shortened with a hyperlink “Show More” which then expands the full post. From a UI perspective, there are limitations to this experience. In mobile especially (where a majority of CB’s traffic originates from) the full post expanding in feed can overwhelm and disorient—requiring the user to scroll all the way back up or down to resume their browsing of the feed.
We hypothesize that by changing this hyperlinked copy to “View Full Post” and instead sending the traffic to the designated Single-Post Page will eliminate this friction, while also increasing Tribute giving. The single-post page contains a tribute widget (16003) that typically converts at a high volume. We believe this could accomplish both goals at once—making the experience more user-friendly while also sending more traffic to the 16003 Tribute ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
To understand the key learnings from this experiment we must first differentiate two tracked metrics, that appear very similar at first glance. The first is Tribute Donations/Revenue (From Widget). This metric tracks tribute gifts only on the single-post page—with the 16003 code. This was important to analyze because ultimately it was a large part of our hypothesis: sending more traffic to single-post page will increase gifts to the widget on this page.

However, there is a more encompassing Tribute Gift metric that was measured in this test as well. Because users can technically make a tribute gift on an author's page to various different widgets as well as the donate button in navigation—tracking this metric was equally important. It would answer a key question: are we simply directing traffic to give on a different widget, or does this lead to an overall lift in gifts and revenue as well.

With this in mind, here are some key callouts from this experiment.
The click rate was about the same between "Show More" and "View Full Post." The changed copy did not necessarily mean users were more likely to expand the post.
 	There was a significant 1376.9% increase in donations to Tribute (Widget/16003) in the treatment with a 100% level of confidence. Confirming part of our hypothesis that by increasing traffic to the single-post page, we would increase donations to the 16003 Tribute Widget.
 	However, pulling back to analyze all Tribute Gifts and Revenue shows that the treatment seems to also result in a decrease overall. This is not statistically valid at an 80% level of confidence, however, the trend did remain consistent throughout the entirety of the test run. This could indicate something very important.
overall

experiments.title: How relocating the Tribute widget to the right for all users impacts tribute giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that relocating the tribute widget to the right side for visitors on a user’s profile will achieve an increase in tribute donations because it will spotlight the ask and keep the CTA’s distinct..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2024 - 06/11/2024
For CaringBridge visitors on a user profile, they are met with a few different action possibilities. They can read a User’s posts, leave a comment of their own, as well as a make a gift a through the Tribute Widget. Historically, this widget has been one of CaringBridge’s highest sources for donations on the site. With the migration to CB2 however, they received complaints from users who found the widget intrusive or misleading — implying that they must make a gift in order to leave a comment. This was not the case. To help address this confusion and remove potential friction for users who do not want to make a gift, we’ve hypothesized that pinning the Tribute Widget to the right would help better spotlight the ask and keep these two CTA’s (whether to comment or to make a gift) as separate for the visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that relocating the Tribute Widget to the right side for visitors on a user's profile did not achieve the intended increase in tribute donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group.

Before abandoning this experiment, we hypothesized a contributor that could be contributing to the decrease in Tribute Gifts:

Have we unintentionally reduced the visibility of the Tribute? There is potential to re-test with a widget that has higher visibility by outlining with a color or making the widget a solid color.

In future experimentation, it would be important to consider the potential impact of changing the placement of key elements on a website, particularly when it comes to donation prompts. It is crucial to test such changes thoroughly before implementing them site-wide. Additionally, further testing should be conducted to determine the actual impact of relocating the Tribute Widget and to understand why it may have led to a decrease in donations in this particular experiment.

experiments.title: How repositioning the tribute widget to be inside the comment section impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that unpinning the tribute widget from the top and placing it within the comment section for visitors on a user’s profile will achieve an increase in tribute gifts because it will address the perceived friction that the widget is a paywall.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
With the migration to  2.0, CaringBridge received complaints from users who found the tribute widget intrusive or misleading — implying that they must make a gift in order to leave a comment. This was not the case. To help address this confusion and remove potential friction for users who do not want to make a gift, we’ve hypothesized that pinning the Tribute Widget into the comment section rather than having it at the top would better spotlight the ask while also clearing up the misconception that there was a paywall to commeting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that moving the tribute widget from the top of the page to within the comment section did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing tribute gifts. In fact, there was a 21.2% decrease in donations for all traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group. While the statistical level of confidence was only 93.5%, indicating that the results are not yet fully valid, they are approaching validity.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the placement and design of features such as the tribute widget to ensure they do not create confusion or friction for users. It may be beneficial to conduct further testing with different variations or to gather qualitative feedback from users to better understand their perceptions and preferences. Additionally, it is essential to monitor user behavior and donation patterns closely to accurately assess the impact of any changes made.

experiments.title: Will a missional ask or a premium offer perform better as a text message
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a mission ask for text message receipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they are focused on impacting and supporting the mission of PTV rather than being motivated to receive an item.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/02/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each June and December, PTV does a match campaign to encourage donors to give as their impact is doubled.
Typically PTV will lead with their premium book with the match to encourage giving.
In email we have seen success in testing the premium book vs a missional ask, and seeing the missional ask prevail as winner.
Therefore we decided to test and see if the same would be true through a text message.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a missional ask in a text message resulted in a 42.2% increase in revenue and 40% increase in donations for Pathway to Victory. This indicates that donors who received a message focused on impacting and supporting the mission of PTV were more likely to donate compared to those who were motivated by receiving a premium book.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of missional asks in different communication channels to see if the trend of higher donor conversion rates holds true across various platforms. Additionally, conducting more experiments with a larger sample size may help to increase the level of statistical confidence and provide more conclusive results.

Overall, the results suggest that emphasizing the mission and impact of Pathway to Victory in donation appeals may be more compelling to donors than offering incentives or premium items. It would be valuable to incorporate this learning into future fundraising campaigns to potentially increase donor engagement and support for the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How using donor-centric language impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing organizational language for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there is more “you” focused language that aligns the donor’s beliefs with the donation ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2024 - 06/14/2024
When sending visitors from a free course registration page to a donation form, we noticed there were multiple references to the organization and fewer references to the page visitor. We wondered if treating this donation page like a conversation with a potential donor would improve conversion on the page. We developed a treatment based on a past variant that had out-performed the control and ran the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing organizational language for donation page visitors can potentially increase donor conversion. The treatment, which involved using more "you" focused language to align the donor's beliefs with the donation ask, resulted in a 20.6% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 82.7%, so the results are not statistically valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue exploring the impact of using donor-centric language on donation page performance. By refining the treatment and running further tests with a larger sample size, we may be able to validate the initial findings and gain more insights into what drives donor conversion on the page.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline for visitors will achieve an increase in our donor conversion rate because  it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 01/05/2024
In an effort to increase our instant donor conversion rate on our Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a continuation headline test. The new headline reads: “Your 18-month calendar will be mailed to your address shortly. Before you go, would you consider something?”
The continuation headline will help to encourage visitors to read further down the page, enabling them to read more value proposition, and then finally see the donation page.
By removing “Thank you” from the headline, we believe less people will perceive the donation page as the final step in the process and rather, make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a continuation headline on the Prayer Calendar funnel led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic at a 73.8% lift. This result suggests that the continuation headline effectively communicated the next steps to the visitors and avoided the perception that their contribution was complete.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is necessary to validate the findings and ensure that the continuation headline consistently generates positive results.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the continuation headline to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be valuable to explore other variations in the headline to identify the most effective messaging for visitor conversion.
Overall, the findings of this experiment provide valuable insights for optimizing the Prayer Calendar funnel, but further testing and refinement are required to cement the effectiveness of the continuation headline.

experiments.title: How increasing an ask from $30 to $50 impacts giving for onsite tribute widgets
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the ask amount  for site visitors will achieve an increase in revenue  because there is a larger initial ask (without doubling the previous amount).
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/12/2024
CaringBridge has a long-standing ask of $30 on their on-site tribute widgets. This was the approximate cost to power a site for a month several years ago but has since needed updating. In order to get closer to the actual cost to-date, we tested using a $50 ask instead – knowing that we could potential lose donors but perhaps make up the difference in revenue by seeing a higher average gift. This was tested among multiple places on-site where a tribute widget appeared.
The first test ran (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/j6kp) was a $60 ask and that proved to be too high for site visitors – resulting in a valid 16% decrease in conversion rate and a decrease in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Increasing the ask amount for site visitors from $30 to $50 resulted in a 10.3% decrease (LoC 90%) in donations for all traffic. When looking at revenue we saw a 1.7% decrease (LoC 40%) - which is virtually no difference in revenue. While the larger ask decreased conversion rate, it did not decrease conversion rate as much as the $60 ask and the higher average gift made up for the difference in revenue.
It's suggested that in the next iteration of the test, we pose a lower ask for a shorter time period (i.e. $25 powers a site for 2 weeks) in order lower the barrier to entry and increase conversion rate.

experiments.title: How removing an image in an MMS message affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image in an MMS message for lapsed donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appear to be more personal and will look less like marketing. because it will appear to be more personal and will look less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running SMS and MMS ads as part of their kickoff campaign. They had leveraged texting because of the personal nature of the communication, two-way conversation ability, and high deliverability, and wanted to test a P2P MMS platform However, they wondered if their personal message was undermined by the image they sent alongside the message, since that’s not something that people usually send along with 1:1 communications.
They split their list in half and sent half of the list an MMS without an image to see if it increased conversion rate. This ended up converting the message to an SMS message, which should be noted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing an image in an MMS message for lapsed donors resulted in a significant increase in donations. The treatment group saw an 83.0% increase in donations as compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is only 84.0%, which is below the desired threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue exploring the impact of removing images in MMS messages for lapsed donors. However, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient and that the statistical level of confidence reaches the desired threshold. This will help to ensure the validity of the results and allow for more confident decision making. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further investigate the reasons behind the increase in donations when images are removed, as this may provide insights for future campaigns and messaging strategies.

experiments.title: Dear Reader You vs. Organization Copy
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the value proposition more you focused for people who land on the dear readers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we will have created appeal in saying you can help people instead of KCBI can help people.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2024
For this experiment we wanted to see if changing the copy of a dear reader to be more “you” focused instead of organizational focused if we could convert more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: For the KCBI audience, it seems that they would like to give to a ministry that is making a difference. This experiment caused a 51% decrease in donor conversion by changing the copy to be 'you' focused.

experiments.title: How a video trailer vs the first video lesson as a YouTube ad affected instant donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a trailer for YouTube viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2023
While we were in our Google Ads account we discovered that we were running the 1st video lesson of the course instead of the course trailer as an ad. We had already seen good performance from the 1st lesson and saw this as an opportunity to test the course trailer against it and see which one performed better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We wanted to try this experiment in order to understand which type of content was more appealing to YouTube audiences and to better understand what types of videos work on YouTube.
Cost per Donor:
C: First Lesson $132.59 (0.48roi)
T: Trailer $164.41 (0.28roi)
The first lesson captures the viewers attention for a greater length of time which amplifies the intent to donate.

experiments.title: How an exit intent popup on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing an exit intent modal for Lamplighters page visitors will achieve an increase in emails without a decrease in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2022 - 07/22/2022
Prison Fellowship International was running a lot of paid traffic to generate donations for their Lamplighters offer to share the gospel with prisoners. They knew that conversion rates were about 4%—and though they wanted to increase the conversion rate, they also wanted to capture the emails of people who were coming to the site but not giving so they could “waste fewer marketing dollars”.
However, they wanted to make sure that their exit intent popup didn’t reduce conversion, as it can sometimes be triggered when people are moving their cursor around without the intent to exit the page.
They built a popup in Unbounce and used Google Optimize to trigger it for half the visitors to determine the impact.
Note: Because the Unbounce popup had to be triggered for all visitors, the “treatment” removed the popup.
experiments.keyLearnings:
On desktop devices (where the exit intent popup fired), there was a 24.7% decrease in conversion with the exit intent popup. This showed that giving people an option not to give (even if it was on their way out) reduced conversion by adding friction.

It also suggests that this popup being triggered by cursor movement might trigger for more people than those who are leaving the page, and get in the way of donations.

The popup has since been turned off, as it wasn't a good exchange for the ~60 emails that were acquired through the popup.

experiments.title: How adding an ebook offer to a survey funnel impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ebook offer in a funnel  for survey takers will achieve a higher instant donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2022 - 06/20/2022
Reasons to believe was running an offer that encouraged people to take a science faith survey. After taking the science faith survey, they were directed to an instant donation page. Seeing that they had a low instant donor conversion rate, we wanted to add an additional step in the funnel that would provide additional value for the user. We selected one of their high performing ebook, Genesis 1, as an additional offer we can provide. The new funnel would lead people to the ebook landing page after they filled out the survey. Once they filled out the ebook form, they were led to an instant donation page. Our hypothesis that by giving the survey taker additional value in the form of an ebook, they would be more likely to give.
This was ran as a redirect experiment as 2 separate funnels. The traffic to this funnel was generated primarily through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the results of this experiment did not validate we did see a near directional 75% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 82%. While there weren't many donations in this experiment it ran for nearly 2 weeks and was not gaining much traction. It's possible that RTB's audience may have already received this ebook and did not take action in downloading it. As a result, they did not get to the instant donation page and had no opportunity to give. It may be worth testing this on some sort of prospecting channel where it is more unlikely that anyone has encountered this specific ebook. It's possible that the donation page itself needs further testing to motivate survey takers to donate.

experiments.title: How moving the donation page copy to “below the fold” impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the donation page copy to “below the fold” to streamline the giving experience for prospective donors in the text to give channel will achieve decrease revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 05/23/2022
As a part of a recent acquisition campaign in the “text to give” channel, the MMS partner noted that the donation page copy was “too long” and felt like it should be moved to “below the fold” (a nice newspaper term), or they wouldn’t agree to promote the offer because it was likely to not be as productive as what their “best practices” would dictate.
So, to appease them, the Leadership Institute agreed to run an A/B experiment to track the performance on the campaign and see how it impacted overall giving from their audiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 91.3% level of confidence, we observed a decrease in revenue of -57.6%, largely driven by a decrease in the average gift of more than 50% coming from visitors flowing through the treatment variant.
What's interesting is that there was the exactly same number of transactions between the treatment and the control experiences (0% difference), however, the generosity of the donors in the control experience was nearly twice as much as the treatment visitors.
The question is: Why?
In reviewing the transactions, we noticed that there were not any outlier gifts in the overall experiment, but there were 4 gifts at or above $100 that came through the control experience.
When you consider the channel used to drive visitors to this page (MMS) — it's clear what is behind this increase in generosity. The MMS channel messaging is designed to get a click, but after a visitor arrives on the donation page — the lack of value proposition and reason to give communicated on the page was enough to drive visitors in the treatment to give at lower amounts. Alternatively, when the value proposition to give in the control experience was placed where it normally has been, it showed that donors to the control decided not only to give at the same rate as those in the treatment experience, but they were more informed on WHY they should give and WHO they were giving to — thereby increasing the amount they decided to give.
When possible, it's highly recommended — especially in campaigns that are not driving donor-motivated clicks (like from your house email file) — to include copy that properly articulates the value proposition on the donation page, so that once they arrive on the page, they'll not only decide to give, but when they do — they'll give more generously.

experiments.title: How the addition of a callout clarifying the impact of a specific donation amount impacted donor conversion on a campaign donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a callout clarifying the impact of a specific donation amount will increase donor conversion on the campaign donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2022 - 04/15/2022
For KCBI’s January Day of Bibles campaign, they were wanting donors to sponsor putting 5,000 Bibles into the hands of people in need. We hypothesized that adding a callout to the donation form on the campaign donation page that clarified the impact of a specific donation amount would increase donor conversion and reduce friction because visitors would have greater clarity about the impact of their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
During the three days of the campaign, we did not observe any noticeable impact from the new callout. However, during the subsequent 10 weeks, as more visitors came to the page, we were able to validate the test for multiple visitor segments and achieve a directional lift for all traffic.
We believe that this greater lift was the result of the improved clarity of WHAT a donor's gift would do, in this case, provide a "bible a day" to those in need. Clarity is one of the key factors determining the strength of an appeal's value proposition.  The greater our clarity in describing the need for and impact from a donor's gift, the greater our conversion rate should be.
Since we did not achieve validity during the short duration of the campaign, we want to test this approach again on a donation page that will get more consistent traffic and have a larger sample size.

experiments.title: How adding tabs to an acquisition donation form affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding tabs to a donation form for the users who downloaded the offer will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2021 - 01/30/2022
For Luther Seminary’s acquisition offer, Reconnect: The Way Forward, we wanted to test the tabbed donation form on their instant donation page in order to increase the donor conversion rate. For this experiment, we kept all steps of the funnel identical in each test with the exception of changing the donation form to have a one-time donation tab and a monthly donation tab. In doing this, we removed the friction in the standard donation form that included asking the donor to check a box to make the gift recurring and asking if this donation was a change to the monthly gift they already provide.
This ebook was offered through paid Facebook advertising and the housefile email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although this experiment did not reach the 95% level of confidence standard to be valid, we saw a directional lift in conversion rate on the tabbed donation form. We believe this happened because we removed the friction within the form causing the overall donation experience to be shorter and more organized for the donor.

This concept can be tested on any acquisition donation page or main donation page, and we believe this will increase the donor conversion rate.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 58.9% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 220.2% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a premium on a donation page impacted donor conversion rates for Hoover Institution
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for a digital audience will achieve an increased average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2021 - 03/04/2022
Hoover Institution has a quarterly publication called the Hoover Digest, which is a compilation of recent articles from Hoover scholars. A donor can receive all ‘subscription’ to all 4 publications by making a donation over $100. In this experiment, we tested whether adding the premium offer to a donation page from an ebook offer impacted donor conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of the Hoover Digest premium drove down conversion rates, but had a large increase in average gift (a lift of 78%). Since the Digest has many articles from different scholars, it might not appeal to as wide an audience as those coming through an ebook funnel. However, the increase in average gift did show that, in this experiment, adding the premium did increase the average gift.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to a donation page impacted donor conversion rates for Hoover Institution
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for a digital audience will achieve increase the average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2021 - 03/09/2022
Hoover Institution has a quarterly publication called the Hoover Digest, which is a compilation of recent articles from Hoover scholars. A donor can receive a ‘subscription’ to all 4 publications by making a donation over $100. In this experiment, we tested whether adding the premium offer to a donation page from an ebook offer impacted donor conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of the Digest premium to this ebook instant donation page led to a decrease in donor conversion rates. This could be attributed to the general nature of the Digest--it is a publication with many scholarly articles, which could appeal only to a limited subset of the audience downloading an eBook.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of a gift amount impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will communicating the impact each gift amount can have increase donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 01/27/2021
Care Net uses a traditional approach to their gift array buttons. This traditional approach simply has the gift amount on them. In an effort to increase donor conversion and, ultimately, overall revenue, we hypothesized that adding the impact of each specific gift amount might help motivate more people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By changing the way the gift array were presented and adding the impact of each specific amount on the button, we saw no difference in donor conversion. That said, we saw a directional lift in overall revenue by 22.8%! Additional here are some other observations from other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 77.0% increase in revenue with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 186.0% increase in revenue with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How making an ask for a specific program impacted donation page results – Part II
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a program-specific value proposition for free online course takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2022 - 03/06/2022
As we looked to increase donor conversion for free online courses, we wanted to find what messaging would most resonate with donors. The existing donation page standard copy was centered on the value proposition of getting the free course into the hands of more people. We wanted to test if transitioning the value proposition to a more specific outcome would motivate donation page visitors. We developed the first treatment to focus on using their gift to promote free online courses to pastors to supplement their training and meet this specific need/problem that exists. We developed a second treatment that highlighted current efforts to translate the free online courses into additional languages to expand their reach, which will require additional funding to build and promote. We wanted to find out if either of these treatments would increase donor conversion by addressing the needs at hand the organization is trying to meet and communicating a specific impact that the donor’s gift would be supporting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the split test across all 3 versions for one month on the donation page. What we found was the first treatment that emphasized pastoral training was decreasing results by 58% with 82% level of confidence. On the second treatment however, we learned that the translation of courses seemed to be lifting donor conversion and at the end of the experiment resulted in a 26% directional increase but only had 36% level of confidence. Because we saw similar results on two additional online course donation pages that were running concurrently, we made the decision to roll out the second treatment as the new control for this donation page experience. We will continue to find opportunities to optimize this copy in future testing. Further analysis on individual transactions will be needed to analyze and uncover the cause for the decrease in average gift and revenue.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 82.4% decrease with a 98% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 70.4% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 54.7% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 4.9% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 35.7% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How making an ask for a specific program impacted donation page results
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a program-specific value proposition for free online course takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2022 - 03/06/2022
As we looked to increase donor conversion for free online courses, we wanted to find what messaging would most resonate with donors. The existing donation page standard copy was centered on the value proposition of getting the free course into the hands of more people. We wanted to test if transitioning the value proposition to a more specific outcome would motivate donation page visitors. We developed the first treatment to focus on using their gift to promote free online courses to pastors to supplement their training and meet this specific need/problem that exists. We developed a second treatment that highlighted current efforts to translate the free online courses into additional languages to expand their reach, which will require additional funding to build and promote. We wanted to find out if either of these treatments would increase donor conversion by addressing the needs at hand the organization is trying to meet and communicating a specific impact that the donor’s gift would be supporting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the split test across all 3 versions for one month on the donation page. What we found was the first treatment that emphasized pastoral training was mostly flat and saw a 1% decrease with 2.98% level of confidence. On the second treatment however, we learned that the translation of courses seemed to be lifting donor conversion and at the end of the experiment resulted in a 68% directional increase with 89% level of confidence. Because we saw similar results on two additional online course donation pages that were running concurrently, we made the decision to roll out the second treatment as the new control for this donation page experience. We will continue to find opportunities to optimize this copy in future testing.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Paid Social visitors had a 106.9% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.New visitors had a 101.8% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 154.0% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 400.0% increase in donations with a 90% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How moving the placement of the premium affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the placement of the premium for prospective donros will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2022 - 03/06/2022
RTB was running a direct appeal ad centered around a premium. In order to increase donor conversion rate we wanted to move the placement of the premium image. Having it above the fold can possibly inspire more conversions as people see what they’re getting in exchange for their donation
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we assumed that changing the placement of the book would increase donor conversion, it seems as if the control was the preferable placement. While the experiment did not validate, we did see a directional decrease in donor conversion rate. However, the traction on this offer was slow (only 3 donations to account for), so we recommend retesting this concept on a page with more traffic.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a matching gift announcement impacted donor conversion rate for non-donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that announcing a matching gift for non-donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2022 - 02/06/2022
A generous donor made a matching gift available to the Canadian Taxpayer Federation so that they could specifically inspire non-donors to become first-time donors.
They wanted to know how a matching gift announcement for non-donors who just signed a petition would react and whether or not the matching gift would increase giving for this audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With an 83.3% level of confidence, we observed a decrease in donor conversion rate of -28.7% for non-donors on acquisition donation forms.
Other observations observed in this experiment include:
Tablet visitors had an 88.8% decrease in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 67.4% decrease in revenue with a 93% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that this message is perhaps too long and not targeted enough. Therefore, we will be re-building this experiment to try another treatment that meets these needs.

experiments.title: How communicating how many more donors are needed in a campaign to meet your goal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the number of target donors for year-end campaign recipients will achieve improve motivation to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/01/2022
As a part of the 2021 year end fundraising campaign, the Leadership Institute wanted to experiment with the final email of the campaign to see what would motivate donors to make their gift before the deadline.
Throughout the campaign, they had been communicating to donors that they needed a certain number of donors to make gifts at a particular gift level. But, they wondered if changing the number of donors on the final email would have any impact on driving gifts before the midnight deadline.
We theorized the following variations for the corresponding reasons:
Control: This variant simply repeats the goal number of donors from the email from earlier in the day.
Treatment 1:  This variant implies that we achieved the goal number of donors from the email earlier in the day, but we wanted to get X more (e.g. “we want to do more!” messaging).
Treatment 2: This variant tells the reader that they could be the last donor that is needed to achieve the goal.
Would any of these variants drive motivation to give to a higher rate and lift overall results?
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although there are no validated results, we observed two outcomes that were of interest:
The "5 More" (treatment 1) variant actually directionally decreased results. Could this mean that communicating that we've hit and are now attempting to surpass the campaign goal is de-motivating for donors? Future experimentation with this messaging type may need to be paired with an additional value proposition as to what additional gifts secured would lead to in order to see if this motivation type can work in the future to lift results. And,The "One More" (treatment 2) variant did not achieve a reliable lift in donor conversion rate. Our team assumed that this would significantly lift response rates, but although there is a slight lift (+18.6%), the confidence score is so low (39.6%) that assuming this kind of lift will be reproduced in a future experiment should be considered unreliable.
Further experimentation is required with these variant types. It would be advisable to carry this motivation messaging sequence throughout multiple emails to ensure that the gift samples are high enough to get a reliable reading in the future.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted revenue per donor
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest contribution for existing donors will achieve increase overall response and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2021 - 12/02/2021
As part of their Giving Tuesday campaign, Care Net wanted to test a relatively new concept known as the downgraded ask. For this experiment, they created a chart of standards based upon the donors’ previous highest contribution where they would ask for a certain number of donors to give a specific amount. For the treatment version of this ask, they asked for a level below what the donor had previously given and added language acknowledging that they were being asked for less.
This was then tested across both emails that were sent out on Giving Tuesday and results were determined based upon all online giving (excluding recurring gifts) during Giving Tuesday and the day following.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we nearly doubled the revenue from the campaign, we did not statistically validate the experiment due to an 88.6% level of confidence. With that said, this is certainly encouraging. When analyzing the individual gift, we did not see a decrease in overall donor average gift. Instead, we saw increases in the number of lower dollar donors and the higher dollar donors ($250+) maintained their previous level of giving.

It would be worth iterating on this concept but to validate this concept with the Care Net donor file, we will need to expand the length of this test for several email during a high urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How suggested gift amounts affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does suggesting gift amounts based upon previous giving increase the revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2015 - 06/22/2015
As part of their fiscal year end campaign, Dallas Theological Seminary wanted to find a way to lower the number of decisions donors had to make when giving their gift. The idea was the suggest a gift amount based upon their previous giving history with the hypothesis that this would lower the friction of the giving form.
To test this hypothesis, we set up an experiment to split traffic between their standard blank gift amount field and a form that would populate three different buttons based upon their last gift amount (this was passed in via the query string).
experiments.keyLearnings:
By suggesting a gift amount, the overall donation page conversion dropped by 54.9%. This was a surprising result given the success of this type of experiment with other research partners.
This outcome taught us something significant about DTS donors. The average gift for their online donors is significantly higher than the most other nonprofits.  As a result, the suggested gift amounts were far larger than would normally be seen on a donation page.  It is our hypothesis that these larger gift options scared away potential donors to the campaign by presuming too much.  We get better results when we let the donor determine their own level of generosity.

experiments.title: Does including a lapsed donor’s last gift information increase donor reactivation?
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does simply including a lapsed donor’s last gift date gently nudge them to give again?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/13/2020
During their Fall Campaign, Oblates wanted to reach out to lapsed donors who had an active email. In order to increase reactivation, we tried a test that has proven successful with other organizations. One version was the same copy as the other campaigns. The test version included the last gift date and last gift amount for each lapsed donor. The purpose was to remind them that it had been some time since they last gave. We only included donors who had not given in the last 5 years. This was tested over a series of two emails, one final day campaign email and then a forward of that email from the primary signer’s secretary.
experiments.keyLearnings: Including their giving amount does seem to have prompted the lapsed donors to give again. However, as there are only three gifts in this experiment, we recommend further testing and perhaps with donors that a deeper lapsed.

experiments.title: How digital brand reinforcement impacts overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing brand ads to the direct mail audience have a positive impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2018 - 03/31/2018
Buckner International has recently made a significant effort to align their messaging across their various communication channels. What this means at a practical level is that the topic discussed in the direct mail letter will align with have several blog posts and what is being discussed on social media. The idea is that the more a constituent hears about a problem, the more likely they will be to response with a gift.
However, not all donors to Buckner follow them on social media or visit their website. In an effort to boost their brand reach, we decided to test showing brand ads that aligned to the current month’s message to those receiving direct mail pieces. We split the direct mail audience in half and showed 50% of their donors their brand ads via Facebook. By splitting their audience in half, we will then be able to analyze the impact of these ads
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach a 95% level of confidence, we did see a directional boost of 23% in donor conversion for those that were shown the brand ads. However, since these brand ads have a cost associated with them and we did not achieve statistically significant results, this is a test we will want to repeat with a new data set to further validate the concept.

experiments.title: How adding a “Get a Recording” option affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “Get a Recording” option to the registration page lead to greater signups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2020 - 02/11/2020
We have many folks who sign up for our webinars and then immediately let us know they can’t actually make it to the live event. We always send out a recording, so there’s not problem. But we wondered if giving them an option on the page to get the recording could increase signups. Our hypothesis was that there could be many people choosing to not register because they can’t make the time work – and there’s no indication on the page that there will be a recording available.
The treatment placed a “Get the Recording” button in the time and date bar. The thought was that this is the key point in which someone would be deciding if they can make it or not. The button opened a lightbox with a dedicated form to sign up for the recording.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the recording option led to an 8.6% decrease in registrations. However, it did not reach a desired level of confidence of 95%. We also looked at webinar attendee rate and playback based on which treatment they signed up from, and the rates are nearly identical. So with the potential for the recording button to decrease registrations – and making no impact on attendance or playback rate – we will not offer the recording option in this format.
It’s possible that the option to get a recording was placed too far away from the form itself. A future test idea would be to make the recording an option within the registration form as that could be the key point in which someone is deciding whether or not they can make the live event.

experiments.title: How placement of an email capture before a quiz improve email registration rates for the Economic Freedom Quiz

rows.name: read the petition now - email capture last
read the petition now - email capture first

experiments.researchQuestion: Does an email registration form at the beginning of the Economic Opportunity Quiz improve email registration rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2019 - 04/06/2019
When we launched the Economic Freedom Quiz, we wanted to improve our email registration rate. When reviewing the user experience, the email capture was originally placed at the end of the quiz, so we wanted to move it to before the quiz started to see if we could improve email registration rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 92.1% level of confidence, we improved the email registration rate by 71.7%.
Our original thought was that the anticipation of finding out your score would lead to a higher rate, which is why it was originally launched at the end of the quiz, however it appears that the quiz takers after clicking would be more willing to give their email address to start the quiz.

experiments.title: How a more emotional message did not make a difference in acquiring new emails
experiments.researchQuestion: Would focusing on what you get from the book increase the email registration rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2019 - 05/28/2019
When launching the new Heritage Foundation eBook offer, we wanted to test a message that was more focused on communicating to the end-user, instead of focusing on the positioning of the contents of the eBook itself to see which treatment would generate more email addresses.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we did not see a noticeable difference only achieving a -2.5% decrease in email registration rate, with an 86.2% level of confidence.
It would appear that at least with this offer, it will not matter if we position the contents of the eBook itself or what you will get from the content as a reader as it pertains to the email registration rate.

experiments.title: How increasing the copy on the page affects acquisition rate

rows.name: Highlights the Content of the eBook

experiments.researchQuestion: Adding an in-depth explanation the contents of the eBook will allow the offer to appeal to more visitors and will, in turn, increase the conversion rate of the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/13/2014 - 11/20/2014
Texas State Historical Association was offering one of their more highly valued publications, the Texas Almanac, as a free digital download. All visitors had to do to was enter their email address to receive the eBook.  With the level of traffic their website receives on a daily basis, we were acquiring large volumes of visitors to the page.
Previous tests had proven that a landing page with less copy did not convert as well as the current control.  We theorized that if we could add additional content to the page to further sell the contents and the value of the eBook, we could increase the number of sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We knew that adding the additional content to the page would increase the friction of the page by forcing visitors to scroll further down to find the form.  Our hypothesis was that the additional content would increase the value in the visitors mind which would overcome the additional friction.  This proved to be incorrect.
The additional content actually decreased conversion rate by 6.3%.  While this is not a large drop, it did prove that we needed to find a different method for communicating the value of the Almanac in order to increase the conversion rate of the page.

experiments.title: How adding visual clarity affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which image produces the most emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/07/2015 - 08/13/2015
Previous experiments had proven that one of the best methods of email acquisition for the Texas State Historical Associate was through free content offers. Utilizing their vast library of Texas history information, they started creating eBooks by bundling popular content. Their first eBook, The Battle of the Alamo was getting a great email acquisition rate but we wanted to see if there was still room to improve.
One of the recurring themes in emails to the organization was that they were confused about the offer they were receiving. They believed they were getting a hardbound version of the book instead of the digital format. We hypothesized that by changing out the visual from an actual book to the book on an iPad, we would be able to increase the clarity of the offer. This would, in turn, lead to increased conversion and decreased complaints.
experiments.keyLearnings: The iPad image of the book statistically increased the number of emails acquired by 3.5%. While not a large increase, this bump in conversion showed that the clarity of the offer does impact the results. In this case, however, the image itself was not as critical to the conversion process as other elements of the page which is why the increase was so minimal.

experiments.title: How promoting impact over facts affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will explaining why the offer is important (impact) versus describing what the offer is (facts) increase the email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2015 - 11/19/2015
The Texas State Historical Association was promoting their latest email acquisition offer which was an eBook featuring an overview of the history of Texas music. The typical approach to promoting these eBooks had been to highlight the details of the offer including the type of content, the media used, and other selling features of the offer.
We had a hypothesis that if we focused less on the facts of the eBook and more on the reason why the content was so important, we could improve the overall email acquisition rate.  We created a new treatment to test this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings: The landing page that focused less on the facts of the book and more on why the book content was so important was able to lift the email acquisition rate by 8.6%. This reiteratives that the “what” behind the offer is less important than the “why.”

experiments.title: How removing an ad’s premium offer affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: What banner will motivate website visitors to become a member during a calendar year end campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2015 - 01/04/2016
The Texas State Historical Association, like many other nonprofits, utilized a banner across their site during their year end campaign to promote an opportunity to give. Historically, all of the banners on TSHA’s sites had been utilized to promote premiums such as eBooks so it made logical sense to promote the premium associated with becoming a member (the Texas Almanac).
However, we realized this premium may not appeal to everyone so we decided to create a treatment that would test out a purely philanthropic ask on the banners.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We discovered that the Almanac premium proved to be a significant motivator for donors.  There was no statistically significant difference in the amount of traffic driven by the ad but those that were driven by the premium offer were 52.6% more likely to become a member.
Another interesting aspect of this experiment revolves around the average gift amount of the two audiences. While not statistically significant, the average gift of the philanthropically driven donors was over double that of the premium driven donors. It is worth revisiting this experiment in future campaigns to see if this trend holds.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of membership levels affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which gift array will result in the greatest number of new members?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2015 - 01/04/2016
During their calendar year end campaign, the Texas State Historical Association created a landing page dedicated to acquiring new members. Their membership program consists of 6 different levels ranging from $25 up to $1,000.  After analyzing previous year end campaigns, we realized that the number of new members that select gifts above $250 are few and far between.
Since few people select them, we had the hypothesis that these upper membership levels may actually be decreasing conversion due to the amount of mental friction that they cause.  We decided to test a form that only had four membership levels to see if we could improve the overall conversion of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment revealed that the donation form with four levels of membership actually decreased conversion by 17.1%. Our hypothesis is that these additional membership levels actually provided clarity around the benefits of each level which were mentioned in the text above the form. Knowing which levels offered which benefits allowed the new members to feel safer in their selection of membership.

experiments.title: How increasing the explanation of benefits on a membership form affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does clarifying the member benefits and adding the option to defer tangible benefits impact the conversion rate of a donation form?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2016 - 03/16/2016
The Texas State Historical Association was in the process of updating their membership levels to reflect new benefits. We also wanted to add an option to opt out of receiving the tangible benefits of membership for those members that did not want them. This was something that could help save costs but did add additional decision friction to the process.
Before this was fully rolled out, we wanted to get an understanding of how this would impact donor conversion. We decided to test the new language and form on a donor acquisition campaign to gauge its impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: While it did not reach statistical validity, this test indicated that adding more description of the benefits near the benefit selection increased conversion rate. We would need to validate these learnings with a larger sample size but it does seem to indicate that this additional language added perceived value to the donors’ minds.

experiments.title: Ask in Email vs No Ask in Email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a direct ask in the email result in a lower number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2016 - 02/04/2016
One of the best practices that we had been following was that we should NOT ask for money directly in the e appeals. We wanted to test that best practice with our constituents.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned the opposite to be true.  In fact, we saw a significant lift by asking directly in the email.  We will continue to test this assumption in the next few appeals.

experiments.title: New Donation Form vs Old Donation Form
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a shorter donation form give us more donors than our standard long form?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2016 - 03/28/2016
We are looking for ways to increase value force, while decreasing cost force on our donation forms. We did this by creating a much simpler form for users based on donation forms we have been seeing. We hoped it would decrease friction and anxiety.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this case, we learned that donors were less likely to donate. We will continue to analyze the cost force and see if we can decrease the cost as well as increase the value force to drive donations.
It is possible that we decreased anxiety, but did not do a good enough job increasing value force.
Also, originally this test reached confidence, but after a few more days, there was not a clear confidence for the original form.

experiments.title: Web Donation Form (form elements)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will compressing the form elements increase conversion?
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment tells us that our donors prefer form elements to be one per line.

experiments.title: How the way a name acquisition offer is presented impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which acquisition feature would acquire more names?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2017 - 02/19/2017
Live Action developed a petition for people to sign to Defund Planned Parenthood. With the recent political climate and conversation around this topic, they wanted to really push this petition and did so through multiple channels. One of those channels was their website. They had a pop-down feature at the top of their site that asked people if they wanted to sign the petition. They hypothesized that they might be able to get more signatures if they had a pop-up modal on their site. They developed a treatment with this modal and tested it against the pop-down feature.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The pop-up modal increased petition signers by 11%. While the results did not reach the 95% validity that we would like to reach, it did reach 91% which is enough for us to draw insights and push out the treatment as the winning version.
The increase in petition signers and names with the pop-up modal can be attributed to it being more disruptive to the end-user’s experience on the page than the pop-down feature. This disruption puts the offer right in front of the person with no other elements buying for their attention.
Additional testing is recommended for this name acquisition opportunity to see if we can increase the conversion rate even more drastically.

experiments.title: OE Test – July 2017 – John 15:5 Appeal I
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a change in the outer envelope improve donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/15/2019
What Was Tested
We changed the outer envelope from a photograph of a church to a photograph of praying hands resting on a bible. The OE Teaser and all other kit components remained the same.

experiments.title: OE/Message Test – July 2014 – KYFAH Appeal I
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a new OE and slightly different appeal outperform the control?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2019
What Was Tested
We changed the Outer Envelope from a photo of Obama, the ACLU, and the Bible with a red X through it which said: What’s behind the unprecedented attacks on your religious freedom … and what you can do about it!
To an OE with a photo of a door that says “Official Notice: Keep your faith at home – or else” with a teaser of: A message to Christians from the ACLU and the Obama Administration.
We also varied the text in the appeal slightly to focus more on the ACLU Triple Threat.
