experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a significant decrease in ROI due to a decrease in average gift, response, and an increase in costs.
It is not recommended to use this kit.

experiments.title: How the delivery method for a content offer impacts name conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase name conversion by changing the delivery method to be more mobile friendly?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2018 - 01/03/2019
Boys Town offers an eNewsletter on their homepage and with the significant mobile traffic on their homepage we hypothesized that we might be able to increase name conversion/sign-ups by changing the delivery method of the offer to be more mobile friendly and also test adding stronger value proposition copy for the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see directional results with a decrease in names acquired from both treatments. This was a bit surprising to us. With the large mobile audience on Boys Town’s website, a slide up offer is one of the best mechanisms to deliver content to people. Our hypothesis in the results of this experiment is that while the delivery mechanism may have been the most effective one, the offer itself wasn’t compelling or appealing enough for people to actually want it when they saw it.

experiments.title: How removing fields that aren’t necessary on an acquisition form impacts name conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a simplified form increase name conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2018 - 06/08/2018
The Missionary Oblates offer a free online course called Celebrating Mercy. They hypothesized that simplifying the acquisition form might increase name conversion for the course. The control had additional fields on it that we hypothesized weren’t necessary. To test this, we streamlined the form and removed the address fields (address, city, state, zip code). All other elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: By simplifying the form we increase name conversion by 8%. This experiment didn’t validate, but the results we were seeing were directional with a 87.5% confidence. The address fields aren’t required for a person to take the course. These additional fields were creating friction to the conversion process and by removing them we were able to capture more people and get them enrolled in the course.

experiments.title: How the headline on a popup affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a headline that reinforces the value proposition of the offer convert more people?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2018
Heartlight Ministries was offering a free two-week online course through an exit intent popup on their website. The control popup focused the headline on getting the free course, instead of focusing on the value proposition of the course. The remainder of the copy and design stayed the same, but the headline was tested.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the test was not statistically valid, the value-focused headline treatment did produce a directional 9% lift in conversions. More testing is necessary, but this test suggests that focusing on the free-ness of the offer without explaining the value behind it produces a decrease in conversion, and that clarifying the value is key to them caring about the free course.

experiments.title: How removing a call-to-action link and asking for a reply affects registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking for a reply lead to greater webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if we could further optimize our resend email that we use in promoting our monthly webinars. For the control, we used the template we had previously tested into. For the treatment, we wondered if an even more personal call-to-action would increase registrations. So rather than insert a CTA link like a marketing email would, we simply asked if they wanted to get a recording of the webinar – and waited for a reply.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we didn’t reach our desired 95% level of confidence, this experiment certainly was approaching validity with a 24.8% increase in webinar registrations for the treatment. This further confirms our overarching hypothesis that people are more likely to engage and respond to personal email than something they view as marketing.
In order for this method to scale, we’ll have to find a way to more efficiently get people signed up who reply to our emails.

experiments.title: How matching the visual congruence of the initial ad affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying and visually matching the original add improve email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2016 - 02/28/2016
As part of the launch and promotion of Hillsdale College’s latest free online course that studied the life and work of CS Lewis, we created an email capture page based upon what we had learned from prior experiments. This page utilized a design and copy similar to past online courses.
However, after reviewing the entire process, we realized that the default email capture form may cause a bit of disorientation for visitors coming from the ad. The ad (shown below) had a completely different look and feel than the landing page.
We had the hypothesis that if we can improve the visual congruence of the page and align the headline better to the call-to-action of the ad, we can increase the number of emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not a exponential lift, we did find that by visually aligning the design with the original ad we were able to increase email capture rate by 3.9%. This is a concept that will be valuable to utilize in future email acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How leveraging the power of the unknown affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does it improve conversion to suggest that the visitor has “never heard of” Imprimis?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2015 - 02/17/2015
Hillsdale College launched a Facebook campaign to grow the subscriber list for Imprimis, their renowned speech digest newsletter. Salon Magazine called Imprimis “the most influential conservative publication you’ve never heard of”, which Hillsdale wanted to use in the copy. Within that quote, there are two claims of value: that Imprimis is “the most influential conservative publication” and that the user has “never heard of” it. We decided to test the first claim independently and then measure the impact of adding the second claim to it.
experiments.keyLearnings: Leveraging the power of the unknown had a significant impact on landing page conversion. Overall conversion rate still leaves something to be desired (to be improved through further testing), but we know that adding an element of perceived mystery to the value proposition increases conversion.

experiments.title: How a shortcut to the acquisition form impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a shortcut to the bottom of the email acquisition page increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2017 - 10/26/2017
The conversion rate for Econ 101 had historically remained around 25% for visitors to the page. This indicated that the visitors had a high motivation. We had the hypothesis that if we could make it easier to convert, we could acquire more email addresses. On mobile especially, the length of the page was a point of friction. We decided to add in navigation bar that would allow the visitor to jump to the bottom of the page to see if eliminating friction this way would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this situation, the addition of a “smooth scrolling” option that jumped visitors to the bottom of the page, decreased email acquisition by 8.3%. While this did not reach a level of statistical significance, it did not seem like it would be a positive impact in either case so we decided to move on to the next experiment. There are multiple reasons why this treatment may not have worked:
The jump to the bottom skipped past the value proposition copy which decreased their likelihood to convert.
The length of the page was not a significant point of friction to start with which meant this change did not fix an actual existing problem.
The thick underline created a visual divider between the headline and copy that interrupted the visual flow down the page.
Visitors did not understand that the arrow was an actual shortcut on the page.
Regardless of the reason, this test would indicate that the “shortcut” to the bottom does not increase the chances of conversion.

experiments.title: How a related background image impacts email acquisition rate on a course signup page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the presence of a related background image increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2017 - 10/26/2017
Hillsdale College was looking for new ways to optimize their online course signup page for ‘Great Books 102.’ They wondered: Will the presence of a related background image increase email acquisition rate? They launched a treatment page that contained the same image used in their Facebook promotion and tracked the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale College found that the presence of a related background image did not increase email acquisition rate on their ‘Great Books 102’ course signup page. Even though the imagery selected for the background does match the image used in the Facebook advertisement, it’s clear that visitors were not significantly affected by the addition of the background image. Therefore, it did not reduce anxiety like it was intended to do.
Further tests are being conducted to determine if any kind of background image is effective in reducing anxiety.

experiments.title: How targeting an appeal to high “D” DISC profiles impact response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will targeting a high “D” style email and landing page at the high “D” constituents increase overall response rate
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2017 - 07/28/2017
For the first email in the 2017 Heritage Board Challenge, we wanted to use the DISC profiles that we were able to append to subscriber and donor records. We created the first email and landing page for the campaign without worrying about the DISC profile of the writing. Once we had the approved copy, we then rewrote the same appeal in the style of the segment that the copy did not address. In this case, it was a high “D”.
We then split the constituents based upon their primary DISC profile and their donor status. We also made sure the split was evenly divided along highest previous giving levels so that one would not be “better” donors than the other.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The high “D” style email and landing page decreased the overall response rate across all profiles by 21% at a 87% level of confidence. When we focused in on just the high “D” segment, we saw a more pronounced decrease. While it did not reach a statistical level of confidence, it would indicate that this particular appeal was not able to better speak to high “D” profiles.
While this test was not successful, it is just the first step in validating the approach. There could be many reasons why the appeal did not work (such as selecting the wrong aspect of the appeal, i.e. the Board match). We will want to continue to validate this approach to reach a high level of confidence on its impact.
As a note, the control was an “Sc” email and an “Ic” landing page. The treatment was a “Ds” email and a “Di” landing page.

experiments.title: How addressing High-I subscriber in their style of communication impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will speaking to subscribers in their preferred DISC style increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2017 - 08/11/2017
During The Heritage Foundation’s 2017 Board Match campaign, we decided to test the style of communication to see if we could better align the messaging style with the subscribers’ known DISC scores. Using the data that we appended from the CrystalKnows website, we segmented the subscriber list by both donor status and primary disc style. We then took the Control version of the email and landing page (written in the I style) and recreated it in the style of a high C.
The goal of this experiment was to see if we could increase the giving of a high C subscriber. However, this write up is based upon the trends we observed when analyzing the high I subscribers of this same test.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment had no significant impact on the high “C” subscriber base which was the target of the experiment. However, after reviewing the data, we discovered that the high “C” landing page and email adversely impacted the giving of the high “I” subscriber base. While we did not hit the 95% level of confidence we strive for, it was quickly approaching statistical validity.

experiments.title: How the copy tone of an ad impacts conversion.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad would acquire more email addresses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2017 - 04/18/2017
The Missionary Oblates offered a special Easter Novena to people through paid Facebook ads. They wanted to test the motivation of the end-user by testing the tone of the copy and how they presented the offer. The control ad presented the offer early on in the copy and had a more uplifting tone with an invitation to get the offer. The treatment talked about the importance of Easter and encouraged people to get the Novena.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test didn’t quite validate and there was only a slight difference between the control and the treatment. The initial data showed the ad with a more uplifting tone that presented the offer earlier in the copy had a 8% increase in acquisition. We recommend that a more radical test be done using some of these same concepts from the winning variant in order to validate the experiment in the future.

experiments.title: How the total number of questions in a quiz affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a shorter quiz increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/13/2017 - 12/08/2017
This year, Illinois Policy Institute has exposed a serious problem in the state of Illinois. It’s been more than 15 years since the Illinois General Assembly has passed a balanced budget, and lawmakers have turned to tax hikes in order to ease the deficit. However, this is not a long-term solution and it’s caused illinoisans to pay some of the highest taxes in the nation. Illinois Policy developed a plan to balance the budget and ease taxes, all at once. In order to get the word out, they developed a 9-question quiz followed by a tax petition that would build awareness among Illinoisans and encourage them to take action to advance this plan. Before launching, Illinois Policy Institute wondered: Will a shorter, 6-question quiz increase petition signatures?
They created a treatment version and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Illinois Policy Institute found that the shorter quiz did not produce a valid increase or decrease in email acquisition. While these results aren’t statistically significant, there is an important takeaway. We’ve seen a correlation in other testing that shows the longer time commitment an individual makes in consuming an offer, the higher their instant and long-term donor conversion rate is. Since their is no significant difference between email acquisition rate in this test, it’s possible that we can increase donor conversion by continuing to offer the longer quiz (i.e. the one that requires more of a time commitment). The next step for this test sequence is to test an even longer version of the quiz to see if we can impact donor conversion without hurting email acquisition.

experiments.title: How reducing specificity affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simpler headline clarify the value proposition and increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2015 - 11/24/2015
The Hoover Institution launched a campaign to grow the subscriber base of the Hoover Daily Report, their flagship policy newsletter. They drafted landing page copy that highlighted the authority of their research fellows (who provide content for the Daily Report), but wanted to test a much simpler value proposition inspired by another email newsletter. They hypothesized that this new simple headline would increase email acquisition rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though it was only a slight lift, there was enough difference to achieve statistical validity. The simpler headline produced a 6% lift in email conversion — which tells us that we don’t always have to be selling. Many times, our prospects can be attracted to subscribe by clarity rather than perceived authority.

experiments.title: How match language on a popup affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does match language on a popup increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2017 - 12/19/2017
FamilyLife was running their year-end campaign and wanted to test match language on their website pop-up versus no match language.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment did not validate, but saw a trend towards a 19% increase in gifts with match language on the popup. People were more likely to give when they know that their gift will be matched and make a greater impact.

experiments.title: How registration form length affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing the perceived length of the form increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2016 - 06/27/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary had just released their third free online course on Genesis. Although they had made plenty of changes to the landing page copy, they hadn’t tried to optimize the form, which asked for both online and offline information, totaling 11 fields. We hypothesized that reducing the perceived length of the form (by nesting fields together) would increase conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: The optimized form produced a 4.1% increase in signups. This slight increase suggested that the form didn’t have as much impact on the page as originally thought, and prompted a series of new tests to increase the force of the value proposition in the copy.

experiments.title: How a more personal copy treatment affects conversion rate on an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a more personal copy treatment affect conversion rate on an acquisition offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2017 - 08/17/2017
The treatment copy produced an over 50% increase in conversion rate. This is likely due to the more personal copy that resonated with the Facebook ad’s personal tone, creating a more cohesive pathway for a more naturally emotionally-charged offer on the affects of poverty on our children’s worldviews.
experiments.keyLearnings: Buckner International was running a name acquisition campaign tied to a free ebook offer. They wanted to test if more personal, emotionally-charged body copy would affect conversion rate on the offer landing page. We also removed a scripture reference and a bolded portion of copy from the body. All other elements remained the same.

experiments.title: How asking for additional information on an acquisition page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does asking for additional information on an acquisition page decrease conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2017 - 10/16/2017
Buckner International wanted to retrieve zip code information from new people coming onto the email file via their content offer, 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Child in your Life. They wanted to see if adding a required zip code field would significantly decrease conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not validate- which in this case, was what they wanted. The less validation of the experiment, the better- since it did not have a statistically significant decrease, they could continue to ask for zip codes and get more data with little to no effect on conversion.

experiments.title: How a direct ask via voicemail impacts donor giving behaviors
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a direct ask in a voicemail increase the the donor’s likelihood to give?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2017 - 12/05/2017
For Giving Tuesday, Buckner International wanted to test out a new channel of communication to see if we could boost donor giving. We utilized a service named SlyBroadcast that allows us to make straight to voicemail phone calls. It is a way of making a phone call to thousands of participants that would immediately show up as a missed phone call on their phone. While it is a recorded message, it would appear to be a genuine voicemail given the nature of the technology.
We had previously used SlyBroadcast for cultivation and stewardship messages to donors. This time around, we wanted to make a direct ask and request that donors go online or call back to make their Giving Tuesday gift.
We focused our audience on those that had both a phone number and email address on file. We then split this audience in half by their highest previous contribution levels to create equal segments so that we could create a treatment and control group that would not be skewed by past giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically significant, we did see a 28% decrease in the likelihood of someone that could have received the phone call to make a gift (the call had a ~60% deliverability rate so not everyone in the treatment group would have actually received the call). Given the low level of confidence, we would not go so far as to say that the phone call necessarily hurt the chances of a gift but it definitely did not increase the likelihood. Based upon these results, we will be reserving SlyBroadcast calls for cultivation and stewardship only.

experiments.title: How a soft call-to-action affected a social media audience’s conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding value proposition language to the calls-to-action at the bottom of the page increase the likelihood of a visitor giving their email or making a gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2015 - 11/02/2015
The Alliance Defending Freedom blog receives high volumes of traffic each day, largely from social media. We wanted to find ways to motivate this audience to take one of two actions: either subscribe to the newsletter or make a donation.
In a previous experiment, we discovered that just placing the calls-to-action onto the blog posts wasn’t enough to convince people to take a specific action. For our next iteration of this test, we decided to try testing out adding some value proposition language around the desired actions. We also softened the buttons so that they would communicate value to the visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: This new treatment was able to succeed where the last experiment failed. We were able to acquire email addresses by implementing a name acquisition device on the page with accompanying value proposition language. That said, a 0.04% email conversion rate is certainly not breaking any records but it gives us a new starting point. This will allow us to continue to iterate and improve the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How a product-level acquisition offer affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer will result in the highest email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2016 - 02/15/2016
In an effort to convert their existing blog traffic, Alliance Defending Freedom has been experimenting with different email acquisition methods and offers. The existing offer promotes a variety of email publications the visitor will receive and unites them under the ADF’s primary value proposition of defending religious freedom.
We had the hypothesis that if we focused the offer around something more tangible, a digital subscription to Faith and Justice Magazine, that the offer would be more appealing to visitors. This same incentive is offered with the current control but it does not describe the details of the magazine or offer a visual.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Faith and Justice offer resulted in a 37% decrease in emails acquired. This would lead us to believe the individual offer of Faith and Justice, while more tangible, was not as attractive of the ultimate goal of the audience which was learning more about their religious freedoms.  The more general offer did a better job of communicating this value proposition.

experiments.title: How the tone of the call-to-action impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which headline will have the greatest email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2016 - 06/06/2016
Alliance Defending Freedom was launching a campaign in light of the recent controversy caused by the North Carolina bathroom bill. As part of this campaign, the landing page contained a statement of belief that encouraged visitors to submit their name and email.
The initial call-to-action that was found right above the form was “Help Keep Women and Children Safe.” We had the hypothesis that this headline may be unintentionally causing friction in the visitors’ minds. With the way it is worded, it infers that the signer will have to do work by “helping”. The statement below it also did not address how adding their signature would impact women and children.
We decided to run a test with a headline that would more closely align with the copy found below the headline.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new headline led to a 15.6% increase to the number of names and emails acquired. By removing the first word, “Help,” from the headline, we took the emphasis off of what the signer had to do and more closely aligned the headline to the statement of belief that followed it.

experiments.title: How a soft call-to-action in the email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call to action will drive more traffic which will, in turn, garner more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2014 - 12/19/2014
In the second email of Heritage’s year end campaign, they wanted to find our which messaging stance appeals most to donors and drives the most donations.  They tried three variations: a direct ask, soft call-to-action around the organization, and a soft call-to-action focused on the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: The emails with softer calls-to-action were able to increase the number of clicks more than 91%, however, the traffic was not as motivated to give when compared to those who were asked to make a gift within the email.   As a result, the email making a direct ask was able to convert 50% more donors than the other treatments.

experiments.title: How a Rotating Exit Intent Offer Impacts Desktop Email Acquisition Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing people a different offer on each visit increase the chances of email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2017 - 06/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom knows that their blog attracts significant amounts of organic traffic. Keeping these visitors engaged is a big opportunity and major step forward in their donor journey. After running a series of tests, they found that using an exit intent pop-up as a final attempt to engage users before leaving a blog page increased email acquisition rate. Though they have seen an increase in engagement and conversion rates since adopting these pop-ups, they wondered if users were weary of seeing the same exit-intent messaging on every visit. To test their hypothesis, they decided to introduce a series of four rotating exit-intent offers. This would provide a new offer on the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th visit.
experiments.keyLearnings: Alliance Defending Freedom noticed that the series of rotating offers appeared to decrease email acquisition rates over the course of one month. However, this test was not statistically valid. Their findings must, consequently, remain anecdotal. This test may suggest that four different offers reduced the familiarity, and therefore the clarity, of the exit intent’s true value proposition–to keep exiting visitor up-to-date on news regarding religious liberty. Further tests will be conducted to discover the answer to this question.

experiments.title: How hard versus. soft calls to action affect click rates in prospecting emails

rows.name: Activate your free course now CTA

experiments.researchQuestion: Does a hard or a soft call to action drive higher click through rates in a prospecting email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2016 - 08/25/2016
Hillsdale College has had great results with prospecting to third party email lists for their Online Course offerings.  Building on recent learnings from Facebook advertising, we decided to test whether a soft or hard call to action would deliver a higher click rate.
The soft version used “Learn More” as the call to action.  The treatment version used a more direct “Activate your free course now” call to action. In Facebook testing the “Learn More” version had driven higher click rates. The hypothesis on that test was that people aren’t fully comfortable committing to an action from a Facebook ad, but would be willing to take a low-commitment action to learn more.
We wanted to see if this same hypothesis would hold true on a prospecting email when the reader has more information before deciding whether to click through. This test was carried out by an A/B split mailing with one of our prospecting partners.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As expected, the softer CTA “Learn more” drove slightly higher click rates. However, an unintended result of the test is that the Learn More version got a 27% lower conversion rate on the landing page.
We were originally planning to focus our learnings on CTR. But since there was not a significant difference (3.2% difference) we decided to focus on landing page conversion rate because that is where we saw the biggest distinction in results.
By clarifying the call to action in the email, recipients had about the same likelihood of taking the next step of clicking.  But when they arrived at the landing page they were much more motivated to complete the final action of enrolling in the course.

experiments.title: How membership benefits affects conversion for new members
experiments.researchQuestion: How does membership benefits impact the conversion rate on the potential new member landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/08/2016 - 01/20/2016
As part of their January membership campaign, The Heritage Foundation wanted to determine what factors were the biggest motivators for new members. Every member receives a handful of benefits such as newsletters, a membership card, and important updates. We wanted to find out if, by describing these benefits on the page, we could increase the perceived value of the membership.  We decided to create a treatment that identified and explained each of these benefits in detail to potential new members. Once finished, this treatment ended up doubling the length of the page with the added copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The membership benefits increased the conversion rate on the page by 20.2%. We had concerns that the added copy would increase the friction on the page more than it would increase the perceived value of the membership. This experiment proved this was not the case.
Interestingly, we performed this same experiment for existing members and it showed no statistical impact when we added the benefits. In that situation, the existing members didn’t need to be reminded about the benefits they received since the got them on a regular basis.

experiments.title: How cognitive tension and the incentive of “free instant access” affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the promise of “instant access” increase conversion when tested against a call to action of “get the facts”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2015 - 11/24/2015
The Heritage Foundation’s team of research scholars assembles an Index of Military Strength each year to assess the readiness of America’s military and the strength of existing global threats. The team at Heritage promoted this on Facebook to acquire new email addresses. Their existing landing page made the offer to “get the facts”, which had previously been a compelling incentive. They developed a treatment that leveraged proven keywords “get instant access”.
This was coupled with a new headline. Instead of aligning the offer to “get the facts”, the treatment headline asked a question: “Is the US Military ready to defend our country?” This was designed to draw the reader into the copy and introduce tension that could be answered by activating the offer.
The only variables tested on this treatment were the headline and call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment increased conversion by more than 20%. This tells us two things:
1. The use of cognitive tension can be a useful element to increase conversion from prospects and visitors.
2. The concept of “instant free access” can increase incentive to convert, if it is combined with an offer that is desirable to our prospects and visitors.

experiments.title: How the right message affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging approach will produce the greatest number of donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2015 - 08/14/2015
As part of a capital campaign to raise funds for a new building, The Heritage Foundation wanted to be sure they were utilizing the most powerful value proposition available to them. Historically, fundraising messages had always been about what Heritage does each day to research and promote conservative values in Washington. This building campaign offered an opportunity to try a different tactic that might be even more appealing to donors.
We created a treatment that promoted the benefits of the new building. This approach allowed us to talk about more tangible aspects such as proximity to the Capital and having a larger foot print.  We then launched the campaign and split traffic between the two options.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By talking about the specific benefits of the building, we were able to increase donor conversion by 21.2%. This is what is known as product-level value proposition. The control talks about The Heritage Foundation at a high level but this specific appeal had tangible elements that were appealing to the donors. The already understand the primary value proposition of Heritage so the treatment just reinforced this specific need.
By taking this approach, we were able to validate the messaging approach before the final days of the campaign. This allowed us to find the ideal message before the bulk of the gifts would come in.

experiments.title: How leveraging the power of the unknown affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does it improve conversion to suggest that the visitor has “never heard of” Imprimis?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2015 - 02/17/2015
Hillsdale College launched a Facebook campaign to grow the subscriber list for Imprimis, their renowned speech digest newsletter. Salon Magazine called Imprimis “the most influential conservative publication you’ve never heard of”, which Hillsdale wanted to use in the copy. Within that quote, there are two claims of value: that Imprimis is “the most influential conservative publication” and that the user has “never heard of” it. We decided to test the first claim independently and then measure the impact of adding the second claim to it.
experiments.keyLearnings: Leveraging the power of the unknown had a significant impact on landing page conversion. Overall conversion rate still leaves something to be desired (to be improved through further testing), but we know that adding an element of perceived mystery to the value proposition increases conversion.

experiments.title: How a soft call-to-action in the email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call to action will drive more traffic which will, in turn, garner more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2014 - 12/19/2014
In the second email of Heritage’s year end campaign, they wanted to find our which messaging stance appeals most to donors and drives the most donations.  They tried three variations: a direct ask, soft call-to-action around the organization, and a soft call-to-action focused on the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: The emails with softer calls-to-action were able to increase the number of clicks more than 91%, however, the traffic was not as motivated to give when compared to those who were asked to make a gift within the email.   As a result, the email making a direct ask was able to convert 50% more donors than the other treatments.

experiments.title: Does adding a donor goal countdown on a slide-up increase engagement and donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the dynamic donor goal in the campaign lower third for Moody Bible Institute homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and downstream donor conversion because it shows visible progress toward a shared goal, which creates urgency and social proof—signaling that others are giving and that each gift meaningfully moves the mission forward right now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2025 - 12/31/2025
During Moody Bible Institute’s Year-End Campaign, we tested whether adding a donor goal to the website’s lower third would increase clicks and downstream donor conversion. The lower-third goal was calculated using the ministry’s overall revenue goal and average gift amount, then dynamically counted down as gifts were completed through the donation form.
The test ran for the full duration of the campaign, with traffic split evenly between the control.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dynamic donor goal to the lower third on the Moody Bible Institute homepage showed mixed—but directionally interesting—results. While the treatment group experienced a 9.7% decrease in lower-third clicks compared to the control (42.5% level of confidence), it also saw a 5.5% increase in completed transactions (14.5% level of confidence). Although neither result reached statistical significance, the pattern suggests that the donor goal did not encourage more users to click the lower third, but it may have helped motivate those who did engage to follow through with a donation. This suggests the donor goal may be influencing donor intent and commitment rather than top-of-funnel interactions. Future testing should prioritize larger sample sizes to increase statistical confidence and yield clearer conclusions. In addition, testing alternative donor goal presentations may reveal more effective ways to communicate progress and reinforce donor motivation. While the findings are inconclusive, the directional lift in transactions suggests the donor goal has potential to strengthen conversion and merits further experimentation to better understand its impact.

experiments.title: How Using Heritage Member Survey Insights to Reframe Homepage Hero Messaging Affects Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reframing the homepage hero message using Heritage member survey insights to emphasize permanence, preventing reversal, and turning wins into law will increase donor conversion rates because it aligns the first on-site message donors see with their strongest motivations—fear of lost momentum and a desire for durable, enforceable outcomes—making the case for giving feel more concrete and urgent.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2025 - 01/05/2026
This experiment was ran during The Heritage Foundation’s calendar year-end fundraising campaign, when homepage traffic and donor intent were elevated across all channels. At the same time, Heritage had just gathered extensive qualitative feedback from its members revealing deep anxiety about recent conservative wins being reversed by courts, bureaucrats, and future elections. Year-end urgency was already present, but the research suggested donors were especially motivated by the idea of permanence—turning temporary victories into lasting law before political momentum fades.
The test was conducted in the homepage hero block on Heritage.org, specifically within the primary text block layered over the homepage hero image. This placement ensured maximum visibility, as the homepage serves as the front door for email, direct, and organic traffic during year-end. Because this block is often the first message visitors see, it plays a critical role in framing both Heritage’s mission and the purpose of giving during high-traffic moments.
The control homepage message emphasized broad values (“Stand for Conservative Values”) and a generic year-end match framing, which aligned with traditional nonprofit appeals but did not reflect the sharper emotional drivers uncovered in the member survey. Survey respondents expressed frustration with symbolic wins and fear that the Left would undo progress unless reforms were codified quickly and aggressively.
This experiment was designed to test whether shifting from abstract values to concrete outcomes—explicitly naming permanence, lawmaking, and dismantling the deep state—would make the homepage appeal feel more urgent, specific, and aligned with donor expectations. The treatment directly translated member language and priorities into the hero message, positioning a year-end gift as a tool to prevent reversal and secure long-term change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The main takeaway from this experiment is that changing the homepage hero messaging did not meaningfully affect overall conversion rates or revenue in a statistically valid way. Conversion rate held steady at 2.4% across control and treatment, and while the treatment showed a lower CTA click rate, none of these differences reached statistical significance. Although the treatment produced a higher average gift and higher revenue per visitor directionally, the experiment did not reach the required sample size or confidence threshold, so these results cannot be treated as conclusive.
Moving forward, this test reinforces that homepage hero copy changes alone are unlikely to drive measurable performance shifts during high-traffic year-end periods, when donor behavior is heavily influenced by prior intent from email and direct channels. We will use this learning to treat homepage hero messaging as a brand- and framing-level lever rather than a primary conversion lever, and continue applying survey-informed language in contexts where it can be paired with clearer calls to action, tighter message-to-donation-page alignment, and tests that can reach validity more efficiently.

experiments.title: Static image vs interactive giving element for driving donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a static image with an interactive gift impact slider for prospective donors  will achieve increased conversions because  showing tangible, real-time impact activates the principle of concreteness and gives donors a sense of agency and efficacy in their giving..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2025 - 09/02/2025
Bible League Canada (BLC) uses story-driven donation pages as part of its digital campaigns, such as DA7 “Transforming Asia,” to engage donors and invite them to place Bibles into the hands of new believers. These campaigns typically pair narrative content with strong visual imagery to create an emotional connection and drive giving.
To test whether making the donor’s impact more tangible would increase conversions, we replaced a static image on the donation page with an interactive slider showing how many Bibles a gift would provide.
Our Control used a static sidebar image of a woman with the handwritten-style caption: “A Bible for every believer.” The Treatment swapped the image for Fundraise Up’s gift array impact slider, which dynamically displayed the number of Bibles placed at each gift level (e.g., $80 = 16 Bibles). The slider was designed to give donors a sense of real-time, visible impact.
The test ran on their Transforming Asia Campaign landing page with a 50/50 traffic split. It reached primarily new donors from facebook advertising, and some warm leads from email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment version using the impact slider intuitively offered donors a clearer picture of what their gift would achieve, the test saw lower conversion performance than the control.
Due to the total sample size falling below the required threshold (3,969) and confidence not reaching 95%, the results are not statistically valid. However, directional insights are still worth reflecting on.
This suggests that, in this context, the emotional storytelling and human imagery may have been more effective at motivating giving than a purely impact-driven interface. One possible interpretation is that the slider introduced a more transactional or analytical tone, which may have weakened the emotional connection that the static image helped to establish.
Additionally, since the campaign itself already emphasized tangible impact in the copy, the slider may have been redundant rather than additive. Future tests could explore hybrid approaches that retain emotional imagery while layering in impact messaging—ensuring the donor’s experience remains both heart-driven and outcome-aware.

experiments.title: How splitting the sticky slide-out donation ask into two steps impacts donor conversion on FamilyLife’s website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that splitting the donation ask into two steps will increase conversion rates. By first asking whether FamilyLife has provided value and then following up with a donation request, we reduce cognitive load and create a smoother, less overwhelming user experience, which will lead to higher engagement and more conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/26/2025
FamilyLife, a nonprofit dedicated to strengthening families through biblically-based resources, uses a persistent sticky donation slide-out on article pages to encourage giving. The Control (current version) features a single message asking users if FamilyLife had provided them with $25 worth of valuable resources and, if so, invites them to donate. Users can select “Yes” to proceed to the donation page or “No” to close the slide-out.
We previously tested this dual-ask approach during FamilyLife’s year-end campaign, where the Treatment led to a 50.9% increase in conversions but did not reach statistical significance, achieving only a 68.9% level of confidence. Despite this, the results were promising enough that FamilyLife chose to implement it for the remainder of the campaign due to the urgency of year-end giving. Given the directional improvement, we wanted to retest this approach in a non-urgent campaign setting to further evaluate its impact.
In this experiment, we tested a modified version (Treatment) that split the question into two steps. The first step only asked if FamilyLife had provided $25 worth of value. If users clicked “Yes,” they were then shown the donation request. If they clicked “No,” the slide-out closed immediately.
We hypothesized that this two-step approach would improve conversion rates by reducing cognitive load, making the initial interaction feel less transactional, and increasing engagement before presenting the donation ask. The experiment was structured as an A/B test with a 50/50 traffic split between the Control (current sticky donate button) and the Treatment (split-question version), measuring whether this adjustment led to higher donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 30 days. The Treatment (dual ask) led to a 65% increase in donations compared to the Control (single ask). However, the result reached a 77% level of confidence, meaning it did not fully validate at the typical 95% threshold but showed promising potential.

The Control (standard sticky slide-out) generated 9 transactions, while the Treatment (dual ask) resulted in 15 transactions.

Similar to the previous test conducted during FamilyLife’s high-urgency calendar year-end campaign, we believe the dual ask improved conversions by reducing cognitive overload. By first asking a low-effort question about the value of FamilyLife’s resources, users were more inclined to engage. Those who clicked “Yes” then encountered the donation request in a way that felt more natural and less transactional. The additional step may have also given users more time to process the ask, leading to a higher likelihood of conversion.

Given the positive directional impact across multiple tests, we recommend retesting the dual ask in future campaigns to further validate its effect on conversions. Additionally, there are opportunities to explore other optimizations for the sticky donate button, such as presenting a content offer or premium or updating the copy to enhance the value proposition.

experiments.title: How does adding an image of the book in the May Challenge Grant banner impact donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an image of the book premium in the May Challenge Grant banner image for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donations because it provides a stronger visual of what the donor would receive with their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2025 - 05/27/2025
During Time of Grace’s May Challenge Grant, we hypothesized that adding an image of the book/premium that donors would receive with their gift would increase the donor conversion rate versus a more general background and call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of the book premium in the May Challenge Grant banner on the homepage led to a 10.5% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 40.9%, which is below the 95% threshold needed for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the banner image with the book premium to see if the results can be replicated and validated. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or any external events that may have impacted donor behavior.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is essential to conduct further testing to validate the impact of adding an image of the book premium on donor conversion rates before making any definitive conclusions or changes to future campaigns.

experiments.title: How does setting the comment box to “Active” impact visitor comments on posts?

rows.name: Focus Comment Box when View All clicked

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that setting the comment box to “Active” for visitors, after they click “View All Comments” will achieve an increase in comments because it eliminates an extra step and point of friction—allowing the user to begin typing immediately..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/09/2025 - 07/11/2025
A key metric for CaringBridge is increasing Visitor comments on an Author’s post. In the current logic, when a user clicks “View All Comments” on a user’s post, they still have to click into the comment box again to make it “Active,” ensuring they can begin typing and post a reply. We hypothesize that eliminating this extra step, updating the comment box so that a Visitor can immediately begin typing after they click “View All” will increase comments.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved setting the comment box to "Active" for visitors after they click "View All Comments," resulted in a 1.6% decrease in comments for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 69.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid, and the experiment did not provide conclusive evidence to support the hypothesis. The primary takeaway from this experiment is that it made no difference to the status quo.

experiments.title: How moving the tribute widget in the Updates Tab affects tribute donations

rows.name: Relocate Tribute Widget in Updates Tab

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the tribute widget in the updates tab for profile visitors will achieve an increase in tribute donations because moving the tribute widget would reduce the confusion of the widget appearing as a paywall before the posts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2025 - 06/17/2025
For this CaringBridge experiment, we wanted to move the tribute widget in the updates tab to be below the comments to see how that would affect tribute donations. This was similar to an experiment we had done for the single post page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the tribute widget in the updates tab below the comments resulted in a 21.6% increase in Donations - Tribute for All Traffic. This indicates that the change had a positive impact on donation conversions.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it might be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period to gather more data and improve the statistical validity of the findings.

Overall, while the initial results suggest that moving the tribute widget may have a positive effect on donations, further testing is needed to confirm these findings. It is important to approach experimentation with a careful and analytical mindset, considering both statistical significance and practical significance when evaluating the results.

experiments.title: The Big 5 Personality Traits effect on email fundraising
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a fundraising email for conservatives who are low in openness and high in conscientiousness will increase donor response.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/13/2025
During our sustainer invite campaign we tested the use of AI in writing fundraising copy for conservatives using 2 of the 5 Big Personality Traits. Copilot was asked to “Rewrite this fundraising email as if it was going to be sent to a conservative who is low in openness and high in conscientiousness according to the big 5 personality traits. ” The prompt did not include any ask to improve the fundraising appeal, which may have been the reason for the average results. As other testing has shown that prompting AI to rewrite an email to increase the likely-hood someone would donate has increased donations.
Here is the control:

Tony asked me to reach out to let you know that the mid-campaign deadline for our Leaders Club membership drive is rapidly approaching.
When you activate/upgrade your Leaders Club membership today, your increased monthly gift will help protect America’s founding principles, increase prosperity for American families, and stop the leftist establishment’s sabotage of President Trump.
The sooner you act, the greater chance we have of defeating the radical Left and ushering in America’s Golden Age.
Activate/Upgrade your Leaders Club membership before the mid-campaign deadline using the secure link below.

Virginia Haggerty
Assistant Director, Membership

Here is the treatment:

Subject: Deadline approaching/ Tony asked that I follow up with you

Dear <First Name>,
Tony asked me to reach out to inform you that the mid-campaign deadline for our Leaders Club membership drive is rapidly approaching.
