experiments.title: How does adding a dear reader on high traffic pages affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dear reader for high traffic pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the likelihood to donate when presented with with an opportunity after receiving content that is of high value is easier.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2024 - 04/10/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion by adding an easy conversion opportunity right on the page.
We ran this experiment with Today in the Word, a daily devotional ministry. We only targeted the pages that contained the daily devotionals. These pages get a ton of traffic each day.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a donation opportunity directly on high traffic pages can lead to an increase in donor conversion rates.

2. The treatment group saw a significant 12.1% increase in transactions for all traffic.

3. The statistical level of confidence was only 55.1%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for validity.

4. These results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

For future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 85% in order to draw reliable conclusions. It may be beneficial to further test the impact of adding donation opportunities on high traffic pages with a larger sample size or different variations to see if the results can be replicated and validated. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or any external events that may have impacted donor behavior.

experiments.title: Will Adding 5 Things You Should Know to the 6 Steps to Share Your Faith Giving Page Increase Donor Conversion Because the Case for Support is Easier to Understand?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that arranging the case for support language in 5 easy points for people who signed up to receive 6 Steps to Share Your Faith will achieve increased completed transactions because the copy on the page is arranged in an easy to follow list and conveys 5 important points about the eBook they are about to enjoy and receive value from.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2024 - 04/03/2024
KCBI’s donor conversion on it’s acquisition pages could see some improvement across all it’s offers. The team wanted to know — as seen with other clients — will arranging the case for support language on the giving page in 5 easy points to understand help convert more eBook sign-ups to become donors?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that arranging the case for support language in 5 easy points on the giving page for 6 Steps to Share Your Faith led to a 33.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 57.6%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%.

While the results suggest that the treatment had a positive impact on donor conversion, it is not statistically significant. We're going to retest this treatment on an offer that has higher traffic to see if we can get a valide result.

Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other methods of arranging the case for support language or testing different variations to see which one resonates best with potential donors. Regular experimentation and testing can help KCBI identify effective strategies for improving donor conversion on its acquisition pages.

experiments.title: How adding a dear reader to a daily devotional page impacted donation conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dear reader style ask for the daily devotion pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we are adding a ask to pages that have high traffic and could be motivated to give because the are already receiving valuable content. because  we are adding a ask to pages that have high traffic and could be motivated to give because the are already receiving valuable content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2023 - 12/11/2023
The Today in the Word Daily Devotion Pages receive a ton of traffic daily. We wanted to see if we could get some of this traffic to convert into being a donor by presenting a ask at the end of the devotion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By presenting an ask at the end of the content we were able to see a 364% lift in conversion at a 100% level of confidence.
This experiment was a test of nothing vs. something (adding an ask), further testing can be done to see if we can increase the donor conversion or average gift by presenting the ask differently.

experiments.title: How adding autonomy a wiki banner affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding autonomy language to the Wikipedia CTA will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we are giving the choice to the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/02/2024
By adding an opportunity for FOTF article readers to determine whether they wanted to participate in the Sustainer campaign – giving them an opportunity to say “Yes, I want to strengthen our nation’s families” or “No, I’m not interested today” — would help inspire more readers to interact with the campaign Wikipedia buttons and insight more donors to give during the sustainer campaign. By adding this language approach, will we see higher donor conversion with this campaign treatment (Wikipedia campaign ad) we’ve seen do well in the past?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding autonomy language to the Wikipedia CTA did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a 5.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, with a statistical level of confidence of 25.0%. However, since the confidence level is below the standard threshold of 85%, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing different approaches to see what resonates best with donors. It's possible that the autonomy language did not have the desired effect in this particular context, but that doesn't mean it won't be effective in other scenarios. By conducting further tests and analyzing the results with a higher level of statistical confidence, you can gather more reliable insights to inform future campaigns and strategies.

experiments.title: How increasing the perceived security on a campaign donation page impacts donation conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of security for the year end campaign donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it is providing clarity of what the donor’s information is being used for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2023 - 01/03/2024
The Ashbrook Center ran a fundraising campaign during Calendar Year End. For this campaign, we wanted to see how increasing the perceived security of the donation page through value proposition impacted overall donor conversions.
For this test, we added a line of copy near the “Donate” button on the form that said, “We take data and privacy seriously. Your payment information is secure and will be used only to process your gift.” We also made the background of this section gray and added a lock icon to drive the secure message home.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a statement of security to the donation page had a positive impact on donor conversion rates. The treatment group, which saw the added line of copy, experienced an 11.7% increase in One-Time Donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence remained below the accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that the results may not be valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to conduct longer tests or gather a larger sample size to increase the statistical power and achieve a higher level of confidence. This will ensure more reliable and valid results. Additionally, it would be helpful to consider testing different variations of the security statement or explore other aspects that could increase donor trust and conversion rates, such as emphasizing the organization's track record of data protection or providing testimonials from previous donors.

experiments.title: How increasing the perceived security on a campaign donation page impacts donation conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of security for the year end campaign donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it is providing clarity of what the donor’s information is being used for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2023 - 01/04/2024
Luther Seminary ran a fundraising campaign during Calendar Year End. For this campaign, we wanted to see how increasing the perceived security of the donation page through value proposition impacted overall donor conversions.
For this test, we added a line of copy below the “Donate” button on the form that said, “We take data and privacy seriously. Your payment information is secure and will be used only to process your gift.” We also made the background of this section gray and added a lock icon to drive the secure message home.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a statement of security to the donation page for the year-end campaign resulted in a 14.2% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.5%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to increase the sample size and run the experiment for a longer duration to obtain more reliable results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test other variations of the value proposition statement to further improve donor conversion rates. This could involve testing different wording, design elements, or positioning of the security message. By continuing to experiment and refine the messaging and design of the donation page, it is possible to achieve a higher level of donor conversion and improve the overall success of fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: Does mentioned a previous text message impact conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing the language mentioning a text message impact conversions?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/27/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
A few weeks ago we tested how an email following a text message impacted over all conversions, https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/6675.
As a follow up to this test we wanted to test the opening paragraph of the follow up email. Does the mention of the text message received impact conversions or is it just the presence of the follow up email with the same offer?
In the previous experiment above we did mention the text message therefore this test is removing the messaging to see if conversions are impacted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the language mentioning a text message did not have a significant impact on conversions. Although the treatment saw a 3.3% increase in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 10.8%, which is below the threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

Therefore, it is important not to draw any definitive conclusions from this experiment. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with larger sample sizes or different variations to validate the results and gain more reliable insights. Additionally, it would be useful to explore other factors that could potentially impact conversions, such as the content of the follow-up email or the timing of the communication.

experiments.title: How does adding a donor statement for lapsed donors impact donor conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor statement for lapsed donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because they will not have to read the entire email to see the last time they made a gift..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 06/30/2023
Pathway to Victory is the international ministry for First Baptist Dallas. They have 2 major match campaigns throughout the year-one during December, the other in June.
For this June match campaign, we wanted to test whether including a donor ‘statement’ at the top of the email, versus an in-line last gift date, would make an impact on lapsed donor recapture. This was tested across all 5 emails in this campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a donor statement at the top of the email might have a positive impact on lapsed donor recapture. The treatment group, which received emails with a donor statement, saw a 6.5% increase in donations compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 30.8%, which is below the desired threshold of 85%. Therefore, these results should not be considered valid enough to make future decisions based solely on them.

We also received some feedback from potential donors on the statement, which considering the low level of confidence, means we need to refine and re-do this test for future campaigns.

Additionally, it could be beneficial to further refine the donor statement and test other variables, such as the wording or placement of the statement, to optimize its effectiveness.

Overall, this experiment provides some indication that including a donor statement may be a promising approach for lapsed donor recapture, but further research and testing is needed to confirm its effectiveness.

experiments.title: How donor-centric language impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donor-centric value proposition for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the value proposition and reasons to give will pertain to them on their impact rather than the organization’s.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2023 - 07/02/2023
DTS’ Genesis course offer is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. When user’s sign up for the Genesis online course, they are redirected to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to give. We want to see if we can get more people to give after signing up for the course.
We’ve developed a treatment which focuses especially on the donor. It includes much “you” language and focuses on the donor’s perspective and what they can help accomplish.
This differs from the control page, which uses much organization-centric language – “we” language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 8 days, the treatment produced a 45.1% increase in donor conversion rate with a 43% level of confidence. It also produced a 30.6% lift in revenue with a 65% level of confidence. Unfortunately, external factors prevented this experiment from running any longer.
Despite the limitations, this promising trend may prove this to be a experiment worth running for your organization.
The treatment was trending in the right direction, but before we can roll it out, we'll need to test it for a longer period of time.

experiments.title: How aligning with a donor’s beliefs impacted conversion on a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing donor alignment and donor autonomy for post-survey donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2022 - 03/27/2022
We were running a survey to the housefile and wanted to test the concept of introducing donor alignment and donor autonomy in treatment copy that would improve donor conversion. The control copy outlines the problem, gives urgency, and shares how the donor’s gift is a solution to the problem. But for the treatment, we developed a version that asks the donor to align themselves with specific beliefs in an attempt to get them to “nod their head” as they read along or give the opportunity for a step by step “micro yes” at each new paragraph on the page. We pose TGC’s beliefs and then ask the donor “Do you believe this as well?” We also ask a question that feeds off the momentum of the survey they just responded to: “If you believe TGC has made a positive impact on your faith and spiritual growth, would you consider giving back today?” Throughout the survey, the readers had a chance to tell us just how much value they had personally received from TGC resources. The donation page treatment introduces an opportunity for them to provide that same value to others. We open the donation page with a conversational question, “In today’s noise world what source can you trust for truth” as opposed to our control copy which leads with a statement “The digital world is a challenging place, full of unhelpful influences.” Rather than talking AT our donors, we made additional effort to talk TO them, and bring them into being a part of the mental conversation on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed was a 14.3% increase in conversion rate on the treatment with a 49.4% level of confidence so the test results are invalid. We did notice some directional increases and decreases for various audience segments: Returning visitors had a 40.0% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence. Email visitors had a 29.2% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence. 65+ visitors had an 18.5% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence. Finally, mobile visitors had a 67.7% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence. Because the confidence level for overall traffic is so low, we would need to re-run this test with more overt changes to be able to discern any true impact on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How shortening the number of fields for a ‘plan your visit’ form impacted conversion rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing form fields for a prospective visitor will achieve  a higher sign-up rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2022 - 03/31/2022
First Baptist Dallas has a ‘plan your visit’ form where they ask prospective visitors for Sunday’s church services a series of questions. The form had multiple required entries; we wanted to test whether splitting the form into two parts drove a higher conversion rate.
In our treatment version, the first part of the form just had basic questions (name, email), while the second part then asked the rest of the demographic questions that were present on the control form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the number of email signups was the same, the 2-step form had a slightly higher conversion rate than the control version, with a 10% increase in email acquisition.
Since filling out the form was not a requirement for attending church services on Sunday, the number of form fills was not enough to fully validate the experiment. Moreover, there was confusion on the back end with the 2-step form, as they were getting duplicate submissions (one with the name/email, one with the rest of the information asked).
For our next test of this, we will test streamlining the back-end process, as well as making the form fill a required part of church attendance. We would also test incentivizing form fills by offering a premium.

experiments.title: Does adding a security box around payment fields on a donation page affect revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a security box around the payment information section of the donation form for donation page visitors will achieve greater revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2021 - 10/30/2021
On RTB’s main donation page, we thought that adding a secure symbol and a security box around the payment information part of the donation page would encourage page visitors to give and to give more. While transactions to this page are already secure, this visual representation can encourage more donations as prospective donors feel like their payment information is safe with the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a lengthy period of time, the results still did not validate. We saw a 14% decrease in revenue with a level of confidence of 65%.
The security box around the payment information portion of the donation form resulted in lower revenue/visitor and lower average gift. The appeal of a "secure" checkout did not encourage donors to give more.
One can conclude that RTB donors already trust the organization and adding a secure box did not persuasde or dissuade them enough to give.
An additional test idea would be to add a security box around the entire donation form. Perhaps if donation page visitors saw the secure symbol earlier in the process they would be encouraged to give and to give more.

experiments.title: How optimizing for a specific purchase event affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing for a specific non-sponsorship action for Lamplighters prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2021 - 07/14/2021
Prison Fellowship International was promoting their Lamplighters donation opportunity and had their Facebook ads optimizing for the typical “purchase” conversion, which had proven to work better than an “email acquisition conversion” or Facebook’s default “donation” conversion.
Since they had sponsorships ($39/month gift) and purchases (one-time gift or recurring gift of any size) both reporting as purchases, they were curious to learn if optimizing for a more specific, non-sponsorship purchase would improve conversion.
They set up an ad set optimizing for non-sponsorship purchases and ran it against one optimizing for all purchases using Facebook’s experiment function.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was no difference in conversion rate between the two optimizations. However, Facebook revealed that it was more efficient to optimize for non-sponsor gifts.
See the Facebook results here: https://capture.dropbox.com/y02dzi7qbfEGhhGE

experiments.title: How reducing the amount of information required on an event registration form impacted conversion rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing form fill fields for event attendees will achieve a higher conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2022 - 04/02/2022
First Baptist Dallas was very excited to announce that Rebecca St. James would be the Worship Resident Artist for their Day One worship service. As part of the landing page providing details on the service, First Baptist included a form that had multiple questions. We wanted to test whether moving most of the questions to a 2nd page would impact form conversion rates. For the treatment, we just asked for first/last/email before directing them to a second form that asked the additional questions included in the control version. Our hypothesis was that by reducing the form-fill friction, prospective attendees would be more likely to fill out the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Reducing the number of fields that a website visitor had to fill out actually reduced the conversion rate by 11% for overall traffic. However, there were not enough conversions to provide a valid test result. Moreover, having two separate forms created some confusion for the staff that was managing entries, as they were receiving double form submission (one with the first/last/email, one with the rest of the information) for the treatment version.
We believe there was a decline in conversion rates as the people potentially attending the Day One service were used to filling out multiple fields from other events, as well as wanted to provide as much information as possible up front. Moreover, since filling out the form was not a requirement to attend, it was easy for potential attendees to just ignore it.
We will continue to test this concept, with future tests focusing on a reduction of friction and labor on the back-end, as well as making it clear what filling out the form would actually achieve.

experiments.title: How donor-centric language on a survey confirmation page can lead to increased giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that copy on a survey confirmation page that connects more deeply with the survey participants will lead to increased giving when presented the opportunity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2021 - 01/04/2022
The team at I Am Second ran a calendar year end survey to it’s email file. The survey is a common strategy used during high urgency campaigns, particularly during CYE, getting people to agree and affirm the mission of the organization. After successfully completing a survey, the survey recipient is taken to a confirmation landing page, where they are thanked for their responses, reminded of the mission of the org, and then presented with an opportunity to make a gift.
The team wanted to test language that was LESS about the organization, and MORE about a connection with the donor. The only changes to the treatment were the headline, body copy, and a more succinct CTA. The bullet points that detail how the gift will be used remained the same as the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the experiment did not validate due to a smaller than required sample size, and the level of confidence was below 95%, we did have some pivotal learnings.
While donations from all traffic saw around a ~37% lift with a 48.3% LoC, donations from NEW visitors saw ~ a 107% increase with a 78% level of confidence.
Key Takeaways: By including language that more deeply connects with the potential donor, I Am Second was able to increase their gifts from people that connected with their mission, using momentum to confirm those affirmations, and motivate the donor to take action immediately after survey completion. Also, the doubling of donations on the treatment for the new visitor segment may be a result of all the value prop work the team has been integrating for the new name acquisition file, thus showing that continued cultivation and value re-enforcement can lead to greater financial impact for the org.

experiments.title: How removing a step from the donation process affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a step (a user-initiated click and a secondary form page load) from donation flow for potential Lamplighter donors will improve conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2022 - 04/19/2022
We tested simplifying the donation flow on the main Lamplighters donation page. By including A) the full donation form on the test variant, B) removing a user-initiated click on donation call to action button and C)  removing  a second page load displaying the full form would reduce friction and improve donor conversion rate. Content flow was streamlined from two-columns to a single column, increasing user focus and reinforcing natural vertical page flow for users. Gift arrays within test variants more clearly defined to make it easy for donors to choose between 4 predefined options to support 1, 2, 3 or 4 prisoners. Despite this litany of changes, we did not experience a significant change in donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results for this experiment are inconclusive, with no meaningful change in overall conversion rate. We did see some interesting data across demos and device types, which are detailed below:
Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 334.8% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 64.8% decrease in donations with a 91% level of confidence.18-24 visitors had a 149.9% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
One interesting takeaway here is that the 18-24 audience (a group we currently exclude from our Facebook acquisition marketing efforts) was our best-converting audience with a 4.4% CVR. The second best-converting audience was from the 25-34 age group with a 3% CVR, another audience we are not targeting with our FB ads. We are currently targeting ages 30+ across current Facebook paid social campaigns.

experiments.title: How changing the content offer affects email acquisition for new visitors

rows.name: 5 Steps to a Better Prayer Life

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the content offer on a pop-up for new visitors will achieve a higher email conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2022 - 04/20/2022
In this experiment, I Am Second wanted to see if changing the content offer within a pop-up would result in a higher number of email conversions for the treatment, specifically with new visitors. In the control, the offer was for a free guide about “struggling with the past”. In the treatment, the offer was for a free guide about “improving your prayer life.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, the goal was to see if a different offer could yield greater email acquisition from new visitors. However, the results were inconclusive. The test did not reach statistical significance with too small of a sample size, even though it ran for 2 months. Also, the level of confidence was below 95%, so the test could not validate.

For future tests, we would recommend running a single offer targeted to new users, with a value prop that might resonate with a new visitor…something with more exclusivity and clarity.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to an email subscription form impacts email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to an email subscription form for website visitors will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2022 - 04/17/2022
Reasons to Believe has a form on their homepage to capture emails for their newsletter subscription. In order to increase name acquisition we decided to add clarity to the language on the form. The original form informed people to “sign up for our newsletter” without detailing what value the recipient was getting. We adjusted the language so there was more clarity on what they were getting and at what frequency. We also added first name and last name fields because this information was not being captured in their original form.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this experiment did not validate, we were seeing a 14% decrease in name acquisition with a 76% level of confidence. The experiment had already been running for a month without much difference in metrics. The perceived decrease in name acquisition could be due to the fact that we added additional fields to this form. It may be better to split this out into 2 different experiments (one focused on language and the other focused on fields) in order to isolate the variables appropriately. We could also test into different aspects of the value proposition. We tested into clarity but our next step can be to test into exclusivity.

experiments.title: How using a flat donation page affects conversion for potential sponsors
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that RaiseDonors’ child sponsorship landing pages for potential donors would convert better than PFI’s own child sponsorship pages.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/12/2022
Prison Fellowship International hosts its child sponsorship catalog pages with both RaiseDonors and on its own site at pfi.org. We tested to see if RaiseDonors child sponsorship pages could potentially convert better than existing sponsorship pages on the PFI domain.
Traffic to these pages is driven primarily by Facebook Ads and focused on new direct donor acquisition. To facilitate this test, we duplicated our existing Facebook PFI child sponsorship carousel ads campaign and created a manual carousel ad set to land potential donors on RaiseDonors child pages versus the existing catalog-driven set that lands potential donors on PFI child pages. To summarize, we tested landing users on RaiseDonor’s URL’s versus PFI URL’s with traffic run to experiment as a 50/50 split. All other campaign elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment showed us that, in this case, RaiseDonors child sponsorship pages do not convert better than existing PFI child sponsorship pages. This result helps to confirm that there is not an obvious technical site issue on pfi.org that is currently negatively impacting child sponsorship conversion rates coming from Facebook advertising efforts.

experiments.title: The Impact of Testing Two Different Devotional Offers to Buckner Visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by testing two competing free devotional offers, we will find the one visitors are most likely to convert on and thus acquire more email addresses.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2022 - 04/16/2022
Buckner International uses Dear Readers to promote free content offers like its devotionals. We wanted to see which free devotional offer acquired more emails. To test this, we split the traffic on their article pages and delivered Faith in Action to half of the visitors and  Messages of Peace to the other half.
We hypothesized that visitors would prefer one to the other and that we would increase email acquisition by finding the best resource.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While neither offer had a significant impact email acquisition, the Faith in Action offer increased revenue by 91% with a 96% Level of Confidence for new visitors. (Though the sample size was still lower than what we would want for the most reliable results.)
This shows how testing can result in significant conversion lift in unexpected areas. Further testing is needed to see if this significant increase in revenue and donor conversion is due to something measurably different on the donation page for this offer vs the Messages of Peace devotional donation page, or if it is something inherent in the offer itself. Additional testing should also focus on pages that get enough engagement to have a larger sample size of donations.  But in the meantime, it is clear that Buckner should use the Faith in Action as its dear reader devotional offer.

experiments.title: How reducing friction and anxiety by additional secure features impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting gift security to reduce anxiety for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/11/2022
When looking for ways to reduce friction and anxiety on an instant donation page, we wondered if it would be helpful to have additional indications of the security of the gift at the point of conversion. We added a lock icon directly on the button and set up a 50/50 split test to see how donor conversion would be impacted.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for about 2 weeks, we were only able to observe a 3.1% increase with 28.4% level of confidence so the test results are not valid. We suspect the results are flat because gift security is already indicated below the button so the additional lock icon might not be necessary. Because we did not see the lock icon cause a decrease in giving, we could try an additional iteration of the test that moves the lock icon positioning to be overlaid on the button for the purpose of replacing the lock icon underneath the donate button and accompanying text with an impact statement highlighting the value of their gift that would motivate them to continue in the giving process.

experiments.title: How improving clarity of message affects email acquisition for returning visitors

rows.name: Longer copy w/ clear value articulation

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clear value articulation in the pop-up copy as to why you should join the community will achieve a greater number of sign-ups to the community (email acquisition) for returning visitors to the film pages.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2022 - 04/10/2022
I Am Second has a goal of getting visitors to join their free community portal to gain access to more content, film archives, and access to life coaching. The experiment was set up to show a pop-up to returning visitors of their popular film pages, with the goal of getting them to join the community for greater engagement.
I Am Second wanted to know if adding clearer copy (w/ greater value articulation) to a pop-up shown to returning visitors would result in greater sign-ups to a community “portal”…even if it meant more text.
The experiment consisted of:
a control with a pop up with a shorter – to the point copy
a treatment with longer copy that articulated the “why’s” behind joining the community
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for just over a month, and while the original goal was not statistically valid due to not enough returning visitors to see statistical significance and only a 73.8% Level of Confidence, there were some practical learnings we took away from this test. We did see over a 20% lift in emails acquired on the treatment, and week over week - we saw trends moving upwards to higher LoC's.

While we cannot definitively say that the longer copy with greater value articulation was the only factor to increasing email signups to the community, we can say (based on numerous other similar experiments run for other NextAfter clients) that it would be worth continuing to test clear messaging strategies when trying to get a visitor to take a specific action.

The key to strengthening value proposition includes the elements of appeal, exclusivity, clarity, and credibility. This experiment really focused the value proposition around the first three factors, in an effort to increase the strength of the message and the likelihood that a returning visitor would respond and take action.

As the goal of the experiment was to see if we could increase email sign-ups to the community from returning visitors, a future test idea might be to acknowledge the returning visitor with language like "Welcome back" or "Nice to see you again", etc.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 27.9% increase in email acquisition with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 162.9% increase in email acquisition with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How using an offer that is relevant to the author of a blog affects email acquisition in a dear reader (Pt.3)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an offer in a dear reader that is relevant to the author for readers will achieve a higher email conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2022 - 04/10/2022
Reasons to Believe is a science-faith organization that has a plethora of blog content that receives a lot of traffic. They also have specific scholars/authors who have their own blogs and articles. We wanted to see if using an offer in a dear reader that is specific and relevant to the author will yield a higher email conversion rate. In this particular case we were targeting Ken Samples’ articles. In this experiment we used an evangelism survey as the control and an eBook authored by Ken Samples as the treatment.
All traffic for this experiment is from organic web traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Using the eBook that was related to the author produced no difference in email conversion rate or any other metric. This is inconsistent with the previous experiment's (Hugh Ross' blog/dear reader and Fuz Rana's blog/dear reader) findings.
We hypothesize that this difference in results is due to the difference in audience. Hugh and Fuz's blogs are much more scientific so their readers are more likely to gravitate towards an eBook with scientific content than an evangelism survey. Whereas Ken's blogs are theology/philosophy based so it is likely that this audience will respond to either an evangelism survey or an eBook from Ken.
We are following up with an experiment that uses the ebook dear reader and uses first person language so it sounds like it's from the author himself.

experiments.title: How writing a more detailed headline affected email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that detailing the offer format in the headline for landing page visitors will achieve greater email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2022 - 03/30/2022
In an effort to improve email acquisition on the sign-up page for one of Focus on the Family’s video series, we decided to create a treatment with a more detailed headline that explained the format of the content the visitor would receive when they signed up.
The headline used in the control did an adequate job of explaining the benefit gained by signing up, but it did not specify that the visitor was signing up for a video series. Because previous experiments have shown that specificity can have a positive impact on conversions, and knowing that in 2020 in the US reached 244.4 million people watched digital videos online, we decided to create a treatment headline that included how the benefit would be gained—in the form of a free video series. For this experiment, we ran an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 48 days, we observed a .5% lift in sign-ups. The low sample size of this experiment prevented us from validating our hypothesis that a more detailed headline would increase the number of visitors who signed up for the video series.
Nevertheless, the low lift in sign-ups does indicate that it was the benefit gained from the video series that appealed most to visitors. The details of the offer (free video series) are also mentioned early in the body copy and so this inclusion in the headline may have been redundant.
For now we can conclude that focusing on the problem the visitor wants to solve in the headline is an effective way to add value to an offer.
Next steps may be to further expand on this benefit, hitting on an emotional pain point by including what this benefit (winning inner battle) means for the visitor, ie. Learn how to win the battle with yourself, so that {how winning the inner battle improves your life}.

experiments.title: How shortening the number of fields for a ‘plan your visit’ form impacted conversion rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing form fields for potential visitors will achieve a higher sign-up rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2022 - 03/30/2022
First Baptist Dallas has a ‘plan your visit’ form where they ask prospective visitors for Sunday’s church services a series of questions. The form had multiple required entries; we wanted to test whether splitting the form into two parts drove a higher conversion rate. The first part of the form just had basic questions (name, email), while the second part then asked the rest of the demographic questions that were present on the control form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the number of conversions was relatively close, the 2-step form had a 1.7% higher sign-up rate, with 100% level of confidence. This is a test we will continue to run on different forms throughout the website.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 10.2% decrease in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 38.4% increase in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.Direct visitors had an 11.2% decrease in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 10.7% decrease in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 22.2% decrease in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 16.8% increase in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 0.7% increase in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 0.2% decrease in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 2.5% increase in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 6.4% increase in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 17.9% decrease in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 0.9% increase in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 78.3% decrease in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.65+ visitors had a 22.2% increase in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had a 9.7% increase in pageviews with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed giving form for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2022 - 03/28/2022
When launching a new acquisition offer, we tested a tabbed giving form on the instant donation page. The existing form has a checkbox to make a recurring gift, but we were curious if visually presenting the option to choose between giving one-time or monthly would increase donor conversion. This concept has had varying results in previous tests with other organizations. First, we wanted to verify that this change would not have a negative impact on donor conversion before adding this feature to other donation pages. Secondly, we wanted to see how this would affect recurring donation conversion. All other copy and donation form elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the experiment for one month, but the results were ultimately inconclusive. We saw a 7.4% increase in conversion rate with a 29% level of confidence so the experiment results did not produce valid learning. We did, however, see some interesting observations on the impact of recurring donations. The control had 0 recurring gifts and the treatment saw 3 recurring donations. While this was too small of a sample size of transactions to be considered statistically significant, we will be able to use these results to replicate the experiment on additional acquisition pages knowing that it did not appear to have a negative impact on recurring donations based on the initial test. We did see some specific segments see a decrease in giving on the treatment that we will need to further explore on the next iteration of the test to see if similar tests produce similar results.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 41.9% decrease in donations with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 33.8% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 50.0% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	45-54 visitors had a 14.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	55-64 visitors had a 7.0% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How making an ask for a specific program impacted donation page results – Part III
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a program-specific value proposition for free online course takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2022 - 03/06/2022
As we looked to increase donor conversion for free online courses, we wanted to find what messaging would most resonate with donors. The existing donation page standard copy was centered on the value proposition of getting the free course into the hands of more people. We wanted to test if transitioning the value proposition to a more specific outcome would motivate donation page visitors. We developed the first treatment to focus on using their gift to promote free online courses to pastors to supplement their training and meet this specific need/problem that exists. We developed a second treatment that highlighted current efforts to translate the free online courses into additional languages to expand their reach, which will require additional funding to build and promote. We wanted to find out if either of these treatments would increase donor conversion by addressing the needs at hand the organization is trying to meet and communicating a specific impact that the donor’s gift would be supporting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the split test across all 3 versions for one month on the donation page. What we found was the first treatment that emphasized pastoral training was mostly flat and saw a 6% increase with 19% level of confidence. On the second treatment however, we learned that the translation of courses seemed to be lifting donor conversion and at the end of the experiment resulted in a 16% increase with 48% level of confidence. Because we saw similar results on two additional online course donation pages that were running concurrently, we made the decision to roll out the second treatment as the new control for this donation page experience. We will continue to find opportunities to optimize this copy in future testing.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 67.5% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 7.9% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had an 11.1% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 10.3% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.18-24 visitors had a 55.8% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 10.3% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How asking for less than their historically largest gift impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a gift less than their historically largest gift for existing donors will achieve increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2022 - 02/28/2022
As part of their Founders Day campaign, The Fund for American Studies wanted to test a relatively new concept known as the downgraded ask. For this experiment, they created a chart of standards based upon the donors’ previous highest contribution where they would ask for a certain number of donors to give a specific amount. For the treatment version of this ask, they asked for a level below what the donor had previously given and added language acknowledging that they were being asked for less.
This was then tested across all 10 emails that were sent out as part of the campaign and results were determined based upon all online giving (excluding recurring gifts) during the month of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the split with the same audiences for the entirety of the campaign, we found that there was no statistically significant difference between the control and treatment. Additionally, we found that the overall average gift wasn't impacted by the treatment either.
Upon further investigation, we did find at least one email that used the same exact language across the control and treatment. This may have skewed the results. In addition this, the list was split and stratified manually before adding it to Hubspot. This was to make sure we had an even distribution of donors and engaged users. However after the split, additional suppressions were added to the the send list. This affected two things: the contact distribution may have been skewed and the sample size for the experiment was reduced. It is recommended that this is tested again perhaps in the context of a reactivation campaign.

experiments.title: How breaking down your campaign goal for donors impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that breaking down our fundraising goal into smaller, more relevant asks based upon their last gift amount for donors will achieve increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2022
As the Canadian Taxpayer Federation continued experimenting with breaking their campaign goal down into smaller, more relevant asks, they wanted to experiment with how this type of ask strategy would impact existing donors.
They segmented their donors based upon their “last gift” (range) to also see what the “right ask amount” for this campaign would be.
Those groups were:
Donors whose last gift was around $25.
Donors whose last gift was around $50.
Donors whose last gift was around $100.
Donors whose last gift was around $200.
They then built a treatment like a previous email used for the same campaign that broke down the fundraising goal into one of those four ask amounts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 71.3% level of confidence (which is not fully validated), we observed a decrease in donor conversion rate of -16.9%.
The revenue decrease didn't exactly reflect the decline found in the donor conversion rate — where we observed only a -6.2% decrease in revenue, however that also did not validate (LoC: 60.3%).
When digging into the numbers more closely, we noticed something of interest ... specifically, this tactic actually increased donor conversion rate by +33.7% (albeit with only a 69.2% level of confidence), while we observed a fully valid (LoC: 97.4%) decrease of -39.3% in donor conversion rate when we asked for gifts larger than $100.
Overall, there is some evidence that this organization can lift response rates on asks made to their donors at/below $50, but if they ask for more than $100 in this way, the lack of responses from those segments overall brought the total treatment segment to the aggregate decrease shown above.
Further experimentation may be required, but this points towards using this tactic for smaller ask amounts rather than really large ones for this particular organization.

experiments.title: How framing the visitor as the “solution” impacts survey completions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing the visitor as the key to finding a solution for email prospecting and paid social media audiences will achieve an increase in survey completion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2022 - 01/27/2022
The American Cornerstone Institute was running a survey on the state of education in America. This was being promoted via email prospecting and paid social media. We had a hypothesis that be framing the visitor as the key to finding a solution to the problem would increase their likelihood to take the survey. We decided to test this hypothesis against the existing control version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for two full weeks, we found that there was no statistically significant difference between the control and treatment versions. This would suggest that either the hypothesis is wrong or the treatment did not lean heavily enough into theory to understand the impact.
Interestingly, we did observe an increase of 30% in donor conversion with a 96% level of confidence. However, when digging deeper into the data, we observed that the increase in conversion rate fluctuated greatly from day to day which suggests that there were outside factors impacting the results. We would need to relaunch this experiment to better understand if it truly was impacting donor conversion.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 6.6% increase in email acquisition with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How revising donation page copy affected year-end giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing clarity for donation page visitors will achieve increase conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2021 - 12/30/2021
When reviewing donation page copy for a year-end campaign, we noticed the headline and intro paragraph copy did not flow conversationally. We wanted to test a new version of the page that highlighted the seasonal relevance of the offer and made a more clear reflection of the need at hand. We updated the headline to propose a question rather than a command to incorporate the concept of donor autonomy and led the copy with the urgent need rather than beginning with Scripture.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were only able to run the experiment for a brief portion of the campaign and during that limited window, we did not reach the minimum number of transactions to be able to thoroughly analyze the results. We only reached 50% level of confidence so we will need to re-test this theory on a future campaign.

experiments.title: How giving the donor autonomy during a match affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving the donor autonomy for prospective donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/11/2022
As CaringBridge was preparing to launch their holiday appeal, they wanted to test integrating the concept of autonomy in how they asked donors to give when their gift was matched. Traditionally, they would ask the donors to give and tell them their gift would be matched. But they decided to add a second layer to this by asking the donor to “allow the Board to match their gift”.
The hypothesis is that giving the donor more autonomy—or power in the transaction—would increase giving.
They split their email file to test the concept.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a slight increase in conversion rate, but not to a statistically valid level.
In the control, the offer to the donor is to "get their gift matched". But autonomy puts more power in the donor's hands—so the treatment copy gave the donor the POWER to allow the Board to match their gift. In essence, it placed the Board in a position of servitude to the donor, rather than making them the most important (and generous) part of the transaction.
This will be re-tested in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a visual match reinforcement affects year-end giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a visual representation for a matching gift for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2021 - 01/01/2022
For TFAS’ calendar-year-end campaign, we wanted to create a visual reinforcement for the matching gift. The goal is to have a visual representation of how a donor’s impact is doubled by the matching gift when they give during the year-end campaign. In this particular case we used a numerical visual representation that would instantly change as the prospective donor selected a different amount in the gift array. When someone selected $50, the counter would show a $100 impact. If $100 was selected, the counter would display a $200 impact, etc. We hypothesized that this visual representation would increase both donor conversion rate and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though this experiment did not fully validate, there was a directional decrease and enough evidence to indicate that this visual representation did not result in a higher donor conversion rate, nor did it inspire a higher average gift. There was a 62% directional decrease in revenue through this experiment. The speculation is that the visual number doubling, may have been confusing for some prospective donors and perhaps felt like they were donating $100 instead of $50. Another way we can test this concept in the future is to test the doubling of the gift through a visual representation on a thermometer instead.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 62.8% decrease with a 92% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 53.5% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 54.9% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had an 84.4% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 100.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.Male visitors had an 87.5% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How keeping a countdown clock visible at all times impacted donor converison
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the countdown timer visible throughout the process for visitors to the end of year donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2021 - 01/04/2022
At the end of the year, American Cornerstone Institute was running a fundraising campaign to raise money for 2022. Given the December 31 deadline, we incorporated an animated countdown clock at the top of the donation page. After reviewing the page, we questioned whether it would help improve conversion by making the clock always visible on the page. It is possible this could also cause a distraction to the visitor so we decided to test the static banner at the top against one that was sticky and would scroll with the user.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for the final days of the campaign, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the control and treatment. This would imply that the implementation of the countdown clock is less important that simply having it on the page.

experiments.title: How personalizing the Working Preacher fall campaign email effected the donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing an email for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/17/2021
This experiment was run on the first email sent to the housefile for the Working Preacher fall campaign.
The goal of this experiment was to increase donor conversion rate by personalizing the verbiage and simplifying the design of the email.
Within the treatment, we changed multiple factors including:
Beginning the email with a personalized story of how Working Preacher has positively affected a preacher’s job and messages
Explaining the why behind how coming up with new and interesting content, week-after-week, can be very difficult and strenuous
Simplifying the design and removing the templated “look” of the email to seem like it’s coming from a real person
Changing the wording from (in the control) speaking from an organization to (in the treatment) speaking in a conversation
Putting more emphasis on the donor and how important their donation is to this campaign
Adding more hyperlinks throughout the whole email to keep giving a donation at the top of mind for the potential donor
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that personalizing an email that is asking for donations to the Working Preacher fall campaign had no effect on the donor conversion rate.

We believe that Working Preacher's donors are already accustomed to how Working Preacher talks throughout their campaigns, so personalizing the email did not have an effect on the donor's motivation to give.

experiments.title: How the sender of cultivation emails during a campaign performed when soliciting for gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the sender of cultivation emails during a campaign for campaign recipients will achieve increased revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2021
The Leadership Institute decided to experiment with adding donor stories on top of their regularly scheduled calendar year-end campaign email appeal schedule to experiment with whether or not it inspired more people to give at all or increased generosity from those that saw the donor story emails.
As the second part of this year-end experiment, we were interested to learn whether the second sender (who sent the donor stories) would increase the donor conversion rate and/or revenue when soliciting gifts from the treatment segment (that saw the donor stories) over that of the control segment (that did not).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 61.1% level of confidence, we observed a -12.3% decrease in revenue.

Similarly, we only observed with a 48.7% level of confidence a -13.6% decrease in donor conversion rate, as well.

Ultimately, we were unable to validate either metric through this experiment and further experimentation may be required.

experiments.title: How adding donor cultivation stories on top of a campaign appeal schedule of emails impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending donor stories for year-end campaign recipients will achieve increased revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 01/02/2021
The Leadership Institute decided to experiment with adding donor stories on top of their regularly scheduled calendar year-end campaign email appeal schedule to experiment with whether or not it inspired more people to give at all or increased generosity from those that saw the donor story emails.
To administer the test, we evenly split the file based upon (a) donor vs. non-donor statuses, (b) the donor’s highest previous contribution tier, (c) how long the person was on the email file, and finally (d) whether or not they were currently considered “engaged” or “disengaged” from the email program (where “engaged” was whether or not they had opened any of the last 10 emails that we had sent to them before splitting the email file evenly for this experiment).
The donor stories were sent from a “secondary sender” for the organization—someone who was planned to be used in two of the year-end campaign messages.
Finally, to ensure that the samples within the study are accurate, we looked at unique individual emails for both segments that received any year-end email (including the “donor story” emails for the treatment segment)
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 53.7% level of confidence, we observed that the treatment segment did not increase revenue in any significant way.

Although this treatment segment saw a decrease in the donor conversion rate of -23.2% (with a valid level of confidence of 99.4%) however, we don't believe that the additional donor story emails drove the decrease in donor conversion rate.

We did notice, however, that there was a +27.8% average gift value for those donors that did decide to give from the treatment segment over that of those in the control segment (who did not receive the donor stories).

Further experimentation should be considered with this approach.

experiments.title: How asking for an upgraded donation from a modeled audience impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for more than their highest previous gift for donors likely to upgrade will increase revenue to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/15/2021 - 09/30/2021
For KCBI’s fall fundraising campaign, we gained access to a new tool (Pursuant’s GivingDNA) with the ability to predict donors who were likely to upgrade. To validate this modeled data, we decided to launch a two-prong experiment. We wanted to see if we could accelerate the upgrade process by asking for a gift above their highest previous gift. We also decided to see if we could increase conversion by asking for a donation less than their highest previous gift for the segment that was not identified as likely to upgrade.
For this experiment, we split the donor file in four ways to create control and treatment groups of the upgrade and non-upgrade identified segments. We then created four distinct emails in an eight email campaign that would either include the standard ask or would ask them for the upgraded/downgraded amount. The upgrade/downgrade language would include logic that explained why we were asking for more or less. This same language was used in all four experiment emails.
In this campaign, we utilize a tactic known as the chart of standards. We segmented each segment by their highest previous contribution and then selected a limited number of donors for a specific ask amount we wanted for this campaign. You can see this breakdown here. This concept is based upon research done on a theory in social psychology known as construal level theory. Our previous study has found this to lift giving in high urgency campaign–this experiment was a continuation of this research effort.
The information below is specific to the segment that was identified as likely to upgrade. It is significantly smaller, which will make it difficult to validate the learnings. However, at the end of the campaign, we analyzed the behavior of these distinct donors to understand how it both impacted their likelihood to upgrade and their likelihood to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After four different emails across a two-week period, we found that soliciting donors identified as likely to upgrade for a gift above their highest previous gift had no statistically significant impact on the overall revenue.

We analyzed both the overall revenue impact as well as the conversion rate. Neither saw a statistically significant change due to the messaging change. We also analyzed those who upgraded, downgraded, or stayed consistent with their historical giving trends and found no change between the control and treatment.

However, we did find a directional increase in revenue for the segment that was not identified as likely to upgrade when we used language that solicited for a downgraded amount. The details on this side of the experiment can be found here.

experiments.title: How presenting the gift frequency as a tabbed form on acquisition pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a tabbed form have a negative impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2021 - 07/11/2021
Focus on the Family has continued to test into the best way to present the gift frequency/gift type section on their donation pages. After learning that presenting it as a tabbed form increased donor conversion by 16% on the primary donation page, they wanted to test this method on other donation pages to ensure that it didn’t have a negative impact on other donation pages so that they could potentially roll this approach out across all donation pages. To test into this, they ran the tabbed form across some of their acquisition donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings: The tabbed form had neither a positive or negative impact on donor conversion. This is a great learning because it confirms that the tabbed approach can be rolled out across all donation pages as the approach to use for presenting the gift frequency/gift kind section.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity that a content offer is free affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making it clear a content offer is free, while tying that to value proposition, will not decrease giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2021 - 07/29/2021
Focus on the Family had been receiving complaints that people didn’t know the video series was free and they believed they needed to make a donation to get the series. We tested one iteration of a donation page that increased the use of the word free in conjunction with the video series. That test decreased donations by 20%. We then tested another iteration of a donation page that focused on clarity and addressed the reader specifically, rather than increasing the reference to the series being free. This did not have a negative impact on donor conversion and only brought more clarity to the page – which was what we had hoped. To make sure that test wasn’t an isolated experience, we tested that exact same approach on one of the most popular video series to see the impact.
This was a 50/50 A/B test that ran for four weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: This iteration of the test didn't decrease donations while it increased clarity that the reader did not have to donate to receive the video series. These results are consistent with the same test we ran on another offer. We will roll this approach out to all acquisition pages.

experiments.title: Does moving the form further down increase sign ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does moving the form further down the enrollment page allow the eye to more easily see the course descriptions, which may motivate them to enroll?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/20/2018 - 04/19/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to see if changing the layout on the sign up page for their Constitutional Conversations course would impact conversions. They decided to test endorsements behind a white background versus a blue background, and made the other course description boxes larger on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The mobile versions of these layouts do not include the quotes or course descriptions, so both versions look identical. Even with a larger sample size, this experiment can’t be validated. In the future, any layout tests should exclude the any traffic that wouldn’t be impacted by the test. In the future, a new test to consider would be a treatment without course descriptions to see if they impact enrollment.

experiments.title: How a membership offer impacts new visitors to ADF
experiments.researchQuestion: Will “membership” be a more appealing offer than “donating” to new visitors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2019 - 05/13/2019
As part of our efforts to evaluate the impact a membership program could have on the ADF fundraising efforts, we set up an experiment on the Thank You page of an email acquisition campaign. The visitors were coming from the blog after signing a Statement of Belief. We then created a new version of this page with the main modification being the final call to action at the end. We testing the concept of “membership” against the standard “donate” messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this experiment targeted at both new and existing visitors but, when digging into the metrics, we discovered that it had no significant impact on existing visitor conversion but it seemed to directionally impact new visitors likelihood to make a gift. This experiment had run for a full two months and would have required another month before validating so the decision was made to end this experiment to avoid being impacted by the “history effect.”
As next steps, we will roll this experiment out to a dedicated Facebook campaign where we will be able to send more new visitors to the treatment to see if we can replicate this impact in a statistically significant manner.

experiments.title: Messaging/OE Test – July 2014 – KYFAH Appeal I
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a new OE and slightly different appeal outperform the control?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/29/2019
What Was Tested
We changed the Outer Envelope from a photo of Obama, the ACLU, and the Bible with a red X through it which said: What’s behind the unprecedented attacks on your religious freedom … and what you can do about it!  To an OE with a photo of a door that says “Official Notice: Keep your faith at home – or else” with a teaser of: A message to Christians from the ACLU and the Obama Administration. We also varied the text in the appeal slightly to focus more on the ACLU Triple Threat.

experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a significant decrease in ROI due to a decrease in average gift, response, and an increase in costs. It is not recommended to use this kit.

experiments.title: How increasing “involving” score affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing “involving” score affect email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2020
Hillsdale College was running a pre-registration campaign for their new course, “Introduction to Western Philosophy”. They had run a test that determined that a “problem-solution” framework increased clickthrough rate, and thus signups and donors. However, they had been informed of a partner who had a method of testing copy on a 2×2 framework that indicated that increasing “narrative” score and “involving” score would increase fundraising conversion. The partner put together a treatment that, through their scoring mechanism, significantly increased “involving” score. They decided to test this copy to see if it had a positive effect on name acquisition, which would get more names into the donor conversion funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the higher “involving” score produced a slight, but not statistically significant decrease in email acquisition rate. When the team looked at the full funnel, there was a slight, but not statistically valid increase in donor conversion later in the process.
Ultimately, there wasn’t a significant difference at either step of the funnel. This may have been because of one of two factors:
The two-step soft offer of preregistration followed by ask might have muted the copy’s effect on donor conversion by placing the involving copy too far from the ask.
The copy that produced the higher involving score also lowered the narrative score, which may have had an adverse effect on conversion.
This shows a path to testing future direct ask copy with this method, provided that both involving and narrative scores both increase.

experiments.title: How the call-to-action within an article offer impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call-to-action would increase reader response on article pages?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2020 - 06/13/2020
On the Focus on the Family marriage articles, they featured a video series called, Cherish. This video series was free for people to sign-up for and was presented at the bottom of every marriage article. The call-to-action on the offer was a button that would then take a person to the sign up page. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase conversion if we presented the sign-up form right there on the article. We split the traffic, 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for a month and we found that there was no significant difference in name conversion between the two different call-to-actions. This was a bit surprising as we have seen that reducing friction to the acquisition process can help conversion. Because the results were a wash, we hypothesize that the offer may have been over saturated on these pages since it had been running for months prior to this experiment. We hypothesize this because of the low conversion rate on the offer. We would like to test this tactic again in the future using a new, fresh, content offer on the article pages.

experiments.title: How unique email messaging to key audience segments affects webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending unique copy to key audience segments increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2020 - 05/12/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if sending uniquely tailored messaging to our key audience personas would impact their likelihood of registering for a webinar. The control used general messaging to a portion of the housefile. The treatment used tailored messaging to fundraising executives and fundraising practitioners.
experiments.keyLearnings: While it seemed that registration was trending up for the segmented messaging group, we did not reach a statistical level of confidence of 95% or greater. This requires further testing, and leaning into the persona based messaging even more.

experiments.title: How unique email messaging to key audience segments affects webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will sending unique copy to key audience segments increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2020 - 05/12/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if sending uniquely tailored messaging to our key audience personas would impact their likelihood of registering for a webinar.
