The control used general messaging to a portion of the housefile. The treatment used tailored messaging to fundraising executives and fundraising practitioners.
experiments.keyLearnings: While it seemed that registration was trending up for the segmented messaging group, we did not reach a statistical level of confidence of 95% or greater. This requires further testing, and leaning into the persona based messaging even more.

experiments.title: How including an image influences conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including a related image on the course registration page improve email acquisition rate for the Jane Austen online course?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2019 - 09/17/2019
For Hillsdale’s free course offer, we wanted to test increasing clarity through providing an image related to the course subject that was used to promote the offer on the email acquisition page. The control version used the same copy and contained no image. Our hypothesis was that adding clarity and familiarizing the audience with the author of the works covered in the course content would increase the perceived value of the offer. We wanted to see if showing a black and white illustrated image would act as a distraction and introduce friction or improve conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a month, we were not able to validate the results of the experiment and will need to pursue further testing with this hypothesis.
Adding some clarity to the offer—enough to remind the visitors of the topic that would be covered in the course and increase the perceived value of the offer—without acting as a distraction, did result in additional course registrations on the treatment page,

experiments.title: How removing visual breaks in a page did not increase email signup rate on a pledge
experiments.researchQuestion: Would removing the design elements and visual breaks on a pledge page increase email signup rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2019 - 11/08/2019
As a part of experimenting with what will increase email signups from the Freedom Pledge offer for Americans for Prosperity, we theorized that the visual breaks in the design may have been creating “mental hitches” for the reader that was limiting our ability to acquire emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for more than 2 weeks, there was not a significant enough difference between the control and treatment experience to validate this hypothesis, showing that there was a difference of less than 1%, with a level of confidence of only 6.75%, as well.
Further experimentation may be required.

experiments.title: Conversational “helpful” ad copy vs ad copy about a “war” on pro-life beliefs
experiments.researchQuestion: Which copy would convert more donors on our online course, copy that appealed to their feeling of being attacked by the media or copy that offered to help them overcome a longstanding fear?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2019 - 08/08/2019
We had a new online course, Defending Your Pro-Life Beliefs, that we wanted to advertise on Facebook. Our control ad focused on how pro-life beliefs are under attacks and how our course could help people defend their beliefs in the public square. We wanted to test this copy against copy that focused on how nervous people feel when they talk about being pro-life and how our course would help them be confident in these conversations. We wanted to see if appealing to the feeling among conservatives that their values were under attack would convert more donors than appealing to their desire to become confident.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The more conversational copy won across the board. The variation had a click to course signup rate of 23% vs 17% for the control. It had a Click to donation rate of 1.34% vs 1.13%. During the two week test, the control brought in $462 while the variation brought in $700.
We found that people were more likely to take the course and donate when we explained in the ad copy how the course would help them feel confident, rather than when we appealed to their desire to protect themselves from the attacks of the pro-choice Left.

experiments.title: How intellectually-focused copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will intellectually-focused copy affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/20/2019 - 06/29/2019
Hillsdale College had developed copy for their fiscal year end campaign, designed to be donor-centric. However, as the copy went through their editing process, it was changed to include more intellectual language that elevated the reading level and put the College at the center, instead of the donor. Hillsdale wanted to test the “pre-edit” copy against the “post-edit” copy, and created two landing pages to split traffic to throughout the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The intellectually-focused copy produced a valid decrease of 12% early in the campaign, but later “high urgency” emails diluted both the impact and the level of confidence by increasing motivation. This resulted in a directional learning, but one that’s important to consider for future copy.

experiments.title: How streamlining an admissions inquiry page affects completion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding additional information to a landing page increase the likelihood of completion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2019 - 04/21/2019
Heartlight Ministries has an admissions inquiry page for anyone interested in getting more information about Heartlight Boarding School, that they wanted to increase conversion on. We hypothesized that adding additional information about Heartlight and answering FAQ’s on the landing page would do this. We also streamlined the page and removed extra navigation and sidebars to keep people’s focus on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version decreased conversion by 12% (not valid). It’s likely that people are so highly motivated to submit the form once they get there that additional copy actually adds friction to the page, instead of helping.

experiments.title: Will asking for a gift to help us reach more people improve donor conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking prospective donors to help us reach more people increase the donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2019 - 08/26/2019
In an attempt to improve donor conversion rates for the Freedom Pledge, we wanted to experiment with asking the prospective donor to help us reach more people to sign the pledge they just signed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment did not validate, we saw a slight decrease in donor conversion rate before we moved away from the offer altogether.
With only 4 total gifts between the two variations, more experimentation with the motivation of “help us get more people with your gift today” is advised.

experiments.title: How isolating an appeal from a newsletter affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will isolating the appeal from a newsletter increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/27/2019
Jerusalem Prayer Team had a daily email newsletter that included an appeal, generally combined with a premium offer. However, they wanted to see if isolating the appeal section from the email would increase focus on that section and increase giving by presenting the reader with a “direct ask”. They created two unique emails and split the file, noting that half the file would not receive the news portion for that day.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The appeal-only email delivered 40% fewer donors, though the low overall donor conversion meant that it wasn’t statistically valid. This points to a few possible hypotheses:
First the lack of news content (if relied on by the prospect) could have lowered motivation to “return the favor” with a gift.
The steady drumbeat of news content could have reduced engagement by not offering what the prospect was used to.
This could have just been due to a random sample of the audience having more donor prospects in it than the other, for that particular offer on that particular day.
Ultimately, more testing must be done to increase donation results from the newsletter.

experiments.title: How isolating one high-performing content offer over segmented offers impacts conversion on parenting articles
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the best performing segmented offer increase conversion if offered as a single offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2019 - 06/11/2019
Through a series of tests on the Focus on the Family parenting articles, we found that presenting segmented GOAA offers based on what people consume on the Focus website increases donor conversion over one single offer. That said, we found that there is one specific GOAA offer that performed best out of all of the segmented offers presented. To isolate this single offer and see if it could increase donor conversion alone, we tested it against the segmented offers.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the results. We found that the best performing segmented offer could not impact donor conversion on it’s own. We propose moving forward with segmented offers for all traffic.

experiments.title: How we used A/A testing to validate the fix to an email acquisition form
experiments.researchQuestion: Is the conversion issue fixed for the NetCommunity landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/19/2016 - 09/22/2016
At the launch of The Jesus Agenda eBook offer for Buckner International, we noticed a significantly low conversion rate that did not align with what we would expect for this kind of offer. After running a series of experiments, we did not see the kind of lift we were expecting. Not wanting to give up on the offer, we decided to launch an A/A test by created an exact duplicate of the email acquisition page in a different system. After launching this test, we noticed that the normal system was seeing a significantly lower conversion rate than the one set up in the other system.
After further investigation in Google Analytics, we discovered that a security error was being thrown for Android devices. We made a fix to the page and then relaunched this test to validate that it had fixed the issue.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 4 days, this A/A test did not show a significant difference in conversion between the two systems. Based upon this result, we concluded the security warning had been the cause of the conversion problem and that it was now resolved.

experiments.title: How a text endorsement compares to a video endorsement for conversion impact
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a text endorsement and/or video endorsement impact sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2018 - 06/02/2018
This experiment was tested on the “Introduction to the Constitution” online course sign up page. We had found in previous experiments that videos negatively impacted a prospects likelihood to make a gift but we had seen some indication that they could help improve email acquisition if used in the right way. We had a video that provided an endorsement for the new online course and we wanted to see if it could help boost email acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall, there was not enough difference in conversions to suggest that the video treatment made any impact. After running for a full month, we decided to move on to the next hypothesis since it did not see to make any significant improvements.

experiments.title: How a homepage promotion for the membership program impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Can a promotion for the membership program on the home page increase donor conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2019 - 04/04/2019
After launching a new Membership program, Alliance Defending Freedom wanted to promote the program on their homepage. We wanted to know how the introduction of the membership program overall from the home page would impact donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the home page promotion for over a month, we did notice a slight increase in donor conversion rate of +8.6%, but only achieved a level of confidence of 48.7%, which means that the experiment was not validated.
We did note that within that slight increase in donor conversion lift, that approximately 10% (13/130) gifts did come directly from the home page promotion.
In the end, we collectively agreed that the promotion itself needed a more “fair” treatment for the promotion of the control’s “donate” option.
For that reason, we ended the experiment and will be launching a new treatment which will more “fairly” promote the control’s “donate” path. We will also change the placement (from the right-side of the home page’s “hero image” area, as shown in the screenshot included to just below the “hero image” and the next section of content titled “Latest from the Blog”.

experiments.title: How shortening a quiz’s first page content decreased email registration rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a shorter amount of text on the first (registration page) of a quiz improve email registration rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/29/2019 - 05/08/2019
When Americans for Prosperity launched their “Economic Freedom Quiz” offer, we wanted to experiment with what would boost email registration rates. In reviewing the first slide of the quiz, we noticed that there was a lot of copy, so we wanted to see if shortening it would keep more of the form on the visitor’s screen to improve the quiz start rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 69.4% level of confidence, we noticed a slight decline (-7.7%) in email registration rates.
Because this experiment did not validate and the difference between the two versions was so slight (< 10%), there is no key learning as a part of this experiment.
Further experimentation is advise.

experiments.title: How a cultivation email prior to an appeal impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will long-form story emails positively impact overall giving to a campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2019 - 05/05/2019
In an analysis of prior appeals, Buckner International had discovered that their long story emails tended to be the lowest performing appeals. However, we had a hypothesis that while these emails may not perform well immediately, they may be influencing downstream giving. Given the time and effort required to write these emails, we decided to run a test to measure the impact of these “cultivation” style appeals when they are a part of a larger campaign.
To do this, we split the file in half and sent one segment a long story prior to the two appeal emails. The treatment just received the two appeal emails. We then monitored the donor giving across the entire campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: At the end of the campaign, we did not see any significant difference in donor conversion with the additional email. However, we did see a 50% increase to unsubscribes (with 100% level of statistical confidence) due to the addition of the extra email. Given that the additional story email did not have the positive impact on giving that we hypothesized, it would seem that we can reduce the number of emails in the appeal while negatively impacting giving.

experiments.title: How multichannel communication impacted a Giving Tuesday solicitation
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a direct ask made via a straight-to-voicemail call increase the likelihood of a donor to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2017 - 11/30/2017
As part of their Giving Tuesday campaign, Live Action wanted to see if they could use multiple channels of communication to drive incremental giving. Using a new technology named SlyBroadcast, we recorded a “voicemail” from the organization’s founder, Lila Rose. SlyBroadcast was then able to send this message straight to the voicemail of several hundred of their donors.
The message itself started with stewardship and led to a direct ask for support on Giving Tuesday. We wanted to see how this would impact the donor’s likelihood to give.
To determine this, we divided all recent donors with an email and phone number in half. We then sent the SlyBroadcast message to one segment. It should be noted that not everyone in the treatment segment would have actually received the voicemail since it would only work with digital phone lines. We saw a 60% delivery rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment shows a percentage increase, it did not come anywhere close to statistical significance. This same test was performed with two other organizations at the same time and all had similar results. What this indicates is that while it didn’t hurt the chances of giving, it didn’t seem to help them either.
In the future, we will likely want to avoid using SlyBroadcast for direct solicitations. Instead, it should be reserved for stewardship and cultivation; basically, any communication where we are not making a call to action.

experiments.title: How text reminders impacted campaign giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will cultivating donors via text during a moneybomb campaign increase overall conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2018 - 04/24/2018
For the 2018 Tax Day Moneybomb, we decided to test out a next texting technology to see if reminding donors to give on the day of a big campaign would increase overall conversion. Since we did not have a significant volume of cell phone numbers, we had to append the numbers to donor records. We began the campaign with a cultivation text the day before the deadline and then a reminder to give on the day of the deadline.
experiments.keyLearnings: When we compared those donors that were sent a text with those that could have received a text, we see that there was no statistically significant difference between the two segments. When we also factor in the time it took to set up the texting, the complaints received from members, and relatively low potential impact, this does not seem like a tactic that would be worth repeating for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a case study example in an email affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a follow-up webinar email that uses a case-study example increase registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2017 - 10/12/2017
When sending a re-targeted email to invite non-responders to register for an upcoming webinar, we wondered if there was a way to write a more appealing follow-up email than simply re-hashing the content of the prior email.
For the treatment, we thought that we could help the user experience the quality of the content we would be presenting by showing a real case study that applied to year-end fundraising.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw no measurable difference in opens, clicks, or conversions between the control and treatment.
This tells us that neither method is better than the other to get more people to register for a webinar. Further testing would be needed to determine whether or not this type of email makes a difference for various segments of the non-responder audience (i.e. Non-opens VS Opens without conversions).

experiments.title: How a stylized email template affects conversion rate for a webinar
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stylized email template affect the conversion rate for a webinar?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2017 - 04/05/2017
We had tested out of using email templates with our nonprofit research partners, but we had never tested what affect an email template would have with our own audience.
So we set up a treatment for a webinar promotional email that included a branded header and footer, as well as an image of the guest speaker. We hypothesized that this change could increase the credibility of both the speaker and the event itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was no statistically valid difference between the performance of the plain-text email and the stylized email template.
Moving forward, we will continue to use the plain-text version since it is easier to set-up, and it is consistent with what we recommend to our nonprofit partners.

experiments.title: Student vs. Celebrity 3rd Party Credibility
experiments.researchQuestion: Which 3rd party credibility source has the most influence on email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/18/2016 - 11/18/2016
In the ongoing effort to improve the Constitution 101 email acquisition efforts, we wanted to see which source would be best to lend 3rd party credibility. To do this, we tested the standard Rush Limbaugh quote against quotes from students that reinforced the value of the course.
experiments.keyLearnings: The student quotes showed no significant difference over the control. Nothing to change here.

experiments.title: How adding a phone number field affects name acquisition conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a required phone number field to the sign-up form affect the conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2016 - 07/20/2016
At NextAfter, we have a monthly live broadcast in which we present findings from the research we conduct with nonprofits, break down tests and experiments, and explore trends that demonstrate what makes a donor give.
We wanted to add a phone number field to our live broadcast sign-up form, but not at the expense of losing attendees.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not reach a significant level of confidence to conclude that adding the phone number field made a difference in conversion. If anything, the results pointed to a higher conversion rate with the phone number.
Moving forward, we will keep the phone number field knowing that it does not create significant enough friction in the sign up process to affect our attendance numbers, and it gives us another way to keep in touch with our attendees.

experiments.title: How the imagery of a page affected the campaign performance
experiments.researchQuestion: What impact does adding imagery to the page have on donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2015 - 09/28/2015
For the September money bomb for Hillsdale College, we wanted to find a way to increase the overall performance of the landing page. We had the hypothesis that by adding supporting imagery that reinforced the messages being communicated on the page, we could make the content more engaging and easier to understand. In this situation, we were using references to the Constitution and quoting notable historical figures such as Thomas Jefferson.  We decided to set up a new treatment that would include images of those elements along with the text.
experiments.keyLearnings: Adding the imagery to the page had no significant impact on the overall performance of the page. We will often debate the amount of influence design has on the overall conversion of the page. In this situation, the design elements did not increase value proposition, lower friction, or reduce anxiety; as a result, that made no significant impact on the likelihood of a donor to convert.

experiments.title: How the length of page copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a shorter version of copy reduce page friction enough to increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2015 - 08/30/2015
TSHA’s standard membership form utilized a long version of copy in order to fully explain the mission of the organization, the history, and the associated benefits of becoming a member. There was a hypothesis that this long copy may actually be turned off potential members due to the amount of friction it introduced into the process.
We decided to create a new version of the page that would dramatically reduce the copy in order to validate this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two landing pages had nearly identical conversion rates and there was no statistically significant difference between the average gift amounts. This result supports the conclusion that the length of copy on the page is not a significant factor in the overall conversion of the page.
In the future, it would be recommended that we need to find ways to add value for the potential member through different value propositions, incentives, and other page elements.

experiments.title: How membership benefits affect conversion during a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Does talking about member benefits on the year end landing page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2015 - 09/30/2015
During the fiscal year end campaign, the Texas State Historical Association had a landing page targeting non-members. It focused on the legacy of the organization and the work being done day-in and day-out. There was a premium for a gift of $50 or more but there were no other mentions of associated member benefits. These benefits had proven to be successful in other experiments so we decided to test them out in the year end campaign as well.
experiments.keyLearnings: The member benefits produced no statistically significant change in donor conversion. This would suggest that either they are not necessary during an urgency-based appeal like the fiscal year end or that the benefits did not add significant value for the potential donors. We will need to isolate these factors on a general donation form to determine which of these hypothesis are true.

experiments.title: How a high conversion rate affects visitor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: Which petition copy will acquire the most “signatures?”
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2016 - 02/01/2016
In response to statements made by Hillary Clinton about repealing the Hyde Amendment, a law that protects tax payer money from being put towards abortion, Susan B. Anthony List launched a petition. The goal was to collect enough signatures to send to the conservative candidates running for president so that they would understand the importance of this law to the American public.
To learn more about the motivations of the SBA List constituency, we developed two landing page treatments. The first positioned Hillary Clinton as the villain and urged the reader to sign the petition to help SBA list combat the attack. The second took a more educational approach in explaining the Hyde amendment but ended with how SBA List was going to fight back and why their signature was necessary.  We then split traffic between the two pages and drove traffic via email and Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither treatment produced a significant lift in visitor conversion or subsequent donor conversion on the next page. Both treatments received an 85% conversion rate which would indicate that the motivation of the visitors was high enough that the copy was not a major influence in the decision to sign. Given the exceptionally high conversion rate on this page, we decided to shift the testing efforts to the Facebook ads and donor conversion on the next step.

experiments.title: How member benefits affected donor conversion during a moneybomb
experiments.researchQuestion: Will member benefits increase the donor conversion or average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2015 - 09/24/2015
As part of their campaign to kick off the election season, the Media Research Center wanted to find a way to incentivize subscribers to become a new member at higher levels. Their standard campaigns have historically not focused on the membership aspect of their gift and instead tried to sell solely the philanthropic aspect of the donation.
We decided to test out language around the benefits associated with membership and include a premium offer of a book that reinforced the messaging of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant lift for either treatment. Since the campaign was for a limited period of time, we decided to end the test in order to try a different treatment that might achieve a greater lift. It would seem these benefits do not carry the added incentive in the donors’ minds when we have a money bomb campaign such as this.

experiments.title: How the learnings from an invalid experiment affects both time and resources
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the length of the video and the amount of content shown in the video produce a significant lift in the overall donation conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2015 - 04/20/2015
As part of their monthly April appeal, Jews for Jesus wanted to validate the work being put into different aspects of the monthly fundraising campaigns. Many of these campaigns include a 60 second video with David Brickner, the president. Previously analysis has found that these videos are able to drive significantly higher amounts of traffic to the landing pages. What we wanted to determine is if we needed the full 60 seconds in the video or if a shorter version could be just as effective.
experiments.keyLearnings: The 9.5% drop in conversions was not statistically valid which proves that the length of video does not have a significant impact on the success of the page. In future campaigns, we will be able to produce shorter videos (which take less time and money) and not adversely affect the results.

experiments.title: How member benefits affected donor conversion during a moneybomb
experiments.researchQuestion: Will member benefits increase the donor conversion or average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2015 - 09/24/2015
As part of their campaign to kick off the election season, the Media Research Center wanted to find a way to incentivize subscribers to become a new member at higher levels. Their standard campaigns have historically not focused on the membership aspect of their gift and instead tried to sell solely the philanthropic aspect of the donation.
We decided to test out language around the benefits associated with membership and include a premium offer of a book that reinforced the messaging of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant lift for either treatment. Since the campaign was for a limited period of time, we decided to end the test in order to try a different treatment that might achieve a greater lift. It would seem these benefits do not carry the added incentive in the donors’ minds when we have a moneybomb campaign such as this.

experiments.title: Prayer Points – Pic vs NO Pic
experiments.researchQuestion: Do pictures create friction or increase empathy?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2015 - 11/13/2015
We are looking to see if its more powerful to have images in the body of the email that will lead to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: Whether we should include images in every prayer points email

experiments.title: Anita Dittman (Background vs. No Background)
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the background image cause friction which leads to less subscribers?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2015 - 01/05/2016
We wanted to see what would happen if we had a background image on our landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: From this test we learned that people who did not see a background image were more likely to become a subscriber. We did not reach confidence, but were given enough confidence to test it again.

experiments.title: Confessing Hebrew Scriptures  (Background Img vs No Background Img)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a background increase friction so that less users complete the form?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2016 - 01/19/2016
We wanted to see if adding a background distracted users from completing the form.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this test, we found that the background did not negatively affect the submissions. In fact, it increased submissions, but not to the level of confidence. We will need to add some more elements and/or try other background tests to determine if this really affects sign ups.

experiments.title: Web Donation Form Multivariate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a new headline, copy, or new combination of copy elements product more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2016 - 01/27/2016
We wanted to test headline and copy elements in a multivariate to see how visitors responded.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that there was a slight variation between the original and variant #1 which combined a new headline with the old copy. The headline focused more on the value proposition, making it clear what we do that is valuable.

experiments.title: How membership benefits affect conversion during a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Does talking about member benefits on the year end landing page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2015 - 09/30/2015
During the fiscal year end campaign, the Texas State Historical Association had a landing page targeting non-members. It focused on the legacy of the organization and the work being done day-in and day-out. There was a premium for a gift of $50 or more but there were no other mentions of associated member benefits. These benefits had proven to be successful in other experiments so we decided to test them out in the year end campaign as well.
experiments.keyLearnings: The member benefits produced no statistically significant change in donor conversion. This would suggest that either they are not necessary during an urgency-based appeal like the fiscal year end or that the benefits did not add significant value for the potential donors. We will need to isolate these factors on a general donation form to determine which of these hypothesis are true.

experiments.title: How identifying the “next steps” impacts donor conversion after email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will explaining the “next steps” in the process increase the perceived impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2017 - 04/25/2017
For Live Action, there had been a significant emphasis on acquiring email addresses using their Pro-Life petition. However, they new subscribers had not been converting to donors at a significant level so we decided to launch a test.
Our hypothesis is that if we explain the process to the potential donors, they can understand how the next step they take (a donation) will play into the bigger picture of defunding Planned Parenthood. This would increase the perceived impact of their gift and, subsequently, the donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two months, we were not able to see a significant impact to donation conversion for the treatment. Our hypothesis is this was likely caused by the low motivation of the audience. Petition signers are not often a good source of donors given the low barrier for entry (signing a petition). Since there is minimal value exchange, the donation ask is not as likely to happen.

experiments.title: How the donation premium can affect both conversion rate and average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: Which premium will increase the overall revenue from existing members?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2015 - 09/30/2015
As part of Texas State Historical Association’s fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to find a way to improve the average gift of existing members in order to boost revenue. The majority of existing members were acquired using the premium of the 2014-15 Texas Almanac. The update to this book was going to be released in the coming months so we had a feeling it would be a popular premium offer to use with the donor base.
However, this book is normally offered for a gift of $50 which meant it wouldn’t do much to raise the average gift. We decided to test another popular book as a premium and increase the minimum gift to $100 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Along Forgotten River premium offer reduced the donor conversion rate of the page by 50%. However, it was also able to increase the average gift by 64%. These two factors cancelled out the combined impact on revenue and produced no statistically significant change in either direction.  This does prove to us that if we can find the right premium offer, we should be able to increase the average gift enough to boost revenue.

experiments.title: How shorter copy in a “Dear Reader” experiment affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does shorter, value focused copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2017 - 06/20/2017
The Live Action team was running a “Dear Reader” experiment on Live Action news, and wanted to test if a shortened version of the copy would convert more donors. The new copy had more personal, value-focused copy on the benefits to the donor, rather than an overview of what Live Action News does.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment were not valid, and the experiment was ended in favor of a new experiment before we could reach conclusive results on the effectiveness of the copy. However, the experiment trended in favor of the control, which could have increased the perception of the impact that Live Action News was having on the worldwide perception of abortion versus the shorter version that had fewer statistics. The statistics could have impacted the motivation of the giver more than the personal, donor-focused approach in this case.
Another factor that could have impacted giving was the stepped approach that the control took in leading potential donors through why they should give, rather than text blocks in the treatment.

experiments.title: How the emphasis on the power of prayer impacted sign up for a devotional
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shifting the benefits of the offer from their child to themselves increase the visitor’s likelihood to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2019 - 03/21/2019
Buckner’s 7 Scripture offer had been running for nearly two years as a tool for email acquisition. The landing page focused on the need and benefits of this offer on the reader’s children. Based upon results we had observed at the ad-level, we had a hypothesis that we could see increased results by modifying the landing page to focus on the benefits to the reader. We also wanted to emphasize the power of prayer to reinforce the kind of good it can do in the individual’s life.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month and a half, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the control and treatment. It would appear, that shifting the focus entirely to the reader may have too far removed the primary purpose of the offer. As a result, we ended up not seeing any change in conversion. A next natural step would be a blending of these two treatments. By positioning the offer so that it applies to both the visitor and their child, we can potentially get the best of both world.

experiments.title: The impact of Scripture in an appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using scripture in an appeal increase donor response rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2019
Buckner International had seen success with recent communications by leaning heavily into scripture. As a Christian organization, they would incorporate scripture verses into their cultivation emails and even had an acquisition offer that focused on scripture. With this in mind, we had the hypothesis that using scriptures in our appeals may increase the reader’s sense of duty which would then increase the likelihood to respond.
However, we wanted to be sure it wasn’t isolated to a single email. To do this, we split the house file equally among donors and nondonors and maintained this split through the whole campaign. We then three distinct emails, each with a scripture and non-scripture, version to measure the impact on the gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After sending three different emails with both scripture and non-scripture versions, we did not see any statistically significant increase in donor conversion. In fact, the only email that showed any increase in giving was the initial email sent to the file (shown above). It is also worth pointing out that the control emails did not omit mentions of God, they simply did not directly incorporate scripture.
This is a fascinating learning as it tells us that we do not need to directly reference scripture in order to invoke the donors’ sense of duty.

experiments.title: How adding a qualifying question affects conversion rate on an opt-in form

rows.name: Do you work for a nonprofit?

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a qualifying question on a general opt-in form affect the conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2019 - 03/14/2019
In a previous experiment on a webinar sign-up page, we learned that asking registrants if they worked for a nonprofit or not led to a significant increase in webinar registrations. We wondered if we could duplicate the same affect on a general opt-in form.
experiments.keyLearnings: The additional qualifying question made no significant impact on conversion rate. At first, this was a bit of let down seeing that it saw a 32% increase on a webinar page. But in reality, even seeing no change in conversion is a positive because it allows us to be more targeted with our segmentation and messaging without negatively impacting opt-ins.

experiments.title: How utilizing a longer funnel during early year end fundraising impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the video series offer result in more donations than a direct donation ask during year end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2018 - 12/21/2018
During the early part of December, Focus on the Family had found in previous years that the direct ask banner ads did not perform well with mobile visitors. During 2018’s year end campaign, they decided to test a tactic of presenting an offer for their new online video series. It is an email acquisition offer that presented new subscribers with a donation opportunity once they had finished the signup process. They launched this offer against their standard direct donation ask that runs throughout all of December.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not reach a level of statistical significance with this experiment. We saw a difference of 12 gifts between the control and treatment. However, it is worth noting that the video series resulted in nearly 400 emails acquired for the organization. These emails have the potential to convert downstream and provide additional value.
In future campaigns, it may be worth testing an offer that is a middle-ground between an email acquisition and direct donation ask. For Focus, that would be a “gift of any amount” offer that offers a book or DVD when they make a donation.

experiments.title: How signifying a recommended gift amount affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does signifying a recommended gift amount and putting a value to that amount affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2019 - 01/31/2019
Buckner International was running their 7 Scriptures campaign and wanted to test if signifying the “most popular” donation amount, along with what that gift amount provides for a child, would affect donor conversion. They kept all copy  the same, and put a box and gift handle on the $50 amount.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment did not validate or produce any specific learnings. We’ve seen suggested gift amounts and “most popular” gifts work in the past but, in this situation, it would appear new donors were not as impacted by these suggestions. The next iteration of this experiment is to potentially increase average gift amounts by testing a premium with a minimum gift amount.

experiments.title: How requiring all survey questions impacted the survey completion rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the survey response rate improve when all questions are not required?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2018
As a part of the 2018 calendar year-end fundraising efforts, we launched the National Concerned Citizen Survey, which was a 13-question survey designed to gather feedback from our donors, email subscribers and prospective donors regarding “how to align our priorities in 2019 and beyond.” We wanted to know if requiring all questions to be answered would negatively impact the survey completion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment did not validate and we decided to experiment with something that would likely more appropriately positively impact our ability to impact the survey completion (subsequently, the email registration) rate.
Further experimentation could be used to validate definitively, but it appears that requiring all fields on a survey of this length has a negligible of any impact on survey completion rates.

experiments.title: How adding location to a seminar sign up affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the word “Constitution” and the location of the seminar in the sign up box increase sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2018 - 08/22/2018
Hillsdale College was promoting a Lifelong Learning Seminar. They wanted to increase sign ups, so they created an experiment to test adding additional information about the seminar — the descriptor “Constitution”, and the location of the seminar. They wondered if adding more information would make people more likely to sign up.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was insufficient traffic to the pages to validate this experiment.

experiments.title: How adding context to a video endorsement impacts sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding copy under a video endorsement impact sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2018 - 06/27/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to know if adding copy to an endorsement video on the “Introduction to the Constitution” sign up page would increase the number of sign ups. They decided to test one sign up page with copy versus another sign up page with no copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a large enough sample size to validate this experiment. However, with a conversion rate of 30.2% with the copy version and a 33.1% conversation rate with the version without copy, it’s likely that with more traffic, this experiment could validate in favor of no copy with video endorsements in the future. The test would need to run longer in order to prove this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How different endorsement methods impact sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a text endorsement and/or video endorsement impact sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2018 - 05/08/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to know if adding an endorsement on the “Introduction to the Constitution” sign up page would increase the number of sign ups. They decided to test different endorsement styles, quotes vs videos vs a combination of both.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall, there was not enough difference in conversions to suggest that the endorsement treatments made any impact. However, there was not a large enough sample size to validate this experiment. With more data, this experiment could likely validate in the future.

experiments.title: How indicating the number of steps for a single-step funnel impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding clarity by adding “step 1 of 2” above the form increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/25/2018 - 11/15/2018
We tested adding “step 1 of 2” on our pro-life 101 free online course landing page and increased conversion by 22%. We wanted to test this on another offer and see if it had similar impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for three weeks, we were unable to validate. There was a slight increase, however it was not significant. We will continue adding the text to our landing pages, but any impact will probably be contingent on the specific offer and how traffic was driven there.

experiments.title: How adding an additional cultivation opportunity affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a checkbox to receive additional content at the end of a form affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2018 - 05/11/2018
Hoover Institution was running their Make America Exceptional Again offer, and wanted to know the effects of offering a checkbox to sign up for the HDR newsletter by the bottom of the form. All copy remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not experience a statistically valid lift. However, this experiment not statistically validating is just as important as if we had seen a lift. This means we can increase the subscribers to the newsletter without adversely impacting acquisition.
The goal of these email acquisition campaigns is to create a file of prospective donors that can also be converted downstream. The best way to do that is through ongoing cultivation.
By getting a new subscriber to opt-in to the ongoing newsletter cultivations, we are putting them into a communication flow that will increase the likelihood of downstream conversion. Since they are having to physically opt-in to the series, it will also increase their perceived value of the communications they will receive.

experiments.title: How utilizing statistics in an email appeal influenced results
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of statistics influence the results of an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/27/2018
Buckner International was sending out their 2018 calendar year end campaign focused on the impact and need behind foster care and adoption. When discussing as a team, there was debate surrounding the use of statistics in the email appeals. There was published research indicating that statistics in an appeal could potentially lower response rates. We decided to test this theory with an email during the year end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that there was no statistically significant difference between either of the treatments with regards to both donations and clicks. It is worth noting that this is just one attempt at validating this theory. We would need to test it a few more times to truly understand the impact of incorporating statistics into our appeals.

experiments.title: How using a more personalized, urgent, and relevant message impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a more personalized, urgent, and relevant message impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2018 - 01/02/2019
During Care Net’s year-end campaign, we hypothesized we might be able to increase donor conversion on the Dear Reader feature at the end of articles on the Care Net blog. Our effort to do this included making it more personal by adding a signer to the appeal and making it more urgent and relevant by including year-end focused language and a countdown clock.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that the changes we made had little to no impact on donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that this might be due to the motivation of the user. We launched this campaign towards the end of the month when donor motivation is much higher. When donor motivation is higher, we normally see that our effort to increase donor conversion doesn’t work or has no impact. We propose testing a similar approach when motivation is more neutral.

experiments.title: How adding an endorsement increases sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an endorsement increase sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2018 - 05/04/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to see if an endorsement on the sign up page would affect sign ups for the Founding Documents offer. This treatment was tested with the following signers: Laura Ingraham, Dennis Prager, Rich Bott, Rush Limbaugh, and Eric Metaxas. The individual endorsements were shown based upon the source of the traffic so that visitors that were directed by Laura Ingraham would see her endorsement.
experiments.keyLearnings: When analyzing the results across all pages, we found that removing the endorsement from the page resulted in no significant impact to email acquisition. All versions that removed a signer endorsement decreased except for Dennis Prager’s, which saw a 4% lift in sign ups without the endorsement (not statistically significant. This is an important reminder to always validate “best practices.” We had always looked for authoritative endorsement but, in this situation, it would appear not to be a factor in the conversion process.

experiments.title: How removing context of a video affects sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding information about video content impact sign ups? Are people motivated by curiosity, or do they like to know what they’re getting into?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2018 - 06/27/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to get more sign ups for their online course “Introduction to C.S. Lewis.” They wanted to know if providing information about the video on the page, rather than no context at all, would impact sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings: Insufficient data to produce takeaway. Need to push more traffic to validate.

experiments.title: How adding more information in the sign up section increases sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding information about the seminar location increase sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2018 - 08/01/2018
Hillsdale College wanted to see if giving more information in the sign up box — the location of the seminar — would lead to more sign ups. They added a treatment to see what would happen.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although there was not enough traffic to validate this experiment, the current conversion rates of 5.6% for the control and 4.8% for the treatment show that there was no statistically significant difference between adding the location of the seminar in the sign up box.

experiments.title: How scripture and emphasizing offer importance impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will incorporating scripture and emphasizing why the offer is important increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/09/2018 - 11/16/2018
In an effort to increase signups to their Parenting Video Series, Buckner decided to try a radical approach of putting a “Parenting Pledge” prior to the Video Series acquisition page. Visitors were asked to pledge to raise their children according to scripture and were then sent to a page with the video series that could help them do that.
After launching this pledge, we noticed that the conversion rate on the page was lower than we would like it to be. To try to improve that, we decided to test out a different approach with the pledge. We rewrote the copy to incorporate scripture and to focus on why signing the pledge is important. We then tested the original pledge against this new version to see which would acquire more emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a week, we did not see any statistically significant change between the two versions. It would appear that the incorporation of scripture and emphasizing why the offer was important was not important for the page. These were two elements that were included in the advertising; it would appear that using them in the ads caused them to be unnecessary on the page.
Given the high cost for acquisition, we decided to turn off this offer and focus on other means to acquire potential donors for Buckner.

experiments.title: How personal asks perform against persuasive asks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a personal ask outperform a persuasive ask?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/31/2018
Leadership Institute was sending an appeal email to people who had donated to a matching gift campaign the previous year. They were curious whether these people, who had already absorbed and responded to the value proposition a year earlier, would need to be re-persuaded—or if a more personal request from an LI staff member would be more effective.
They had a control that sought to persuade through a story of impact, followed by an ask. They developed a personal treatment that very simply reminded the recipient that they had given last year, the need was still there, and requested a gift again this year. Though there was a small sample size to begin with, they launched a split test to see if they could validate a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The sample size was too small to validate, but the treatment delivered twice as many gifts as the control. This needs to be tested with a larger sample size, which means that the copy will need to be adjusted to include non-donors. However, this experiment does deliver a directional learning that can be tested with future donor-specific segments.

experiments.title: How streamlining an acquisition page affects course signups
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more streamlined, value-focused acquisition page increase conversions on an online course?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2018 - 06/08/2018
The Missionary Oblates were offering a free online course called Finding Your Prophetic Voice, and wanted to increase conversion on the landing page. To do this, they streamlined the page and used more value-focused body copy, along with a headline that better explained the value of the course to the taker. They also included testimonials from people who had taken another Oblates course to further explain the value.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment was not statistically valid, and will require more testing to identify specific learnings.

experiments.title: How a banner image impacts email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the banner increase the visual clarity of the headline leading to increased email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/06/2018 - 07/17/2018
For the Open Letter to the Senate for the confirmation of Judge Kavanaugh, we had a highly converting email acquisition page. However, we had the hypothesis that it was possible the headline (which is one of the most important aspects of the landing page) was getting lost in the banner image at the top. To validate this hypothesis, we set up two versions of the page with the treatment including the exact same headline but placed inline with the copy with the banner removed.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no statistically significant difference between the control and the treatment. In this situation, it would appear the headline was not as difficult to read as we initially thought it might be.

experiments.title: How 3rd-party credibility impacts conversion at the point of conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including the 3rd party evidential of “3.2 million other Americans” decrease anxiety and, subsequently, increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2018 - 07/05/2018
Americans for Prosperity (AFP) was launching an open letter to the US Senate regarding the upcoming Supreme Court nominee as a means to acquire new emails and new donors. AFP has a unique aspect of their organization in that they have 3.2 million activists that are a part of their efforts. We had the hypothesis that including this fact near the point of conversion (the submit button) would help decrease the anxiety associated with giving their information. We decided to test this.
experiments.keyLearnings: After showing the two treatments to over 5,000 visitors, we did not see any significant increase in a visitors likelihood to convert. It is worth pointing out that with a 65% conversion rate on the page, the visitors are already highly motivated. It is our hypothesis that, given this high motivation, the visitors likely do not have a significant degree of anxiety which means the 3rd party evidential was not necessary.

experiments.title: How an additional content offer checkbox affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an additional content offer through a checkbox decrease conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2018 - 05/11/2018
Hoover Institution was offering an eBook, Make America Exceptional Again to build their email file. They also had the Hoover Daily Report, a daily newsletter as an additional content offer. They wanted to give their visitors the chance to opt in to receive the daily newsletter in addition to the eBook, but didn’t want to disrupt conversion on the initial offer.
They decided to set up the checkbox opt-in on a separate page and split the traffic to test the impact of the second offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced no significant impact—increase or decrease—in conversion. Further data analysis showed that 73% of people who saw the treatment also opted into the Hoover Daily Report, which provided additional cultivation and engagement. 

Ultimately, though there wasn't a lift, this test was a win—conversion rate was not affected, and most users chose to receive additional content from Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How an additional opt-in impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an additional opt-in option for have any impact on email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2018 - 05/03/2018
The Heritage Foundation did an analysis and discovered that subscribers that were not cultivated with their daily newsletter, The Morning Bell, were far less likely to convert to become donors. However, we had also found that The Morning Bell by itself was not an appealing enough offer to collected subscribers for a reasonable costs. With these two facts in mind, we decided to launch an experiment to see if we could acquire Morning Bell subscribers through other offers.
We decided to add a checkbox on the acquisition form for one of their eBook that would allow people to get both the eBook and the Morning Bell newsletter. We wanted to test this before rolling it out to measure the impact this checkbox would have on acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for over a month, we did not see any statistically significant impact on email acquisition. Given the additional friction this checkbox could have caused, this is actually the ideal outcome since we are now able to acquire higher potential subscribers with no impact to conversion rate or cost.

experiments.title: How a targeted serial impacted conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will aligning the messaging of the serial ask around the individual campaign increase overall conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2018 - 04/23/2018
For the 2018 Tax Day Moneybomb, we wanted to see if we could increase the number of gifts made to Heritage Action as part of the serial ask. In past testing, we found that a more relevant message was able to increase those giving to the serial ask. We decided to see if crafting this serial ask around the individual campaign would have increased results.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, we did not get enough donors to the primary ask for this campaign to be able to to validate a serial ask message. Since the samples for this treatment is based upon those that make an initial gift, we were not able to get a large enough sample size to validate the experiment.

experiments.title: How making the daily resource less transactional on Adventures in Odyssey impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer increased conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2018 - 03/08/2018
The Adventures in Odyssey page gets significant traffic. In an effort to turn this traffic into donors, we hypothesized that turning the daily resource offer into something less transactional might increase conversion. To do this, we developed a treatment that told people the kind of impact they would have when they gave to Focus on the Family, and specifically when they supported Adventures in Odyssey. We also offered them a free 90 Day Devotional for Kids as an incentive for their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: Because of the low conversion on this page, there was not enough data to validate or see a significant difference between the two offers. The majority of the traffic on this page is returning visitors. The motivation of people on this page is to get the audio. Knowing that, we will give them something of value instead of asking them to give and get to see if we can increase conversion on the page.

experiments.title: How an additional incentive impacts webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an additional incentive increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2018 - 02/27/2018
For February’s webinar on email acquisition, we wanted to see if offering an additional resource would help incentivize people to signup for the webinar. In this case, we offered the 6 Ways to Grow Your File eBook since it was related to the topic we were covering on the webinar – email acquisition. We simply added an offer box highlighting the fact that when people signed up to attend the webinar they would also get the eBook for the treatment and the control only had who the presenter would be (as usual).
experiments.keyLearnings: The increase was slight and the sample size was too small so we cannot determine if the eBook incentive helped with webinar registrations. Worth testing again in the future.

experiments.title: How copy length in a webinar promotional email affects registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shorter and to-the-point email copy increase webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2018 - 02/14/2018
In this experiment, we were sending the initial promo for an upcoming webinar. We wondered if a shorter and more straight to the point email would affect webinar registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment made no significant difference in webinar registrations. What this means moving forward is that we don’t always have to write a long-narrative email to get webinar registrations. It’s possible to convey enough of the value proposition in a shorter email to move motivated people to the landing page to register.

experiments.title: How adding an acquisition opportunity to a homepage affects conversion rate
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Is a sticky bar or an exit intent more effective in acquiring names on a website?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/20/2018
The Missionary Oblates were wanting to take advantage of the traffic to their website as a channel for email acquisition. To test the most effective way to do this, they tried two options for popups: an exit intent and a sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings: Because of the low traffic and conversion rate, this test won’t validate. We recommend putting one of these acquisition opportunities on the website since there currently isn’t an opportunity to turn site traffic into names and potentially donors for the organization.

experiments.title: How “minor mystery” language in a headline affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will “minor mystery” language in a headline affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2018 - 01/14/2018
CaringBridge had run extensive testing on their “tribute widget”, where the majority of donations on their site begin. They wanted to run a headline test, with the knowledge that such a slight change might not produce any noticeable effect. They wanted to test the angle of a “minor mystery”, which had proven in other experiments to increase engagement (and subsequently conversion) by provoking the reader that they might not know something.
The headline on the tribute widget asked a simple question: Can you help power [tribute name]’s site? They proposed a test to the headline to introduce the “minor mystery”: Did you know you can help power [tribute name]’s site?
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no significant difference in the two headlines. This means that the next experiment needs to be more radical in nature to affect results.

experiments.title: How removing an extra decision point in an email affects webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing an extra decision point in the email increase webinar registration?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/11/2018
In this experiment, we were promoting a new webinar on donor cultivation. The control version of the email asked a question in the middle of the copy before introducing the the actual call-to-action. We hypothesized that this question would create an extra decision point – forcing readers to say “yes” or “no” before actually presenting them with the call-to-action.
We thought that by removing this question, we might see an increase in webinar registration.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although we saw a 3.4% increase in conversion rate, we did not reach a statistical level of confidence for this to be definitive. It’s possible that this result could hold true, but we would need a much larger volume in order to validate this result.

experiments.title: How contextualization of a premium offer affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will contextualizing the premium offer increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/02/2016
Harvest Ministries was promoting their midmonth premium offer, which was a book by Joni Earackson Tada. The landing page contained the heart of the value proposition and the call-to-action to get the book, which was divided from the rest of the copy by a horizontal line and a subheadline.
We hypothesized that the offer would be better contextualized if it was integrated into the copy to provide a seamless reading experience and decrease friction on the page. We created a treatment and ran an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The contextualized ask appeared to produce a 10.2% lift, but the experiment was launched late in the appeal and did not receive enough traffic to generate a statistically valid result. We will re-test this in the next premium appeal.

experiments.title: How adding a signer affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing branding and adding a picture and signature increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2015 - 12/10/2015
Harvest Ministries prepared an appeal for their year end campaign that offered a book, “The Biggest Story”, as a premium. They had prepared an email that told a personal story, made a financial ask with a call-to-action button, and then followed up with some information about the premium.
The control email was branded with a logo at the top and had no signature. They hypothesized that removing the logo to make the email more personal and adding a picture and signature would increase motivation and decrease anxiety, leading to an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Even though the email was more personal, there was no valid difference in conversion rate for the two emails. However, a closer look shows that the placement of the signature might have affected the results. Since a signature generally indicates the end of a letter, this would necessitate a second test where the signature was placed after the call-to-action button. Additionally, it might increase conversion to add the signer’s printed name and title as well.

experiments.title: How the length of copy affects donor conversion at the end of a money bomb campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the length of copy impact the donor conversion of a money bomb as it grows closer to the end of the campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2015 - 11/01/2015
As we grew closer to the end of the September money bomb campaign with Hillsdale College, we wanted to find a way to increase the conversion rate of the page. Our preview experiments had failed to move the needle in either direction so we decided to try a new tactic. We had the hypothesis that donors would be more highly motivated as the campaign deadline grew closer. As a result, we hypothesized that these donors would not need as much value proposition copy to convince them to make a gift.  We decided to create a treatment to reduct the amount of copy on the page in an effort to reduce the associated friction that comes with long copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The shorter copy did not produce any significant impact on a donor’s likelihood to make a gift. We were able to reduce the length of the page but it was not significant enough to really impact the amount of mental friction associated with the length.

experiments.title: How an additional priming question in a survey affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does an additional priming question in a survey affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2017 - 12/27/2017
Hillsdale College sent a survey to donors during their year-end campaign that was designed to prime them for a gift to support Hillsdale as an independent educational institution that did not accept any federal funds. The survey guided the reader’s thought pattern to contrast Hillsdale with the vast majority of schools that are reliant on federal funds. It also made the case that
The team had an additional question at the end of the survey to directly ask for a donation within the survey process—”Will you stand with Hillsdale and make a tax-deductible gift today to help secure Hillsdale’s independence?”. They wondered if removing this would result in an increase in donations, as it didn’t tip the hand about what happened next—or, if this actually was a catalyst in post-survey donations.
They created a treatment that removed this final question (which also shortened the quiz by one question) to see how donor conversion was affected.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The removal of the final question had no significant impact on donor conversion. This suggests a few things—that donors were already motivated to make a gift coming into the survey, and the direct ask did not increase that motivation, or that the initial questions were the actual motivating factors, and the final question had little to do with it.
Surveys have proven to be a powerful motivator for donation asks. This experiment prompts future testing for additional learnings.

experiments.title: How varying types of third party credibility affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will student quotes outperform a endorsement from a former president?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2017 - 04/06/2017
Hillsdale launched a course designed to give students a better understanding of the role of the Executive Branch. Previous optimization efforts from a similar test on a different course had already shown that student validation outperformed the presence of a course schedule by 20.5%.  They were interested in discovering how student quotes as third-party validation would perform against an endorsement from former President Ronald Reagan. They hypothesized that student quotes would outperform an endorsement from a former president because it was more personal, experienced-based, and spoke from the point of view of the page visitor.
They launched an A/B test to find out which treatment produced the highest email acquisition rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test were statistically insignificant. Even though the treatment with student quotes increased conversion by a 2%, the test could not be validated, which showed Hillsdale that prospects were not heavily influenced by varying types of third party validation. This prompted a reassessment of which page elements should be further tested.

experiments.title: How the Favicon impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a favicon increase email acquisition rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2017 - 10/26/2017
We had found in a previous experiment that added a favicon (the little image seen in the browser tabs) increased email acquisition. With this being such a minor factor in the page, we wanted to revalidate this experiment to see if this same result would hold up for other pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With this experiment, we did not see a statistically significant difference between the control and the treatment. Logically, this was our expected result given how minor the change was in the scheme of the page. In all likelihood, the last experiment that targeted the favicon fell into to the experiments that produced a false positive.
When we seek to achieve a 95% level of confidence, what this means is that there is a 5% chance the same effect could occur if both pages were actually the same. This demonstrates the importance of re-testing important test wins where we see a result that is unexpected.

experiments.title: How hint text forms impact email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the presence of hint text forms increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2017 - 10/26/2017
Hillsdale College was looking for new ways to increase their online course signup rate for their popular course ‘Western Heritage.’ After reviewing other similar course signup pages, they found that ‘hint text’ forms were a common practice. They wondered: Will using hint text forms on an email acquisition page reduce friction and increase email acquisition?
They launched an A/B test on their ‘Western Heritage’ course signup page to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale College found that the treatment with hint text forms did not have a valid impact on email acquisition rate. Therefore, the results of this test are not conclusive. However, we can take away something important from this experiment. It’s important to note that this test didn’t have a significant impact, despite the difference in design. Though most would agree that visually the hint text version is more visually appealing and less cluttered, it ultimately did not impact the visitors motivation to sign up for the course.
Further tests are being conducted on similar course signup pages, in regards to connection between the visual and functional components of these pages.

experiments.title: How the style of donation form impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a traditional form design increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2017 - 03/28/2017
The Heritage Foundation underwent a website redesign for their primary website, Heritage.org. In order to maintain visual congruence, we updated the design of the donation forms to match the look and feel of the new site. Part of that new design incorporated a more modern style of form fields and buttons.
There was concern that this new style for form fields may cause confusion for older Heritage donor base. We decided to test these form elements against the more commonly used designs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Based upon these results, the design of the form fields did not have any significant impact on donor conversion. It would seem that the more modern style of form fields were clear enough that they did not cause any friction or anxiety.

experiments.title: How addressing High-C subscriber in their style of communication impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will speaking to subscribers in their preferred DISC style increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2017 - 08/11/2017
In a follow up experiment during the 2017 Board Match, we wanted to test writing an appeal email with associated landing page in the style of a high “C” DISC profile.
Using the data that we appended from the CrystalKnows website, we segmented the subscriber list by both donor status and primary disc style. We then took the Control version of the email and landing page (written in the I style) and recreated it in the style of a high C.
