experiments.keyLearnings: The high-C treatment showed no significant difference in conversion rates between the control and treatment. Interestingly, the treatment which was written in the high-C format, decreased the conversion rate for the high-I group by 56%. It had no significant impact on the other groups.

experiments.title: How a phone number field on a donation form affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will adding a phone number field impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2017 - 10/31/2017
The Heritage Foundation had started using phone numbers to reach out and thank their donors for their gifts. Historically, the phone number was something that only came from direct mail donors. In order to increase the number of donors that had an available phone number on file, we wanted to see if adding a phone number field to the donation form would have an impact on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this situation, the insignificant result was exactly what we were hoping for. This proves that there is no significant impact to conversion by adding a phone number field and making it optional. Since it doesn’t impact conversion and provides us with additional data that will be helpful downstream, this has since been rolled out to all donation forms.

experiments.title: How the order of a donation ask impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What type of offer will lead to the greatest return in terms of email subscribers and donations acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 09/05/2017
In a previous test, we had found that an inline donation ask, was able to increase the number of donations that were received from readers of Heritage.org articles. However, despite the percentage increase, the total number of donations was still not very high.
We had the hypothesis that this audience might not yet be ready for a donation ask. Instead, we wanted to see we could get significantly more email addresses that would outweigh the immediate value received from the donations. We would set up the email acquisition offer to also have an instant donation ask for immediate conversion and we also know that we can convert 1% to 5% of all emails acquired downstream. Based upon these factors, we wanted to see if offering an email acquisition offer before the donation ask would achieve better long-term results.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that there was no statistically significant difference in donations between the two treatments. However, during this same period, the email offer also collected nearly 1,000 new subscribers for the house email file. Given the long term conversion that is likely to happen with these new subscribers, this makes the email acquisition offer a clear winner.

experiments.title: How membership benefits impact instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will membership benefits increase donor conversion for signers of the petition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2016 - 05/03/2016
For the instant donor conversion page that followed the petition against nominating a Supreme Court justice, we wanted to determine if membership benefits would be a strong incentive that could increase the overall donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two months, we did not find that membership benefits had any significant impact on donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that this is the result of a low motivated audience; not that the offer did not work. We have found the petition signers do not make the best donor prospects given the low barrier to entry onto the file.

experiments.title: How donor-centric copy affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will donor-centric copy increase the number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/05/2016 - 10/14/2016
The National Breast Cancer Foundation was giving away a free eBook, What Every Woman Needs To Know during Breast Cancer Awareness Month. They featured this eBook on their Breast Cancer Awareness Month page, with a form right on the page to download it.
The team at NBCF hypothesized that using copy that addressed the reader directly would increase email acquisition rate. They created a treatment that personalized each element: rather than “Get the free breast health guide”, the treatment offered the visitor the chance to “Get your free breast health guide”. Additionally, they revised the paragraph copy to show how the content of the eBook allows the visitor to be proactive about their own health. Finally, they added the value proposition element of “free” into the call-to-action.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced no statistical lift or decrease in email acquisition—in short, it made no difference. This revealed a few key insights:
It’s possible that visitors who were going to download this eBook were highly motivated and the slight changes in copy were not enough to affect that initial motivation.
It’s possible that the copy changes were too slight, and need a more radical treatment.
The copy may not be the primary factor affecting email acquisition—it may be location on the page.
This prompts future tests to see how the eBook can be better leveraged for name acquisition.

experiments.title: How the cover artwork for an eBook offer impacts name conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which eBook cover would be more appealing to people and acquire more names?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/28/2017 - 03/14/2017
The Missionary Oblates set up an acquisition offer for Lent. They were offering people an eBook of Lenten Prayers. The eBook was a resource they already had created, but we hypothesized that something visually brighter and more modern might be more appealing and potentially acquire more names. To test this hypothesis, we developed a new cover for the Lenten Prayers eBook and set up two different Facebook campaigns – one with the original cover that looked more traditional, and one with the new cover that was more modern.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the two covers were drastically different in appearance, there was no signifiant difference in conversion. This experiment didn’t validate, but what it tells us is that the cover of the eBook had no impact on name conversion for the offer. This is great insight to have as we continue to launch new offers using content and artwork that is already developed.

experiments.title: How the length of copy affects the follow on conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which treatment will acquire the most donors for a follow on ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2015 - 09/25/2015
One of the best fundraising methods for Heritage Action for America is a follow on ask for Heritage donors. After a Heritage donor has completed their gift, they are prompted with a second ask for their sister organization. Friction for this ask has been dramatically reduced by requiring them to only enter the amount they want to give.
The copy associated with this follow on ask is relatively long since the value proposition changes between the first ask and the second. We had the hypothesis that this longer ask might not be necessary for donors and may actually be hurting our conversion. We decided to simplify the language and test the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After three months of testing, we discovered a handful of learnings.
The first is that we did not have enough traffic to this follow on ask to validate a small change like reducing the copy.  We either needed to expand the area of the website being tested or need to develop a more radical change.
The second is that the friction associated with the length of the copy did not impact the overall conversion of the page.

experiments.title: How reduced copy and a more direct headline affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reduced copy and a more direct headline positively affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2016 - 01/05/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary offered a free online course called How to Read the Bible Like a Seminary Professor to allow their donors and prospects to experience the value proposition of the Seminary. Although they had a good donor conversion rate, they wanted to see if they could improve email acquisition by making the value proposition on the signup page more appealing.
They created a treatment with a more direct headline: “Learn to read the Bible like never before”. Then, they removed the bulleted list from the copy to reduce the overall length of the copy, hypothesizing that it was not core to the value proposition on the page.
They split traffic to the page and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version produced a slight decrease in email acquisition, though it was far from valid. This shows that more radical changes need to be made to the page to significantly increase email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How prioritizing match language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does putting match language before a story affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2016 - 01/17/2017
CaringBridge had a matching gift offered during their year-end campaign from a user who had personally experienced the value proposition of the organization. They knew that stories had been powerful vehicles for fundraising campaigns, but this story had the added power of a matching gift.
They deliberated whether to put the match announcement at the beginning of the email or after the story. Putting it at the beginning would allow people who were already motivated to give to make their donations right away. But putting it at the end might allow people to be drawn in by the story first and potentially result in more donations once the powerful story was augmented by the match. They decided to put it to a test and split their file to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which led with the matching gift announcement, produced a 32.7% lift in conversion, although the experiment did not reach statistical validity. This suggests that the directness of the match language was a powerful motivator, or that it possibly framed the story and resulted in more donors being led to take action.
However, this test will need to receive more traffic to produce a valid result.

experiments.title: How 3rd party quotes impacted the email acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will positive reviews of the book increase the appeal of the offer and, subsequently, the conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/22/2016 - 12/23/2016
As part of the promotion for their free eBook offer, Buckner International wanted to determine if adding book reviews increased the appeal of the offer. We were able to pull quotes from the book’s Amazon page and add them directly on to the page before the form.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a full 2 months, we did not see any statistically significant impact of the reviews. The page was converting at a ~40% conversion rate and a small adjustment like this did not seem to have any influence on the visitor’s likelihood to submit their information.

experiments.title: How the imagery of the page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a flat image or an eCard video produce the best email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2016 - 12/23/2016
As part of the December acquisition campaign, Buckner International was launching a dedicated eCard to be sent out to all foster and adoptive families. We then launched Facebook ads to collect “signatures” for the card. During our initial conversations, we had a debate on the best way to show the “card” that they would be signing. Initially, it was believed that a flat image would be the least distracting but then the hypothesis of a video was raised since it was believed that it would be more engaging.
experiments.keyLearnings: Since this offer had a limited time period that it could be reasonably run, we were not able to validate the experiment. However, it appears that the animated eCard video produced a lift over the flat card. It would have taken several hundred more visitors to statistically validate this result but this is definitely worth remembering for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How high visitor motivation affects donor conversion rate

rows.name: Stand Up Headline with Matching Grant

experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment is going to produce the best conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2015 - 05/31/2015
The standard appeal for Alliance Defending Freedom will begin with an email to their main constituency that contains a strong call-to-action to give a gift. In an effort to increase the conversion rate of the page, we wanted to try several changes add additional value to the potential donors’ gift and reduce friction and anxiety.
We created a treatment that dramatically expanded upon the value proposition being discussed on the page both in the main page copy and in the button at the bottom. We also moved the matching gift incentive to the end of the copy to focus on the primary reasons to give to ADF. We also expanded elements that emphasized the security and financial accountability.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment did not reach significance with either sample size or level of confidence, but we did learn something about the motivation of the donors.
The initial driver of traffic (the email) was crafted in such a way that only the most highly motivated visitors made to the page. We realized that in order to improve the overall response rate of the campaign, we needed to move the optimization efforts back in the donor funnel to the email itself.  If we can increase the number of visitors to the page, we can then impact the overall results of the campaign.

experiments.title: How a Faith and Justice offer impacted the blog subscriptions
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the incentive of Faith and Justice lift the email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2016 - 03/31/2016
In our continued efforts to increase the conversion rate for visitors on the blog, we decided to isolate the Faith and Justice offer to see what impact that would have in visitor conversion. In past experiments, we discovered that Faith and Justice by itself decreased conversion so by adding incremental language to the slide out that offered the subscription we were hoping to see if the added incentive increased the value in the visitors minds.
experiments.keyLearnings: With statistical validity sitting at 73%, we discovered that Faith and Justice did not increase the value of the email acquisition offer in the minds of the visitor. It did not detract from it but we learned that it would not serve as an added incentive for subscription.

experiments.title: How the positioning of the offer affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing the slide out offer in the middle of the blog post increase the email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2016 - 04/20/2016
Our most successful acquisition devices for the ADF blog is currently a slide out offer that appears once the visitor reaches the end of each blog post. The position of the offer was chosen based upon the opinion that people that finish the content would be more likely to convert. We decided to put this hypothesis to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings: Initially, we saw positive results with the slide out offer appearing in the middle of the page but over time, these results diminished until there was no statistical difference. It would appear that there is no major impact between showing the slide out in the middle or at the end of the page.

experiments.title: How a Topical Inline Email Offer Impacts Email Acquisition Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing someone an email acquisition offer that is specific to the article they are on increase the likelihood of conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2017 - 06/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom has identified their blog as a high-traffic section of their site. They noticed, however, that too many mobile visitors were leaving without signing up to receive more Alliance Defending Freedom content. To assist in email acquisition, they decided to add an inline offer for mobile visitors that prompted them to ‘stay informed to changes to your religious freedom,’ by signing up to receive the latest generic updates from Alliance Defending Freedom. After noticing that this inline offer increased email acquisition, they decided to create more specific inline offers for each major topic: Sanctity of life, religious liberty, and marriage and family. They set these treatments up against the more generic control, and documented the results.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test did not validate. However, we can still draw some conclusions from the results. The addition of the specific inline offer did not dramatically increase or decrease email acquisition for mobile visitors. This may indicate that blog visitors were not drawn to one particularly topic but rather Alliance Defending Freedom’s high level value proposition. It may also indicate that the more generic inline offer had a great perceived value (i.e. updates on sanctity of life vs. updates on sanctity of life, religious liberty, and marriage and family). Though not statistically valid, this test gave Alliance Defending Freedom a head start in understanding the motivations and behavior of mobile visitors.

experiments.title: How a ‘Blog Topic’ Survey Impacts User Engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a ‘Blog Topic’ survey increase user engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/07/2017 - 08/11/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom found in a previous test that offering blog readers additional relevant content at the end of the article increased engagement. Since this discovery, they have looked for new ways to (a) further increase engagement and (b) gather feedback on which topics they should cover. With these goals in mind, they launched a ‘Blog Topic’ survey that prompts the reader to select which topic they would like Alliance Defending Freedom to cover next.
After the user made a selection, they were offered one of two different options: additional articles to explore related to their selection or an email acquisition offer. Both of these actions are metrics that we are trying to increase for blog readers so we wanted to see if this style of engagement could impact either one.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Neither of the two survey options had any significant impact on either of the two tracked metrics: reading more articles and acquiring more emails. The data set above is for the email acquisition rate.
Though this test did not show a statistical difference in the primary metrics, we can draw one conclusion from the results. The ‘Blog Topic’ survey did not have a negative impact on email acquisition or engagement rates. Because of this, we know that Alliance Defending Freedom will be able to gather insightful data from the blog users. This data–collected with no net impact on engagement–can help them determine strategic focuses in the future.

experiments.title: How aligning the content of the email offer to the article impacted acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will offers that better relate to the content on the page increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2017 - 06/30/2017
On the mobile version of the Alliance Defending Freedom blog, we wanted to see if we could better align the value of the email offer with the content on the page. The belief was that people reading an article on “Sanctity of Life” or “Culture” would be more likely to respond to an email offer on the same topic.
To do this, we created four different versions of the email acquisition offer targeted at the four categories of blog posts on ADFLegal.org’s blog.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment did not produce any significant lift to the email acquisition rates when it aligned with the content of the article. After running for a full month, it seemed to be negatively impacting the results so we decided to shut it off before it validated. (It wasn’t worth running for another month).
This reinforces something we’ve seen in the past with ADF. Supporters connect with the organizational value proposition of defending religious freedom. Whenever we try to target a specific aspect of that proposition, it negatively impacts results.

experiments.title: How an ‘Up Next’ slideout affects email acquisition rate on Desktop
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase clickthrough rate on desktop by providing a single article that is “Up Next”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/05/2017 - 10/02/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom recently conducted an experiment  on mobile devices, where they asked: Is it possible to increase clickthrough rate by providing an ‘Up Next’ article? The result was a valid 29% increase in traffic without a valid decrease in conversion. They wondered if they would see the same results if they conducted this experiment with desktop users as well. They launched a desktop-only test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Alliance Defending Freedom’s experiment did not achieve validity. Though the results were inconclusive, this test may tell us an important thing or two about desktop users. Behavior from one device to another can change significantly. We know that those who view a blog post on a mobile device are already more likely to read more than one article at a time. Due to the differences in motivation and the likely environment of mobile and desktop users, it’s important that we test each separately. Another variable that makes this test different than the mobile-only version is the ‘Up Next’ content the desktop viewer saw. It’s possible this piece of content was less compelling to viewers than what was shown as a treatment in the mobile test.
Further studies are being conducted to determine whether desktop visitors benefit from an ‘Up Next’ slideout or not.

experiments.title: How a case-specific exit intent offers impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an exit intent offer geared towards a particular legal case increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2017 - 10/17/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom was looking for a new way to improve the exit intent offer that visitors see before leaving their site. They wondered if a more specific call-to-action would cause visitors to take action with more urgency. They decided to test an offer around one of their largest legal cases right now: The defense of Jack Phillips. So they created a treatment version and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for over a month, this test did not validate for Alliance Defending Freedom. While they did begin to see a lift with the more case-specific messaging, when compared with the mission-specific messaging (the control), the end results were inconclusive. This has prompted further test ideas regarding the nature and offer of exit intent offers.

experiments.title: How exclusivity and impact factor into donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will exclusivity or impact have the greatest influence on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2017 - 07/14/2017
During the acquisition campaign for Heritage Action’s Leaders Club, we were trying to acquire a new group of donors for the organization’s recurring giver club. As we were reviewing past campaigns, we realized that our historical messaging had focused on the exclusivity of joining the club. However, we had the hypothesis that donors would more likely join due to the impact their gift would have over the exclusivity of the club. We decided to test these two approaches against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It would seem that both factors were equally important in the donors’ minds. There was no statistically significant difference between the two versions.
Now, it is worth pointing out that both versions incorporated both exclusivity and impact into the copy; this test was focused on determining which one of these factors should be led with.

experiments.title: How the function of the form elements affects conversion (and the “most beautiful form in the world”)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a most condensed donation form that utilized more modern labeling techniques increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2016 - 09/12/2016
In an effort to continue to iterate on the general donation form used throughout the Heritage properties, we developed a test that would incorporate the modern web technique of adaptive placeholders. The goal of this technique is to visually condense the form by using placeholder labels inside of the text fields but to then move those same placeholders to the top of the field once a visitor focuses on it so the purpose of the field is clear. This is a technique that was taken from some of the larger eCommerce sites like Hotwire.com.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new form with adaptive placeholders did not produce any statistically significant results. While the form was more condensed, it looks like it was not a significant enough change to increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How getting visitors to identify with the target audience affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will identifying the target audience for the course increase the number of email addresses acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2015 - 09/01/2015
As part of the continued optimization of the Constitution 101 email acquisition page, Hillsdale College wanted to find a way to boost conversion. We had a hypothesis that visitors might not be converting because they didn’t know if the course was right for them.
We decided to create a new treatment that identifying the “target audience” for the course. This would let the visitors mentally bucket themselves in one target audience which would increase overall name acquisition efforts.
experiments.keyLearnings: Both treatments produced nearly identical email acquisition rates which resulted in no statistically significant lift. Based upon these results, it would seem that the visitors not converting with the control version where not questioning whether they were the right audience for the course.

experiments.title: How membership benefit language affects the conversion of existing members
experiments.researchQuestion: How will listing membership benefits on the page impact the likelihood of an existing member renewing their membership?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/08/2016 - 01/20/2016
During the January membership campaign, we had the hypothesis that the associated benefits may not serve as much of an incentive to existing members. We decided to create two versions of the existing member page, one with a handful of benefits listed out and another without. We then split the existing member traffic between the two pages and monitored conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were not able to produce a statistically significant result in either direction. It would seem that a reminder of their benefits does not have any impact on the likelihood of an existing donor to renew their membership.
Interestingly, we performed this same experiment for new members and it produced a 20% increase when we added the benefits. The benefits would appear to have a higher perceived value with those that have not yet experienced them.

experiments.title: How the load time of a page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a faster loading page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/15/2016 - 01/20/2016
We noticed a decrease in the landing page conversion in the last Heritage year end campaign. One of the hypotheses for the cause was that the load time of the new server infrastructure may have been adversely impacting the conversion.  To test this hypothesis, we created a flat version of the same landing page that did not require any database calls, had a reduced amount of styles and code, and excluded several elements for tracking users.  The net result of these changes was a 47% decrease in the load time of the page (3.23 ms vs. 1.70 ms).
We then tested these two pages against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not reach any statistically significant results with this experiment. Directionally, it would seem the faster page gave a minor boost to conversion but the initial load time of 3.23 milliseconds may already have been fast enough that it was not as impactful to the conversion process as we initially envisioned it would be.

experiments.title: How seasonal imagery affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will seasonal background imagery impact the conversion rate on the page during the holiday season?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2015 - 12/28/2015
We had a hypothesis that if we could subtly incorporate seasonal imagery into Heritage’s calendar year end campaign, we would be able to encourage the spirit of giving that comes with the holiday season. We created two landing page treatments, one of which incorporated a bokeh image (blurred Christmas lights).
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment produced no significant impact on conversion rate or average gift. It would appear that either the image was too subtle and didn’t remind visitors of the Christmas season or the imagery played such a small part in the likelihood to convert that a change to this area didn’t make much of a difference.

experiments.title: How an alternate call-to-action, positioned at the right time, affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will an alternate call-to-action impact the visitor’s likelihood to give a gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2015 - 10/28/2015
With an upcoming radio membership campaign, we wanted to find a way to get more out of the traffic being driven to the landing page. We had the idea of offering a free subscription to the Morning Bell when people showed the intent to leave the page.  The hypothesis is that this would not interfere with the giving process but would still allow us to get capture visitor information for those who decided not to give.
We decided to test this hypothesis to ensure that A) we did not impact donor conversion and B) the offer was strong enough that people would give their email address.
experiments.keyLearnings: We knew that by offering the opportunity for a visitor to give their email address would acquire more emails than not even giving that option. What we learned in this experiment was that there was no significant impact on donor conversion by having the secondary call to action.  We subsequently rolled out the exit intent offer to 100% of the traffic.

experiments.title: How limiting copy length affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which copy treatment will produce the highest conversion rate during a money bomb campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2015 - 06/24/2015
During the 2015 fiscal year end money bomb campaign, we decided to run a test at the start of the campaign to see if the campaign theme needed added copy to increase the strength of the value proposition. Our goal was to validate this experiment so that we would have an optimized donation form by the end of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found no statistical improvement in the short or long copy treatment on the donation form. It would seem the length of the copy was not as important as the concept itself. Both treatments adequately laid out the need and the reason to give which is what produced no significant difference between the two.

experiments.title: How a soft call-to-action affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call to action to “Stand with Heritage” will increase the click through rate which will increase the number of donors that convert on the page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2014 - 12/22/2014
On the third email in Heritage’s year end campaign, we ran a test asking constituents to either “Stand with Heritage” or “Make a Donation.”
experiments.keyLearnings: Even though the softer call-to-action drove 436% more visitors to the landing page, the traffic was not motivated enough to have a significant number of those visitors convert to donors.  On offers like this in the future, we may consider special messaging or landing page to help continue them down the donor funnel.

experiments.title: How removing Challenge Gift language affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will not including a challenge gift with the campaign have minimal impact on the fundraising campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2014 - 12/28/2014
As part of their year end campaign, the Heritage Foundation secured multiple gifts from major donors to serve as challenge gifts to their members. It has always been said that Challenge Gifts can increase conversion rate and average gifts but we have never known how much the increase is. We decided to test this well-known tactic to determine how much impact a Challenge Gift can have on a fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a slight increase in conversion rate for those that received the version with language about the challenge gift but it did not reach significance with sample size or lift. This would suggest that the challenge gift may not have the level of impact that best practices tells us it has. However, this test would need to be run with a larger segment to validate that conclusion.

experiments.title: How the placement of an impact statement affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What copy treatment will produce the highest conversion rate and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/25/2015 - 06/30/2015
In Hillsdale’s fiscal year end campaign, we were able to utilize a matching gift as a way to inspire donors to give more. The grant was talked about in in the copy above the donation form but we also wanted to find a way to incorporate it into the form itself.
As a result, we created a treatment that added an impact statement to the donation form which reminded a donor how their gift would be worth due to the matching grant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test proved to be inconclusive. After further reflection, we would hypothesize that the impact statement appeared after the donor had already made their decision as to how much money to donate.  As a result, it didn’t really impact their decision on how much and whether to give.
To make better use of the matching grant in future campaigns, the impact language needs to be higher up on the form so it affects the donors’ decision prior to selecting a gift amount.
